leibel2023_disruption_index.title: What do we know about the disruption index in scientometrics? An overview of the literature
leibel2023_disruption_index.research_idea: This paper provides a systematic review of the literature on the disruption index (DI1) and its variants in scientometrics, focusing on their technical and theoretical properties, validity, and limitations.
leibel2023_disruption_index.research_question: What are the properties, validity, and limitations of the disruption index (DI1) and its variants in scientometrics?
leibel2023_disruption_index.data_source: Literature review of published studies on the disruption index and its variants
leibel2023_disruption_index.data_description: The paper reviews studies published since 2017 that examine the disruption index (DI1) and its variants, including their application in various scientific disciplines and their theoretical and empirical properties.

statistical_method.specification: Systematic literature review and synthesis of findings from empirical studies on the disruption index.

grouping_strategy.rationale: N/A

result_claims.value: some modified index variants (e.g., DI5) show higher degrees of convergent validity than DI1
disruption indices suffer from several data-induced biases
disruption indices suffer from time-sensitive biases

partial_results.note: The literature on convergent validity is not entirely conclusive.

conclusion_claims: The disruption index (DI1) and its variants have been widely applied in scientometrics, but their validity and limitations need further investigation.
Modified index variants, such as DI5, show higher degrees of convergent validity than DI1.
Disruption indices suffer from inconsistency, time-sensitive biases, and several data-induced biases.
More research is needed on the validity of disruption scores and a more precise understanding of disruption as a theoretical construct before the indices can be used in research evaluation practice.
limitations: The literature on convergent validity is not entirely conclusive.
Disruption indices suffer from inconsistency, time-sensitive biases, and several data-induced biases.
The theoretical construct of disruption is not yet fully understood.

_source.title: What do we know about the disruption index in scientometrics? An overview of the literature

funk2017_dynamic_network.title: A Dynamic Network Measure of Technological Change
funk2017_dynamic_network.research_idea: This paper introduces a network-based measure (CDt and mCDt indexes) to quantify the extent to which new inventions consolidate or destabilize existing technology streams. The approach allows for capturing the impact of technological change on prior technologies, which is not detectable with traditional citation-based measures.
funk2017_dynamic_network.research_question: How do new inventions reshape the network of interlinked technologies, and what factors influence whether they consolidate or destabilize the status quo?
hypotheses: Federal research funding pushes universities to create inventions that are more destabilizing.
Deeper commercial ties lead universities to produce technologies that consolidate the status quo.
funk2017_dynamic_network.data_description: The dataset includes U.S. patents with citation information, used to analyze technological change and university research commercialization.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly stated, but likely post-1990s due to NBER data availability

independent_variables.name: Federal Funding
independent_variables.definition: Whether the university received federal funding for the research

independent_variables.name: Commercial Engagement
independent_variables.definition: Depth of commercial ties of the university, such as industry partnerships or licensing agreements

dependent_variables.name: CDt Index
dependent_variables.formula: Quantifies the extent to which an invention consolidates or destabilizes the use of prior technologies

dependent_variables.name: mCDt Index
dependent_variables.formula: Combines the CDt index with an impact weight to characterize the magnitude of consolidation or destabilization

control_variables.name: Patent Impact
control_variables.rationale: To account for the general impact of patents as measured by forward citations

control_variables.name: Field of Technology
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in technological fields that may influence the results

statistical_method.specification: Regression models comparing the CDt and mCDt indexes with independent variables such as federal funding and commercial engagement

network_construction.construction_method: Patents are nodes in a network, with directed and weighted edges representing citations between patents. The network evolves over time as new inventions are added.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the effects of commercial engagement and federal funding on the type of technological change produced by universities.

result_claims.value: Higher for universities with federal funding
Higher for universities with commercial engagement

partial_results.note: Forward-citation-based measures do not distinguish between destabilizing and consolidating inventions, leading to ambiguity in results.

conclusion_claims: Federal research funding encourages universities to produce more destabilizing inventions, while deeper commercial ties lead to the production of technologies that consolidate the status quo.
The CDt and mCDt indexes provide a novel way to quantify the impact of new technologies on prior technologies, offering insights that are not detectable with traditional citation-based measures.
limitations: The study focuses on university-related patents and may not generalize to other types of organizations.
The measurement of commercial engagement and federal funding may be subject to biases or incomplete data.

robustness_checks.method: Alternative definitions of commercial engagement and federal funding
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not sensitive to the specific operationalization of these variables.

_source.title: A Dynamic Network Measure of Technological Change

petersen2023_disruption_index.title: The disruption index is biased by citation inflation
petersen2023_disruption_index.research_idea: The disruption index (CD) is systematically biased by citation inflation, making it unsuitable for cross-temporal analysis of scientific and technological disruption.
petersen2023_disruption_index.research_question: Is the observed decline in the disruption index over time a true reflection of reduced innovation or an artifact of citation inflation?
hypotheses: The disruption index (CD) systematically declines over time due to citation inflation.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase triadic closure in citation networks.
petersen2023_disruption_index.data_source: Clarivate Analytics Web of Science, Microsoft Academic Graph (MAG)
petersen2023_disruption_index.data_description: The Web of Science citation network and the MAG citation network, spanning decades of scientific publications and patents.

filters: publications and patents with complete citation data

independent_variables.name: citation_inflation
independent_variables.definition: Systematic increase in the number of citations over time, driven by longer reference lists and self-citations.

independent_variables.name: reference_list_length
independent_variables.definition: Number of references cited in a publication, which has increased over time.

independent_variables.name: self_citations
independent_variables.definition: Number of self-citations in a publication, which increases triadic closure in citation networks.

dependent_variables.name: disruption_index_CD
dependent_variables.formula: CDp = (Ni - Nj) / (Ni + Nj + Nk)

control_variables.name: team_size
control_variables.rationale: Team size is a proxy for production effort and may correlate with citation patterns.

control_variables.name: citation_count
control_variables.rationale: Citation count is a time-dependent variable that may confound the relationship between disruption and innovation.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses deductive analysis, empirical analysis of citation networks, and computational modeling of synthetic networks to demonstrate the bias in the disruption index.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are built using the reference lists and citing papers for each focal paper p.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the disruption index across different eras and assess the impact of citation inflation.

partial_results.note: The decline in CD is attributed to citation inflation rather than a true decline in innovation.

conclusion_claims: The disruption index is systematically biased by citation inflation and is unsuitable for cross-temporal analysis.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase triadic closure in citation networks.
limitations: The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and does not include formal statistical testing.
The paper does not propose an alternative to the disruption index that accounts for citation inflation.

robustness_checks.method: computational modeling of synthetic citation networks
robustness_checks.rationale: To demonstrate that the disruption index converges to 0 in the absence of citation inflation.

_source.title: The disruption index is biased by citation inflation

ke2023_network_based_normalized_impact.title: A network-based normalized impact measure reveals successful periods of scientific discovery across disciplines
ke2023_network_based_normalized_impact.research_idea: The paper introduces a network-based methodology to normalize citation counts, correcting for temporal and field biases, and uses it to identify high-impact research across disciplines.
ke2023_network_based_normalized_impact.research_question: How can we develop an unbiased citation normalization method to compare scientific impact across disciplines and time periods?
hypotheses: Cocited papers can be used to define a local comparison group for normalization, eliminating the need for a priori discipline labels.
The proposed normalized citation measure (ˆC) will follow a universal distribution across disciplines and time, making it a fair indicator of impact.
ke2023_network_based_normalized_impact.data_source: Web of Science, Scopus, and other citation databases (not explicitly named but implied through methodology)
ke2023_network_based_normalized_impact.data_description: The study uses a comprehensive dataset of scientific publications and their citation networks, spanning multiple disciplines and decades.

independent_variables.name: year_of_publication
independent_variables.definition: The year in which a paper was published, used to account for temporal citation trends.

independent_variables.name: cocitation_network
independent_variables.definition: The set of papers cocited with a given paper, used to define a local comparison group for normalization.

dependent_variables.name: normalized_citation_count (ˆC)
dependent_variables.formula: ˆci_t = ci_t / ⟨cnt⟩n∈N it, where ci_t is the number of citations received by paper i in year t, and ⟨cnt⟩n∈N it is the average yearly citations of papers cocited with i in year t.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To account for systematic differences in citation practices across disciplines.

control_variables.name: publication_venue
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation rates between journals or conferences.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and network analysis to compute and analyze the normalized citation measure ˆC.

network_construction.construction_method: Cocitation networks are built by connecting papers that are cited together in the same reference list of citing papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the distribution of normalized citations (ˆC) between papers identified as high-impact and those identified as low-impact.

result_claims.value: follows a universal distribution across disciplines and time
diverse fields such as geosciences, radiology, and optics are identified as sources of high-impact research

partial_results.note: The paper compares ˆC with RCR but does not provide detailed statistical significance for the comparison.

conclusion_claims: The proposed normalized citation measure (ˆC) provides an unbiased indicator of scientific impact across disciplines and time.
The method reveals high-impact research in diverse, smaller disciplines that are often overshadowed by highly cited biomedical sciences.
limitations: The method relies on the availability of comprehensive citation data, which may not be accessible for all fields or time periods.
The cocitation-based normalization assumes that cocited papers are topically related, which may not always be accurate.

robustness_checks.method: comparison with existing normalization methods (e.g., RCR)
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in correcting for field and temporal biases.

_source.title: A network-based normalized impact measure reveals successful periods of scientific discovery across disciplines

bikard2013_exploring_tradeoffs.title: Exploring Tradeoffs in the Organization of Scientific Work: Collaboration and Scientific Reward
bikard2013_exploring_tradeoffs.research_idea: The paper investigates the tradeoff between the benefits of collaboration in scientific work and the costs associated with credit allocation. It develops a theoretical model and uses empirical data to analyze how scientists balance collaboration with individual credit.
bikard2013_exploring_tradeoffs.research_question: How do scientists balance the benefits of collaboration (e.g., higher quality outputs) with the costs of credit allocation, and what factors influence their collaborative choices?
hypotheses: Collaboration leads to higher quality scientific outputs (e.g., more citations).
Collaboration reduces individual credit allocation for scientists.
Cross-departmental collaborations yield higher quality outputs at a lower productivity cost than within-departmental collaborations.
bikard2013_exploring_tradeoffs.data_source: Academic publications of 661 faculty scientists at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT).
bikard2013_exploring_tradeoffs.data_description: The dataset includes annual research activity of 661 faculty scientists at MIT over a 30-year period (1976-2006).

independent_variables.name: collaboration_size
independent_variables.definition: Number of co-authors on a paper

independent_variables.name: cross_departmental
independent_variables.definition: Whether the collaboration involves authors from different departments

independent_variables.name: seniority_of_coauthor
independent_variables.definition: The seniority level of the coauthor (e.g., junior, senior)

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by a paper

dependent_variables.name: fractional_citation
dependent_variables.formula: Citation count divided by the number of authors (fractional credit)

control_variables.name: individual_fixed_effects
control_variables.rationale: To account for individual-level tendencies in collaboration and productivity

control_variables.name: department_year_fixed_effects
control_variables.rationale: To control for the broader organizational environment and departmental trends

statistical_method.specification: Fixed-effects regression models to analyze the relationship between collaboration and scientific output at the individual level.

network_construction.construction_method: Coauthorship networks are constructed based on the collaboration history of MIT faculty scientists over the 30-year period.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the quality and productivity outcomes of different types of collaborations.

result_claims.value: higher quality papers at lower productivity cost
low quality gain and high productivity loss

partial_results.note: The paper suggests that credit may be disproportionately allocated in collaborative work, but the exact mechanism is not fully quantified.

conclusion_claims: Collaboration is associated with higher quality scientific outputs (e.g., more citations) and increased fractional credit for individual scientists.
Cross-departmental collaborations tend to produce higher quality papers at a lower productivity cost compared to within-departmental collaborations.
limitations: The study is limited to a single institution (MIT), which may not be representative of the broader scientific community.
The paper does not fully quantify the mechanisms of credit allocation in collaborative work.

robustness_checks.rationale: To account for individual-level tendencies in collaboration and productivity
To control for the broader organizational environment and departmental trends

_source.title: Exploring Tradeoffs in the Organization of Scientific Work: Collaboration and Scientific Reward

park2023_disruptive.title: Papers and patents are becoming less disruptive over time
park2023_disruptive.research_idea: The paper investigates whether scientific and technological progress is becoming less disruptive over time by analyzing citation patterns and textual data across multiple fields and time periods.
park2023_disruptive.research_question: Are papers and patents becoming less disruptive over time, and if so, what factors might explain this trend?
hypotheses: Papers and patents are becoming less disruptive over time.
The decline in disruptiveness is not due to changes in the quality of published science, citation practices, or field-specific factors.
park2023_disruptive.data_source: Web of Science (WoS), United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) Patents View database, JSTOR, American Physical Society corpus, Microsoft Academic Graph, PubMed
park2023_disruptive.data_description: The study uses data on 45 million papers and 3.9 million patents from six large-scale datasets, including 390 million citations, 25 million paper titles, 13 million abstracts, and 35 million patent citations.

independent_variables.name: time_period
independent_variables.definition: The year of publication or patent filing, used to assess trends over time.

independent_variables.name: field
independent_variables.definition: The scientific or technological field to which a paper or patent belongs.

dependent_variables.name: CD5
dependent_variables.formula: CD5 = 1 - (2 * sum(fit * bit) / nt), where fit = 1 if i cites the focal paper/patent, 0 otherwise; bit = 1 if i cites the predecessors of the focal paper/patent, 0 otherwise; nt = number of forward cites to the focal work and/or its predecessors as of time t.

control_variables.name: citation_practices
control_variables.rationale: To account for changes in citation behavior over time.

control_variables.name: quality_of_science
control_variables.rationale: To account for potential changes in the quality of published science.

control_variables.name: field_specific_factors
control_variables.rationale: To account for field-specific trends that might influence disruptiveness.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and trend analysis to examine changes in the CD5 index over time and across fields.

network_construction.construction_method: The network is built using citation data to assess how papers and patents cite their predecessors and successors.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics of disruptive and consolidating papers and patents.

result_claims.value: decreased from 0.52 in 1945 to 0.04 in 2010 for 'social sciences'
decreased from 0.30 in 1980 to 0.06 in 2010 for 'computers and communications'

partial_results.note: The rate of decline is greatest in the earlier parts of the time series and appears to stabilize for patents between 2000 and 2005.

conclusion_claims: Papers and patents are becoming less disruptive over time, as indicated by a decline in the CD5 index.
The decline in disruptiveness is not due to changes in the quality of published science, citation practices, or field-specific factors.
limitations: The study relies on citation data, which may not fully capture the impact or novelty of a paper or patent.
The CD5 index is a novel metric, and its interpretation may be subject to debate.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed trends are not specific to a single dataset.
To assess the sensitivity of the CD5 index to different time periods.

_source.title: Papers and patents are becoming less disruptive over time

arts2021_natural_language_processing.title: Natural language processing to identify the creation and impact of new technologies in patent text: Code, data, and new measures
arts2021_natural_language_processing.research_idea: The paper develops natural language processing (NLP) techniques to identify the creation and impact of new technologies in U.S. patents, and validates these techniques against traditional metrics based on patent classification and citations.
arts2021_natural_language_processing.research_question: Can natural language processing techniques improve the identification of the creation and impact of new technologies in patents compared to traditional metrics?
hypotheses: NLP-based measures will better identify patents with high novelty and impact compared to traditional metrics.
NLP-based measures will show weak correlation with traditional metrics based on patent classification and citations.
arts2021_natural_language_processing.data_source: USPTO, patent claims research dataset, PATSTAT
arts2021_natural_language_processing.data_description: The dataset includes all granted U.S. utility patents from March 1969 to May 2018 (n=6,252,916). It includes titles, abstracts, and claims of patents.

independent_variables.name: new_word
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique keywords (unigrams) in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: new_bigram
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique bigrams in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: new_trigram
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique trigrams in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: new_keyword_combination
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique keyword combinations in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: cosine_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Cosine similarity between the technical content of a focal patent and all prior patents.

dependent_variables.name: new_word_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new keywords from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: new_bigram_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new bigrams from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: new_trigram_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new trigrams from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: new_keyword_combination_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new keyword combinations from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: cosine_similarity_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Cosine similarity between the content of a focal patent and all succeeding patents.

control_variables.name: patent_class
control_variables.rationale: Patent classification is used as a control variable to account for the subject matter of the patent.

control_variables.name: citation_count
control_variables.rationale: Citation count is used as a control variable to account for the prior art referenced by the patent.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and case-control study designs to validate the NLP-based measures against traditional metrics.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Awards-linked patents are expected to cover radically new technologies with a major impact, while rejected patents are expected to lack novelty and have little impact.

partial_results.note: The weak correlation suggests that NLP-based measures capture different aspects of novelty and impact compared to traditional metrics.

conclusion_claims: NLP-based measures outperform traditional metrics based on patent classification and citations in identifying the creation and impact of new technologies in patents.
New keyword combinations and their reuse by later patents show the strongest discriminatory power to classify both patents linked to prestigious awards and rejected patents.
limitations: The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the limitations of the NLP-based measures.
The validation is based on case-control studies and may not generalize to all patents.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the NLP-based measures against traditional metrics using two distinct groups of patents: awards-linked patents and rejected patents.

_source.title: Natural Language Processing to identify the creation and impact of new technologies in patent text

schaper2025_frontier_scientists.title: Not like the others: Frontier scientists for inventive performance
schaper2025_frontier_scientists.research_idea: The paper investigates the impact of inventors who are also frontier scientists—those who publish in top-general biomedical journals—on the technological and commercial success of corporate inventions. It finds that these inventors produce more impactful patents compared to other inventors, including those who are not at the scientific frontier.
schaper2025_frontier_scientists.research_question: Do corporate inventors who are also frontier scientists produce more impactful inventions compared to other inventors, and what mechanisms explain this impact premium?
hypotheses: Inventions by frontier authors will have higher technology impact as measured by patent citations.
Inventions by frontier authors will be more likely to become technology hits and have broader impact.
The impact premium of frontier-author patents will be stronger in young, scaled-up biopharmaceutical firms and when frontier authors are internally employed.
schaper2025_frontier_scientists.data_source: PubMed-indexed scientific articles, U.S. corporate biomedical patents, and disambiguation data from Torvik (2018) and Li et al. (2014)
schaper2025_frontier_scientists.data_description: The dataset includes all U.S. biomedical patents assigned to firms between 1980 and 2009, and all PubMed-indexed scientific articles published in Science Citation Index (SCI) journals during the same period.

independent_variables.name: frontier_author
independent_variables.definition: Whether the inventor is a frontier author (i.e., has recently published in a top-general biomedical journal).

independent_variables.name: journal_quality
independent_variables.definition: Whether the inventor has published in a top-general journal, a field-specific top journal, or no top journal.

independent_variables.name: publication_recency
independent_variables.definition: Whether the inventor's publication is recent or not.

independent_variables.name: firm_type
independent_variables.definition: Whether the firm is a young, scaled-up biopharmaceutical firm or not.

independent_variables.name: employment_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the inventor is internally employed, externally hired, or an external collaborator.

dependent_variables.name: technology_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patent citations received.

dependent_variables.name: technology_hit
dependent_variables.formula: Whether the patent is classified as a technology hit.

dependent_variables.name: private_value
dependent_variables.formula: Estimated private value of the patent.

dependent_variables.name: claims_breadth
dependent_variables.formula: A measure of the breadth of patent claims.

control_variables.name: inventor_experience
control_variables.rationale: To account for the potential influence of inventor experience on patent impact.

control_variables.name: firm_size
control_variables.rationale: To control for the effect of firm size on patent impact.

control_variables.name: technology_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in impact across different technology fields.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses regression analysis to compare the impact of patents by frontier authors to those by non-frontier authors, controlling for various firm, inventor, and technology characteristics.

network_construction.construction_method: Patent citations are used to construct a directed, weighted, and temporal citation network to assess the impact of frontier-author patents.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of inventions by inventors who are at the scientific frontier with those who are not.

partial_results.note: While frontier-author patents are more likely to reference frontier science, the impact premium is not solely explained by this factor.

conclusion_claims: Inventions by frontier authors have a significant impact premium in terms of technology impact, likelihood of becoming technology hits, and private value.
The impact premium is strongest in young, scaled-up biopharmaceutical firms and when frontier authors are internally employed.
limitations: The study is limited to the biomedical industry and U.S. patents, so the findings may not generalize to other industries or countries.
The paper does not fully disentangle the causal mechanisms behind the impact premium of frontier-author patents.

robustness_checks.method: Using lower benchmarks for journal or article quality
robustness_checks.rationale: To test whether the impact premium is sensitive to the definition of frontier science.
To assess whether the impact premium is specific to general-interest journals or also holds for field-specific journals.
To test whether the recency of publications is a key driver of the impact premium.

_source.title: Not like the others: Frontier scientists for inventive performance

gebhart2023_mathematical_framework.title: A Mathematical Framework for Citation Disruption
gebhart2023_mathematical_framework.research_idea: The paper proposes a mathematical framework for measuring citation disruption through the lens of network centrality, particularly betweenness centrality, and unifies existing disruption measures like the CD Index with broader network science concepts.
gebhart2023_mathematical_framework.research_question: How can citation disruption be formalized using network centrality to unify and generalize existing disruption measures?
hypotheses: Reinterpreting disruption through network centrality will unify existing citation-based disruption measures.
Centrality-based disruption measures will better discern future award-winning scientific innovations compared to conventional metrics.
gebhart2023_mathematical_framework.data_source: Real citation data (not specific datasets mentioned)
gebhart2023_mathematical_framework.data_description: The paper analyzes real citation data to validate the proposed framework, though specific datasets are not named.

independent_variables.name: disruption_measure
independent_variables.definition: Quantification of how disruptive a scientific or technological work is, based on its position in the citation network.

independent_variables.name: betweenness_centrality
independent_variables.definition: A network centrality measure indicating the extent to which a node lies on the shortest paths between other nodes.

dependent_variables.name: future_award_winning_status
dependent_variables.formula: Whether a scientific work is recognized as award-winning in the future.

control_variables.name: citation_count
control_variables.rationale: Citation count is a widely used indicator of scholarly impact and is controlled to isolate the unique contribution of disruption measures.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses network centrality measures and compares disruption metrics computed over ego networks of increasing radius.

network_construction.construction_method: A directed, unweighted graph is constructed where an edge from u to v indicates that paper u cites paper v.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics of disruptive versus non-disruptive scientific contributions.

result_claims.value: higher discernment of future award-winning innovations
less discernment of future award-winning innovations compared to centrality-based measures

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide specific numerical results but validates the theoretical framework through empirical analysis.

conclusion_claims: The proposed centrality-based framework unifies and generalizes existing citation disruption measures.
Centrality-based disruption measures outperform conventional metrics in identifying future award-winning scientific innovations.
limitations: The paper does not specify the exact datasets used for empirical validation.
The theoretical framework is not tested across a wide range of scientific domains.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the proposed centrality-based measures provide better discernment of disruptive works.

_source.title: A Mathematical Framework for Citation Disruption

ke2023_sleeping_beauties.title: Defining and Identifying Sleeping Beauties in Science
ke2023_sleeping_beauties.research_idea: The paper introduces a parameter-free measure called the 'beauty coefficient' to quantify the extent to which a scientific paper can be considered a 'Sleeping Beauty'—a paper whose importance is not recognized for several years after publication. The study reveals that the SB phenomenon is not exceptional and occurs across disciplines.
ke2023_sleeping_beauties.research_question: How common are Sleeping Beauties in science, and what is the nature of their delayed recognition?
hypotheses: Sleeping Beauties are not exceptional but rather part of a continuous spectrum of delayed recognition.
The SB phenomenon is more apparent in multidisciplinary datasets.
ke2023_sleeping_beauties.data_source: Not explicitly mentioned, but it is implied to be a large-scale multidisciplinary bibliographic dataset.
ke2023_sleeping_beauties.data_description: The study analyzes 22 million scientific papers published in all disciplines of natural and social sciences over a time span longer than a century.

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the paper was published.

independent_variables.name: citation_history
independent_variables.definition: The number of citations received by the paper in each year after publication.

independent_variables.name: max_citation_year
independent_variables.definition: The year when the paper received its maximum number of citations.

dependent_variables.name: beauty_coefficient
dependent_variables.formula: B = sum_{t=0}^{tm} (ℓ_t - c_t) / max{1, c_t}, where ℓ_t is the reference line connecting (0, c_0) and (t_m, c_{t_m}), and c_t is the number of citations in year t.

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation patterns across scientific fields.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis to examine the distribution of the beauty coefficient and its relationship with citation dynamics.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations between papers are used to construct a directed, weighted, and temporal network.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation dynamics of papers that exhibit delayed recognition with those that do not.

partial_results.note: The study suggests that SBs achieve delayed importance in disciplines different from their original publication field, but the exact mechanisms are not fully explored.

conclusion_claims: The SB phenomenon is not exceptional but rather part of a continuous spectrum of delayed recognition.
The SB phenomenon is more apparent in multidisciplinary datasets, where papers achieve delayed importance in different fields.
limitations: The study does not explicitly mention the specific bibliographic database used.
The analysis is descriptive and does not provide a causal explanation for the SB phenomenon.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed SB phenomenon is not easily reconciled with simple citation accumulation models.

_source.title: Defining and Identifying Sleeping Beauties in Science

bentley2023_disruption.title: IS DISRUPTION DECREASING, OR IS IT ACCELERATING?
bentley2023_disruption.research_idea: The paper challenges the claim that scientific disruption has decreased over time by introducing a citation-weighted disruption index that accounts for exponential growth in publications and citations. It finds that disruption has remained stable or even increased in recent decades.
bentley2023_disruption.research_question: Is the observed decline in disruption over time a true decline or an artifact of exponential growth in scientific output?
hypotheses: The observed decline in disruption is due to the exponential growth in the number of publications and citations.
A citation-weighted disruption index will show a more accurate trend in disruption over time.
bentley2023_disruption.data_source: Millions of scientific publications and patents from the dataset used in [1], as well as English-language books data from the Google Books Ngram database.
bentley2023_disruption.data_description: The dataset includes annual numbers of citations and publications over eight decades (since 1930), as well as word usage data from English-language books over time.

filters: publications and patents with citation data

independent_variables.name: year
independent_variables.definition: The year of publication, used to track trends over time.

independent_variables.name: citation_count
independent_variables.definition: The number of citations a paper receives per year.

independent_variables.name: exponential_growth
independent_variables.definition: A proxy for the exponential growth in the number of publications and citations over time.

dependent_variables.name: CD5
dependent_variables.formula: The original disruption index, calculated as the average of (f_it - b_it) across all papers in year t, where f_it is 1 if a future paper cites the focal paper and 0 otherwise, and b_it is 1 if a future paper cites a predecessor of the focal paper and 0 otherwise.

dependent_variables.name: mCD5
dependent_variables.formula: The citation-weighted disruption index, calculated as the sum of (f_it - b_it) * cit_i / sum(cit_i) for all papers in year t, where cit_i is the average number of citations per year for paper i.

dependent_variables.name: unique_words_ratio
dependent_variables.formula: The ratio of unique words to total words in the corpus of publications or books in a given year.

control_variables.name: publication_growth_rate
control_variables.rationale: To account for the exponential growth in the number of publications over time.

control_variables.name: citation_growth_rate
control_variables.rationale: To account for the exponential growth in the number of citations over time.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and normalization techniques to analyze trends in disruption and word usage over time.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations between papers are used to construct a directed, weighted, and temporal citation network.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare disruption trends before and after the digital age of publishing.

partial_results.note: The CD5 metric shows a decline over time, but this is attributed to the exponential growth in publications and citations rather than a true decline in disruption.

conclusion_claims: There is little evidence that disruption is decreasing; the citation-weighted disruption index has been close to its expected neutral value over the last fifty years.
Disruption may actually be increasing in the current age of digital publishing, as indicated by a modest but consistent growth in the weighted disruption index since 2000.
limitations: The analysis relies on the same dataset as the original study [1], which may have its own biases or limitations.
The paper does not fully explain why information and publication growth occurs exponentially.

robustness_checks.rationale: To account for the exponential growth in citations and ensure that highly cited papers contribute more to the disruption index.

robustness_checks.method: comparison with word usage data from English-language books
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the claim that the decline in unique words is expected in an exponentially growing corpus.

_source.title: Is disruption decreasing, or is it accelerating?

traag2025_citation_models.title: Science of science—Citation models and research evaluation
traag2025_citation_models.research_idea: This paper reviews the literature on citation distributions and models, and connects it to the field of research evaluation. It emphasizes the importance of causal reasoning and the unobservable nature of research quality in interpreting citation-based indicators.
traag2025_citation_models.research_question: How can citation models and research evaluation be integrated to better understand the role of citations in science and their implications for research quality assessment?
traag2025_citation_models.data_source: Web of Science, American Physics Society (APS), PACS codes, journal subject categories
traag2025_citation_models.data_description: The paper references datasets from Web of Science and APS, covering multiple fields and years. These datasets include citations to papers, authors, institutions, and journals.

independent_variables.name: citation_count
independent_variables.definition: The number of citations received by a paper, author, institution, or journal

independent_variables.name: normalized_citation_count
independent_variables.definition: Citation count normalized by the average citation count in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: field
independent_variables.definition: Scientific field based on journal subject categories or PACS codes

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: Year of publication of the paper

dependent_variables.name: citation_distribution
dependent_variables.formula: Empirical distribution of citations across papers, authors, institutions, or journals

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: Citation patterns vary significantly across scientific fields

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: Citation counts accumulate over time, so year of publication is a confounding factor

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and distribution fitting to analyze citation data. It also discusses normalization techniques and universal scaling.
statistical_method.estimation: Maximum likelihood estimation and goodness-of-fit tests are mentioned for fitting distributions.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are constructed based on citation links between papers, with edges representing citations from one paper to another.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Different levels of analysis (papers, authors, institutions, journals) are compared to understand how citation patterns vary across these entities.

partial_results.note: Some studies suggest universality, while others find field-specific differences, indicating uncertainty in the universality claim.

conclusion_claims: Citation distributions are highly skewed and can be modeled using power laws or lognormals, depending on the normalization method.
Citation-based indicators are influenced by unobservable research quality and require causal reasoning to interpret correctly.
limitations: The unobservable nature of research quality limits the conclusions that can be drawn from citation-based indicators.
Field-specific differences in citation patterns challenge the universality of citation models.

robustness_checks.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across fields and over time
To validate the universality of citation distributions across different data sources

_source.title: Citation models and research evaluation

obadage2023_citations_reproducibility.title: Can Citations Tell Us About a Paper’s Reproducibility? A Case Study of Machine Learning Papers
obadage2023_citations_reproducibility.research_idea: The paper explores the potential of using downstream citation contexts as a signal of reproducibility in machine learning (ML) research. It introduces a sentiment analysis framework to interpret the outcomes of reproduction attempts and explores correlations between citation context sentiment and reproducibility scores.
obadage2023_citations_reproducibility.research_question: Can citation context sentiment be used as a signal to infer the reproducibility of a paper in the field of machine learning?
hypotheses: There is a correlation between reproducibility scores and the sentiment of citation contexts.
ML models can be trained to classify citation contexts into reproducibility sentiments with high accuracy.
obadage2023_citations_reproducibility.data_source: Semantic Scholar Graph API (S2GA), Machine Learning Reproducibility Challenges (MLRC), and manually labeled citation contexts.
obadage2023_citations_reproducibility.data_description: The dataset includes 130 original ML papers, 145 reproducibility studies, 13,314 citations, and 41,244 citation contexts. A subset of 22 papers and 1,937 manually labeled citation contexts were used for training and validation.

independent_variables.name: citation_context_sentiment
independent_variables.definition: The sentiment of the citation context, categorized as positive, negative, or neutral, indicating reproducibility-related cues.

independent_variables.name: normalized_citation_context_count
independent_variables.definition: The normalized count of citation contexts with a certain sentiment.

dependent_variables.name: reproducibility_score
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated based on the reports of direct reproducibility studies, with an extended score for papers with multiple findings.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and correlation analysis to explore the relationship between reproducibility scores and citation context sentiment.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze the distribution of reproducibility scores across different sentiment categories in citation contexts.

result_claims.metric: correlation between reproducibility scores and citation context sentiment

partial_results.metric: correlation between reproducibility scores and citation context sentiment
partial_results.note: The correlation is based on a pilot dataset and may not generalize to the broader ML literature.

conclusion_claims: There is a correlation between reproducibility scores and citation context sentiment in a pilot dataset of ML papers.
ML models can be trained to classify citation contexts into reproducibility sentiments with reasonable accuracy.
limitations: The study is based on a relatively small pilot dataset of 22 papers and 1,937 citation contexts.
The correlation observed may not be generalizable to all ML papers or other fields.

robustness_checks.rationale: To address the skewed distribution of sentiment labels in the ground truth dataset.

_source.title: Can citations tell us about a paper's reproducibility? A case study of ML papers

sun2023_ranking_mobility.title: Ranking mobility and impact inequality in early academic careers
sun2023_ranking_mobility.research_idea: This paper investigates the ranking mobility and impact inequality in early academic careers across 57 disciplines using a random walk model to analyze historical data. It finds that mobility is low for top and bottom-ranked authors and that mobility has increased over time, accompanied by a decline in impact inequality.
sun2023_ranking_mobility.research_question: How difficult is it for an early career academic to climb the ranks of their discipline?
hypotheses: Mobility is the lowest for the top and bottom-ranked authors.
Mobility of impact rankings has increased over time, accompanied by a decline in impact inequality.
sun2023_ranking_mobility.data_description: The dataset includes publication profiles of 5,194,173 authors active in 57 disciplines spanning four macro areas of WoS data: Life Sciences & Medicine, Physical Sciences, Social Sciences, and Technology. The data covers authors whose careers began between 1986 and 2008.

filters: Authors with at least one paper in each five-year window

independent_variables.name: career_start_year
independent_variables.definition: The year an author's career began, identified as the year of their first publication.

independent_variables.name: decile_rank
independent_variables.definition: The decile rank of an author's impact within their cohort based on the sum of citations received within 5 years after publication.

dependent_variables.name: ranking_mobility
dependent_variables.formula: The difference ∆Q between an author’s decile rank in the second and first five career years.

dependent_variables.name: impact_inequality
dependent_variables.formula: The Gini coefficient or similar measure of inequality in impact rankings.

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices and career dynamics across disciplines.

control_variables.name: publication_volume
control_variables.rationale: To account for the number of publications as a potential confounder of impact.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses transition matrices to quantify the probability of authors moving between deciles and compares these to a null model based on random reshuffling. A random walk model is implemented to estimate the diffusion coefficient D, which quantifies the degree of mobility.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the stability and mobility of the top and bottom strata of impact rankings.

result_claims.value: remarkably low for top and bottom-ranked authors

partial_results.note: The results vary across disciplines, with some showing increased stability in the top 10% and others showing increased mobility in the bottom 10%.

conclusion_claims: Ranking mobility is the lowest for the top and bottom-ranked authors, indicating a high degree of stability in these strata.
Mobility of impact rankings has increased over time, accompanied by a decline in impact inequality, suggesting a more equitable distribution of academic impact.
limitations: Citations are used as a proxy for academic impact, which may not fully capture the broader impact of research.
The analysis is limited to authors whose careers began between 1986 and 2008, which may not reflect current trends.

robustness_checks.rationale: To create a null model and compare the observed mobility against a baseline of random transitions.
To quantify the relationship between mobility and inequality and assess the significance of the observed trends.

_source.title: Ranking mobility and impact inequality in early academic careers

akella2023_reproducibility_signals.title: Reproducibility Signals in Science: A preliminary analysis
akella2023_reproducibility_signals.research_idea: The paper investigates features of scholarly articles in computer science that correlate with reproducibility, aiming to identify signals that can help distinguish reproducible from irreproducible research.
akella2023_reproducibility_signals.research_question: What features of scholarly articles in computer science correlate with reproducibility?
hypotheses: The readability of scholarly articles is positively correlated with reproducibility.
The accessibility of software artifacts through hyperlinks is positively correlated with reproducibility.
akella2023_reproducibility_signals.data_source: Brown University dataset, Retraction Watch Database (RetractionDB), and Badged ACM Papers
akella2023_reproducibility_signals.data_description: The dataset includes 305 scholarly articles in computer science, with 206 classified as reproducible and 99 as non-reproducible. Data was collected from three sources: Brown University (207 papers), RetractionDB (34 papers), and Badged ACM Papers (64 papers).

filters: Papers labeled as reproducible or irreproducible
Full text available in PDF format

independent_variables.name: Readability
independent_variables.definition: Measured by the mean of readability metrics such as Flesch Reading Ease Level, SMOG Index, Coleman-Liau index, etc.

independent_variables.name: Software artifact accessibility
independent_variables.definition: Whether the article provides hyperlinks to software artifacts.

independent_variables.name: Number of sections
independent_variables.definition: The count of specific sections in the article.

independent_variables.name: Number of tables
independent_variables.definition: The count of tables in the article.

independent_variables.name: Number of figures
independent_variables.definition: The count of figures in the article.

independent_variables.name: Number of algorithms
independent_variables.definition: The count of algorithms in the article.

independent_variables.name: Average word length
independent_variables.definition: The average length of words in the article.

independent_variables.name: Average sentence length
independent_variables.definition: The average length of sentences in the article.

independent_variables.name: Frequency of words greater than average word length
independent_variables.definition: The frequency of words longer than the average word length in the article.

independent_variables.name: Syllable count
independent_variables.definition: The total number of syllables in the article.

independent_variables.name: Yule’s I measure of lexical diversity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of lexical diversity in the article.

independent_variables.name: Complex words
independent_variables.definition: The number of polysyllable words in the article.

independent_variables.name: Sentiment score
independent_variables.definition: A sentiment score for the full text of the article.

dependent_variables.name: Reproducibility
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the article is classified as reproducible, 0 if it is classified as non-reproducible.

statistical_method.specification: Point biserial correlation and significance tests such as the Mann-Whitney U test and Chi-squared test were used to identify significant features.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the features of reproducible and non-reproducible scholarly articles.

partial_results.note: The results section is incomplete in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: Readability of scholarly articles is a strong signal for reproducibility.
Accessibility of software artifacts through hyperlinks is a strong signal for reproducibility.
limitations: The dataset is limited to computer science publications.
The analysis is preliminary and may not generalize to other disciplines.

robustness_checks.rationale: To test the significance of differences between reproducible and non-reproducible articles.
To test the significance of categorical features between reproducible and non-reproducible articles.

_source.title: Reproducibility Signals in Science: A preliminary analysis

luo2024_hunting_room_temperature_superco.title: Hunting for Room Temperature Superconductors
luo2024_hunting_room_temperature_superco.research_idea: The paper reviews the historical and current efforts in the search for room temperature superconductors (RTS), highlighting the challenges, false claims, and potential pathways for discovery.
luo2024_hunting_room_temperature_superco.research_question: What are the possible strategies and challenges in the search for room temperature superconductors?
hypotheses: Room temperature superconductors can be discovered through high-pressure hydrides.
Unconventional pairing mechanisms can lead to higher Tc superconductivity.
Two-dimensional interface superconductivity can enable room temperature superconductivity.
Artificial intelligence can predict new superconducting materials.
Highly disordered materials may host room temperature superconductivity.

statistical_method.specification: The paper provides a descriptive analysis of the history, challenges, and potential pathways for room temperature superconductivity.

conclusion_claims: The discovery of room temperature superconductors remains a major goal in condensed matter physics.
Multiple pathways, including high-pressure hydrides, unconventional pairing mechanisms, and AI-driven discovery, offer potential for achieving room temperature superconductivity.
limitations: Many reported room temperature superconductors have not been reproducible.
Theoretical understanding of unconventional superconductivity is still incomplete.
High-pressure conditions required for some materials are difficult to achieve and maintain.

vasarhelyi2023_who_benefits.title: Who benefits from altmetrics? The effect of team gender composition on the link between online visibility and citation impact
vasarhelyi2023_who_benefits.research_idea: This paper investigates whether online visibility helps female researchers overcome the gender-based citation penalty and explores how team gender composition interacts with online visibility in affecting citation impact across different research areas.
vasarhelyi2023_who_benefits.research_question: Does online visibility help female researchers overcome the gender-based citation penalty, and how does team gender composition interact with online visibility in affecting citation impact across different research areas?
hypotheses: Online visibility positively affects citation impact.
The effect of online visibility on citation impact varies by team gender composition and research area.
vasarhelyi2023_who_benefits.data_source: Web of Science, Altmetric, Open Academic Graph
vasarhelyi2023_who_benefits.data_description: The dataset includes research articles from the broad research areas of Computer Science, Engineering, and Social Sciences. It includes altmetric data (number of article shares on 14 major online platforms) and citation data (number of citations within five years of publication). Additional metrics include co-author team size and each author’s h-index at the time of publication.

filters: Articles with altmetric data and citation data

independent_variables.name: online_visibility
independent_variables.definition: Total number of article shares on 14 major online platforms tracked by Altmetric.

independent_variables.name: team_gender_composition
independent_variables.definition: Gender composition of the co-author team, particularly the gender of the first and last authors.

dependent_variables.name: citation_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received within five years of publication.

control_variables.name: co_author_team_size
control_variables.rationale: Larger teams may have more visibility and citations.

control_variables.name: author_h_index
control_variables.rationale: Author’s h-index at the time of publication may influence citation impact.

control_variables.name: research_area
control_variables.rationale: Different research areas have distinct citation cultures and online dissemination practices.

statistical_method.specification: Coarsened Exact Matching (CEM) is used to identify statistical twins (pairs of similar articles where one was successful online and the other was not). Regression models are then run on the matched data to isolate the effects of online visibility, team gender composition, and their interaction.

groups: Articles with male first and last authors
Articles with female first and/or last authors
grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation impact of articles with different team gender compositions and assess whether online visibility helps female researchers overcome the gender-based citation penalty.

key_quantities: Interaction effect of team gender composition and online visibility

result_claims.value: Citation benefit for teams with female first and/or last authors

partial_results.note: The result depends on the research area, which invites discussions about custom strategies to promote citation equity.

conclusion_claims: Online visibility positively affects citation impact across research areas.
Team gender composition interacts differently with online visibility in different research areas, suggesting the need for area-specific strategies to promote citation equity.
limitations: The study is limited to three broad research areas (Computer Science, Engineering, and Social Sciences).
The use of name-based algorithmic gender inference may introduce errors, although the authors devised a method to emulate and account for these errors.

robustness_checks.method: Emulation of errors in gender labeling
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the robustness of the models to likely error levels in gender inference.

_source.title: Who benefits from altmetrics? The effect of team gender composition

deng2023_enhancing_disruption.title: Enhancing the robustness of the disruption metric against noise
deng2023_enhancing_disruption.research_idea: The paper proposes an improved disruption metric to better distinguish highly innovative scientific papers from developmental ones by eliminating the impact of highly cited references. The new method retains all node information in the citation network while reducing the influence of hot-spot papers.
deng2023_enhancing_disruption.research_question: How can the disruption metric be improved to better distinguish innovative papers from developmental ones and increase its robustness against noise from highly cited references?
hypotheses: The modified disruption metric will better distinguish Nobel prize winning papers and Milestone Letters from review articles.
The new disruption metric will be more robust against random link removal and the influence of highly cited references.
deng2023_enhancing_disruption.data_description: The dataset includes 482,566 papers published from 1893 to 2010, with author names disambiguated by Sinatra et al. (2016).

filters: Review articles from Review of Modern Physics

independent_variables.name: hot_spot_elimination_percentile
independent_variables.definition: The fraction of top highly cited papers (hot-spot papers) eliminated from the citation network.

dependent_variables.name: disruption
dependent_variables.formula: disruption = ni + nj + nk, where ni is the number of papers citing only the focal paper, nj is the number of papers citing both the focal paper and its references, and nk is the number of papers citing only the references.

dependent_variables.name: new_disruption
dependent_variables.formula: new_disruption = ni + nj + nk, where edges to hot-spot papers are removed, effectively increasing ni and decreasing nj.

control_variables.name: citation_window
control_variables.rationale: The time period considered for citations can affect the disruption metric, as disruption stabilizes after several years.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and rank comparisons to evaluate the performance of the new disruption metric.

network_construction.construction_method: The citation network is built using the references and citing papers of each focal paper. Edges to hot-spot papers are removed in the modified disruption metric.

grouping_strategy.rationale: These groups represent different levels of innovation and disruption. Nobel and Milestone papers are expected to be more disruptive, while review articles are expected to be less disruptive.

key_quantities: average rank of Nobel and Milestone papers
Kendall’s τ between original and new ranks

result_claims.metric: average rank of Nobel prize winning papers
Kendall’s τ between original and new ranks
result_claims.value: decreases with higher fraction of hot-spot papers eliminated

partial_results.note: The optimal fraction is 3%, but the paper does not provide a statistical justification for this value.

conclusion_claims: The modified disruption metric better distinguishes innovative papers from developmental ones.
The new disruption metric is more robust against the influence of highly cited references and random link removal.
limitations: The dataset is limited to physics papers from the American Physical Society.
The paper does not provide a statistical justification for the optimal hot-spot elimination percentile.

robustness_checks.rationale: To test the robustness of the new disruption metric against random noise in the citation network.

_source.title: Enhancing the robustness of the disruption metric against noise

galuez2023_technology_transfer.title: Scientific literature cited in patents: A Technology Transfer indicator in the Portuguese universities
galuez2023_technology_transfer.venue: Revista Ibérica de Sistemas e Tecnologias de Informação
galuez2023_technology_transfer.research_idea: The study aims to identify the process of transfer from science to technology in the main Portuguese public universities by analyzing the scientific literature cited in patents.
galuez2023_technology_transfer.research_question: What is the evolution of the scientific literature cited in patents and what are the main research fronts in the context of Portuguese universities?
hypotheses: If scientific publications are typically assessed by citations in scientific journals, then citations in patents could similarly be used to assess the technological impact of scientific publications.
galuez2023_technology_transfer.data_description: The study retrieved 10,514 scientific articles cited in patents from the Lens database, focusing on six major public universities in Portugal.

filters: Only public universities in Portugal with the highest number of scientific publications cited in patents were selected

independent_variables.name: Scientific Discipline
independent_variables.definition: The scientific field in which the cited research is conducted (e.g., Chemistry, Biology, Materials Sciences, Medicine).

independent_variables.name: Research Front
independent_variables.definition: The main research areas identified through the analysis of cited literature (e.g., cancer, nanoparticles, biomaterials, tissue engineering, molecular biology).

dependent_variables.name: Citations in Patents
dependent_variables.formula: The number of scientific articles cited in patents.

control_variables.name: Institution
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in research output and citation patterns among universities.

statistical_method.specification: A descriptive analysis of the data was performed, including the creation of science maps to visualize the main research trends.

network_construction.construction_method: Science maps were created to visualize the main research trends based on citations in patents.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the research output and citation patterns among the most productive Portuguese public universities.

result_claims.value: Cancer, nanoparticles, biomaterials, tissue engineering, molecular biology

partial_results.note: The temporal evolution of the scientific literature cited in patents was not explicitly analyzed in detail.

conclusion_claims: The research produced by Portuguese universities has generated relevant knowledge for patented inventions.
The science-technology flow is evident in specific areas such as Chemistry, Biology, Materials Sciences, and Medicine.
limitations: The study focuses only on public universities in Portugal and excludes other higher education institutions such as polytechnic institutes and private universities.
The temporal evolution of the scientific literature cited in patents was not thoroughly analyzed.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the findings by visualizing the main research trends and validating the descriptive results.

baccini2025_coara.title: COARA WILL NOT SAVE SCIENCE FROM THE TYRANNY OF ADMINISTRATIVE EVALUATION
baccini2025_coara.research_idea: The paper critically examines the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) principles through the lens of Philip Kitcher’s concept of well-ordered science, arguing that CoARA's approach to research evaluation is inconsistent with democratic ideals and may perpetuate the 'tyranny of expertise' and 'tyranny of ignorance.'
baccini2025_coara.research_question: Whether the generalized adoption of the CoARA principles could surely make the evaluation of research desirable and solve all its problems.
hypotheses: CoARA's approach to research evaluation is inconsistent with the principles of well-ordered science.
Administrative evaluation of research, even if correct according to CoARA principles, is at odds with the principles of well-ordered science.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses philosophical and theoretical analysis rather than statistical methods.

conclusion_claims: The generalized adoption of CoARA principles will not make research evaluation desirable or solve all its problems.
CoARA's approach to research evaluation is at odds with the principles of well-ordered science as defined by Philip Kitcher.
limitations: The paper does not present empirical data or statistical analysis to support its claims.
The analysis is based on theoretical and philosophical arguments rather than quantitative evidence.

wanga2023_preprints.title: The role of preprints in open science: Accelerating knowledge transfer from science to technology
wanga2023_preprints.research_idea: This study investigates the role of preprints in accelerating knowledge transfer from science to technology by analyzing patent citations to bioRxiv preprints. It provides empirical evidence that preprints contribute to more efficient linkage between science and technology, suggesting an acceleration in the pace of innovation.
wanga2023_preprints.research_question: How do preprints influence technological innovation and knowledge translation within the open science framework?
hypotheses: Preprints accelerate the dissemination of scientific knowledge into technological innovation.
Preprints enhance the visibility of early research findings in the technological innovation process.
Preprints differ from journal papers in terms of accelerating the dissemination and application of scientific knowledge in technological innovation and scholarly communication.
wanga2023_preprints.data_description: The study analyzes patent citations to bioRxiv preprints submitted between 2013 and 2021. It also compares patent citations to preprints with those to journal papers.

independent_variables.name: preprint_citation
independent_variables.definition: Whether a patent cites a bioRxiv preprint.

independent_variables.name: journal_citation
independent_variables.definition: Whether a patent cites a journal paper.

independent_variables.name: preprint_journal_version
independent_variables.definition: Whether a journal paper has a preprint version.

dependent_variables.name: citation_speed
dependent_variables.formula: Time between preprint/journal publication and patent citation.

dependent_variables.name: citation_frequency
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents citing a preprint or journal paper.

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior across scientific fields.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To control for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: funding_source
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of funding on research impact.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics to analyze trends in patent citations to preprints and journal papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of preprints versus journal papers on technological innovation and to explore differences in citation behavior.

result_claims.value: Growing trend of patent citations to bioRxiv preprints, with a notable surge in 2020.
Preprints expedite the dissemination of scientific knowledge into technological innovation.

partial_results.note: The study does not provide statistical significance for the observed differences in citation speed.

conclusion_claims: Preprints play a critical role in accelerating innovation by expediting the dissemination of scientific knowledge into technological innovation.
The open science model, particularly the 'open access' effect of preprints, amplifies the impact of science on technological innovation.
limitations: The study focuses only on bioRxiv preprints and may not generalize to other preprint servers.
The analysis is descriptive and does not include statistical modeling to test the significance of observed trends.

robustness_checks.method: Comparative analysis of preprints and journal papers
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed trends are not due to differences in the nature of the publications.

hossain2025_similarity_reproducibility.title: Similarity-Based Assessment of Computational Reproducibility in Jupyter Notebooks
hossain2025_similarity_reproducibility.research_idea: The paper introduces the Similarity-based Reproducibility Index (SRI), a metric for assessing the reproducibility of results in Jupyter Notebooks by comparing rerun outputs against original outputs using similarity metrics for different types of Python objects.
hossain2025_similarity_reproducibility.research_question: How can computational reproducibility in Jupyter Notebooks be quantitatively assessed using similarity metrics?
hypotheses: A higher similarity between original and rerun outputs indicates better reproducibility.
The proposed SRI metric can effectively quantify reproducibility across different types of outputs in Jupyter Notebooks.
hossain2025_similarity_reproducibility.data_source: Jupyter Notebooks and their rerun versions
hossain2025_similarity_reproducibility.data_description: The data consists of Jupyter Notebooks and their rerun versions, with outputs from code cells categorized into stream, display_data, execute_result, and error outputs. The case study involves 15 notebooks.

independent_variables.name: output_similarity
independent_variables.definition: The similarity between original and rerun outputs, measured using specific metrics for different output types (e.g., text, image, data structures).

dependent_variables.name: reproducibility_score
dependent_variables.formula: The SRI score for each cell, calculated as a value in the range [0, 1] based on the similarity between original and rerun outputs.

control_variables.name: output_type
control_variables.rationale: Different output types (e.g., stream, display_data, execute_result) may require different similarity metrics, so controlling for output type ensures accurate comparisons.

statistical_method.specification: The SRI is calculated using similarity metrics for different output types, and the results are aggregated to compute an overall reproducibility score for the notebook.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Comparing original and rerun notebooks allows for the assessment of reproducibility by evaluating the similarity of their outputs.

result_claims.value: Quantitative reproducibility score in the range [0, 1]
Insights into reproducibility aspects of outputs

partial_results.metric: SRI score for specific output types
partial_results.note: The SRI score may vary depending on the type of output (e.g., text, image, data structure), and the effectiveness of the metric for each type is not fully evaluated.

conclusion_claims: The proposed SRI metric provides a quantitative and qualitative assessment of computational reproducibility in Jupyter Notebooks.
The case study demonstrates the effectiveness of using similarity metrics to quantify reproducibility across different types of outputs.
limitations: The SRI metric may not account for all factors affecting reproducibility, such as changes in library versions or computational environments.
The effectiveness of the metric for specific output types (e.g., images) may require further validation.

robustness_checks.rationale: The case study provides empirical validation of the SRI metric by applying it to a set of Jupyter Notebooks and visualizing the results.

maddi2024_peer_review_reports.title: On the peer review reports: does size matter?
maddi2024_peer_review_reports.research_idea: This study investigates the relationship between the length of peer review reports and the citation impact of scholarly publications, aiming to determine whether longer reports contribute to higher-quality and more visible articles.
maddi2024_peer_review_reports.research_question: Does the length of peer review reports influence the citation impact of scholarly publications?
hypotheses: Longer peer review reports are associated with higher citation impact due to the requested improvements enhancing the quality of the manuscripts.
The length of peer review reports is a significant predictor of citation impact after controlling for other variables.
maddi2024_peer_review_reports.data_description: The study uses a dataset of 57,482 publications from Publons linked to Web of Science. The dataset includes peer review reports and bibliometric indicators such as citations.

filters: Publications with peer review reports available on Publons
Publications indexed in Web of Science

independent_variables.name: length_of_reviewer_reports
independent_variables.definition: The number of words in the peer review reports.

dependent_variables.name: citation_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by the publication.

control_variables.name: journal_impact_factor
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the journal's prestige on citation impact.

control_variables.name: open_access_status
control_variables.rationale: To account for the potential effect of open access on visibility and citations.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To control for temporal trends in citation behavior.

statistical_method.specification: A regression analysis is performed to examine the relationship between the length of peer review reports and citation impact, while controlling for other variables.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation impact of publications based on the length of their peer review reports.

partial_results.note: The relationship between the length of peer review reports and citation impact is observed, but the study acknowledges that citations are influenced by sociological and strategic factors.

conclusion_claims: Longer peer review reports are significantly associated with higher citation impact, suggesting that they contribute to the quality and visibility of scholarly publications.
The study highlights the importance of timely and comprehensive peer review reports and suggests that open access to reports may encourage more detailed reviews.
limitations: Citations are a contested proxy for quality, as they can be influenced by sociological, strategic, and stochastic factors.
