The study does not account for the content or quality of the peer review reports, only their length.

robustness_checks.rationale: To neutralize the effects of variables affecting citation impact and the length of peer review reports.

zheng2025_male_female_retractions.title: Do male leading authors retract more articles than female leading authors?
zheng2025_male_female_retractions.research_idea: This study investigates gender disparities in scientific article retractions, focusing on differences in retraction rates between male and female first authors in relation to their research productivity. It provides insights into the role of gender in research integrity and scholarly publishing.
zheng2025_male_female_retractions.research_question: Do male leading (i.e., first and corresponding) authors have a higher rate of article retractions compared to female leading authors, relative to their research productivity?
hypotheses: Male leading authors have a higher overall retraction rate than female leading authors.
Gender disparities in retractions vary across retraction reasons and subject fields.
zheng2025_male_female_retractions.data_description: The dataset includes 11,622 retracted articles and 19,475,437 non-retracted articles published between 2008 and 2023.
available_fields: gender of first and corresponding authors

filters: articles labeled as 'retracted publication' in Web of Science

independent_variables.name: gender_of_first_author
independent_variables.definition: Gender of the first author (male or female)

independent_variables.name: gender_of_corresponding_author
independent_variables.definition: Gender of the corresponding author (male or female)

independent_variables.name: retraction_reason
independent_variables.definition: Reason for retraction (e.g., plagiarism, authorship disputes, ethical issues, duplication, fabrication/falsification)

independent_variables.name: subject_field
independent_variables.definition: Scientific discipline of the article (e.g., biomedical and health sciences, life and earth sciences, mathematics and computer science)

dependent_variables.name: retraction_rate
dependent_variables.formula: Number of retracted articles per author, normalized by research productivity

control_variables.name: research_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the number of articles published by male and female authors

control_variables.name: country
control_variables.rationale: To control for potential country-specific differences in research practices and retraction policies

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and comparative analysis to examine gender differences in retraction rates across retraction reasons, subject fields, and countries.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare retraction rates and patterns between genders

result_claims.value: Higher for male first authors in misconduct-related reasons (plagiarism, authorship disputes, ethical issues, duplication, fabrication/falsification)
Higher for male first authors in biomedical and health sciences, life and earth sciences; higher for female first authors in mathematics and computer science

partial_results.note: No significant gender differences were found in retractions attributed to mistakes.

conclusion_claims: Male first authors have higher retraction rates, particularly for scientific misconduct such as plagiarism, authorship disputes, ethical issues, duplication, and fabrication/falsification.
Understanding these gendered patterns of retraction may contribute to strategies aimed at reducing their prevalence.
limitations: The study is based on self-reported or publicly available gender data, which may not be fully accurate.
The dataset may not capture all retracted articles, as some retractions may not be indexed in Web of Science or Retraction Watch.

robustness_checks.method: Comparative analysis across different subject fields and countries
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed gender disparities are not driven by specific disciplines or regions.

zheng2025_social_media_retraction.title: Can social media provide early warning of retraction? Evidence from critical tweets identified by human annotation and large language models
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.venue: Journal of the Association for Information Science and Technology
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.research_idea: This study explores whether social media commentary, particularly critical tweets, can serve as an early warning signal for retracted scholarly articles. It combines human annotation and large language models (LLMs) to identify critical tweets and assess their potential as indicators of problematic research.
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.research_question: Can social media commentary, specifically critical tweets, serve as an early warning signal for scholarly articles that are later retracted?
hypotheses: Retracted articles are more likely to be associated with critical tweets than non-retracted articles.
Human annotation and LLMs will identify similar patterns of critical tweets related to retracted articles.
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.data_source: Twitter, Crossref, and journal retraction databases
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.data_description: The study analyzed 3,815 tweets referencing 604 retracted articles and 3,373 tweets referencing 668 non-retracted articles. The tweets were collected from Twitter and annotated for critical content.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly specified, but data collection occurred before 2025
filters: Tweets referencing retracted and non-retracted articles

independent_variables.name: retraction_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the article was retracted (1) or not (0)

independent_variables.name: critical_tweet
independent_variables.definition: Whether the tweet was identified as critical of the article (1) or not (0)

dependent_variables.name: critical_tweet_association
dependent_variables.formula: Whether an article was associated with at least one critical tweet prior to retraction (1) or not (0)

control_variables.name: article_publication_date
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time between article publication and tweet posting

control_variables.name: article_field
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in disciplinary context that may influence social media engagement

statistical_method.specification: The study primarily uses descriptive statistics to compare the proportion of retracted and non-retracted articles associated with critical tweets.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the likelihood of critical tweets being associated with retracted versus non-retracted articles.

key_quantities: Proportion of retracted articles with critical tweets
Proportion of non-retracted articles with critical tweets

result_claims.metric: Proportion of retracted articles with critical tweets
Proportion of non-retracted articles with critical tweets
Alignment between human annotation and LLMs

partial_results.metric: LLM performance in identifying critical tweets
partial_results.note: LLMs only partially aligned with human annotation, suggesting limitations in fully automated monitoring.

conclusion_claims: Critical tweets identified by human annotation are more common for retracted articles than for non-retracted articles, suggesting their potential as early warning signals.
A human-AI collaborative approach may offer a more reliable and scalable method for monitoring post-publication discourse and identifying problematic research.
limitations: The study relies on a relatively small sample of retracted and non-retracted articles.
The use of LLMs for identifying critical tweets may introduce noise or miss context, highlighting the need for human oversight.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison of human annotation and LLM results
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the reliability of LLMs in identifying critical tweets and highlight the need for human-AI collaboration.

donner2024_data_inaccuracy.title: Data inaccuracy quantification and uncertainty propagation for bibliometric indicators
donner2024_data_inaccuracy.research_idea: This study introduces a method to estimate the uncertainty in bibliometric indicator values caused by data errors using Bayesian regression models and Monte Carlo simulation.
donner2024_data_inaccuracy.research_question: How can uncertainty in bibliometric indicator values be quantified and propagated due to data inaccuracies?
donner2024_data_inaccuracy.data_source: Empirical data samples and real bibliometric research evaluation data
donner2024_data_inaccuracy.data_description: The data includes citation counts and document type assignments, with correct and observed (possibly erroneous) values collected for analysis.

independent_variables.name: observed_citation_count
independent_variables.definition: The number of citations recorded in bibliometric databases, which may contain errors

independent_variables.name: document_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of document (e.g., article, review) as recorded in the database, which may be misclassified

dependent_variables.name: error_free_citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Predicted citation count after accounting for data errors using Bayesian regression models

statistical_method.specification: Bayesian regression models are estimated from empirical data samples to predict error-free values of variables. Monte Carlo simulation is used to propagate uncertainty.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Not applicable

partial_results.note: Only two out of many known bibliometric error categories are considered, so the results likely underestimate the full uncertainty

conclusion_claims: Uncertainty in bibliometric indicator values due to data errors can be quantified using Bayesian regression models and Monte Carlo simulation.
Citation impact scores of research groups often have large margins of error due to data inaccuracies and should be used with caution.
limitations: Only two out of many known bibliometric error categories are considered, leading to an underestimation of total uncertainty.
The method does not account for all sources of data inaccuracies.

robustness_checks.rationale: Used to propagate uncertainty and validate the robustness of the estimated indicator score distributions

suebsombut2023_smart_farm.title: The using of bibliometric analysis to classify trends and future directions on 'Smart Farm'
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.research_idea: This study explores the research trends and identifies research clusters on smart farm using bibliometric analysis to support farming and improve the quality of farm production.
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.research_question: What are the research trends and clusters in the field of smart farm, and how can they inform future research directions?
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.data_source: Web of Science (WOS) and Scopus online database
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.data_description: The study examines selected research articles in the smart farm field, focusing on keywords related to smart farm, agriculture, supply chain, knowledge management, traceability, and product lifecycle management.

statistical_method.specification: Bibliometric analysis is used to explore the relationship of the articles from a co-citation network of the articles, and science mapping is used to identify clusters in the relationship.

network_construction.construction_method: The co-citation network of the articles is used to explore the relationship of the articles, and science mapping is used to identify clusters in the relationship.

groups: soil carbon emission from farming activity
grouping_strategy.rationale: The area of research in smart farm is categorized into two clusters based on the VOSviewer tool with keywords related to research articles.

result_claims.metric: major cluster of smart farm research
result_claims.value: soil carbon emission from farming activity

partial_results.note: The study identifies two clusters but does not provide detailed statistical significance for the clusters.

conclusion_claims: The major cluster of smart farm research is the soil carbon emission from farming activity, which impacts on climate change that affects food production and productivity.
The contribution is to identify the trends on smart farm to develop research in the future by means of bibliometric analysis.
limitations: The study does not provide detailed statistical significance for the identified research clusters.
The time period of the data is not specified.

rosendo2023_kheops.title: KheOps: Cost-effective Repeatability, Reproducibility, and Replicability of Edge-to-Cloud Experiments
rosendo2023_kheops.research_idea: The paper introduces KheOps, a collaborative environment designed to enable cost-effective repeatability, reproducibility, and replicability of Edge-to-Cloud experiments. It addresses the limitations of existing platforms and provides a methodology for deploying experiments on heterogeneous resources.
rosendo2023_kheops.research_question: How can Edge-to-Cloud experiments be made cost-effectively repeatable, reproducible, and replicable across different testbeds?
hypotheses: KheOps will enable authors to perform reproducible experiments on testbeds like Grid5000 and FIT IoT LAB.
KheOps will allow readers to cost-effectively replicate experiments on different testbeds like Chameleon Cloud and CHI@Edge with high accuracy.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive analysis to evaluate the effectiveness of KheOps in enabling reproducibility and replicability of experiments.

result_claims.value: Authors can perform reproducible experiments on Grid5000 + FIT IoT LAB testbeds
Readers can replicate experiments on Chameleon Cloud + CHI@Edge testbeds with >88% accuracy for all performance metrics

conclusion_claims: KheOps provides a cost-effective solution for enabling repeatability, reproducibility, and replicability of Edge-to-Cloud experiments.
KheOps helps authors and readers achieve consistent results across different testbeds with high accuracy.
limitations: The paper does not provide quantitative statistical analysis of the results.
The evaluation is based on a single real-life application case study.

fletcher2024_reproducibility.title: Reproducibility study of “LICO: Explainable Models with Language-Image Consistency”
fletcher2024_reproducibility.research_idea: This paper investigates the reproducibility of the LICO method, which aims to improve model interpretability and classification performance by leveraging natural language supervision from a vision-language model.
fletcher2024_reproducibility.research_question: Can the claims made by Lei et al. (2023) regarding the LICO method be reproduced, particularly in terms of improved classification performance and interpretability?
hypotheses: Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves quantitative and qualitative model interpretability.
Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves classification accuracy.
Tasks with more classification categories benefit more from LICO because the prompt guidance comes from a manifold consisting of more classes.
fletcher2024_reproducibility.data_source: CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and subsets of ImageNet
fletcher2024_reproducibility.data_description: CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are benchmark datasets for image classification with 10 and 100 classes, respectively. ImageNet is a large-scale image dataset with subsets used for evaluation.

independent_variables.name: LICO_training
independent_variables.definition: Whether the model was trained with the LICO method (1) or without it (0).

independent_variables.name: number_of_classes
independent_variables.definition: The number of classification categories in the dataset (e.g., 10 for CIFAR-10, 100 for CIFAR-100).

dependent_variables.name: classification_accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Proportion of correctly classified images.

dependent_variables.name: interpretability_score
dependent_variables.formula: Quantitative measure of interpretability derived from Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and RISE methods.

control_variables.name: model_architecture
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in performance are not due to variations in model architecture.

control_variables.name: training_procedure
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in performance are not due to variations in training procedures.

statistical_method.specification: Comparative analysis of classification accuracy and interpretability scores between models trained with and without LICO.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance and interpretability of models trained with and without the LICO method.

partial_results.note: The effect of LICO on tasks with more classification categories could not be conclusively determined due to incomplete data.

conclusion_claims: The claims made by Lei et al. (2023) regarding the LICO method could not be consistently reproduced.
The study highlights the importance of rigorous evaluation and transparent reporting in interpretability research.
limitations: The original codebase was incomplete, requiring manual implementation of certain components.
The study focused on a subset of the original experiments, which may not fully capture the scope of the LICO method.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with multiple interpretability methods (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, RISE)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not dependent on a single interpretability method.

robustness_checks.method: Evaluation on multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet subsets)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are generalizable across different image classification tasks.

_source.title: Reproducibility study of "LICO: Explainable Models with Language-Image Consistency"

hanson2023_strain_publishing.title: The strain on scientific publishing
hanson2023_strain_publishing.research_idea: The paper investigates the exponential growth in scientific publications and its implications for the quality and sustainability of scientific publishing. It introduces five data-driven metrics to analyze the strain on the publishing system.
hanson2023_strain_publishing.research_question: What factors are contributing to the strain on scientific publishing, and how can this strain be addressed?
hypotheses: The exponential growth in scientific publications is outpacing the growth in the number of practicing scientists.
The adoption of special issues by publishers contributes to the strain on scientific publishing.
The increase in journal impact factors is a result of the strain on publishing rather than an indicator of quality.
hanson2023_strain_publishing.data_source: Scopus, Web of Science, web scrapes, and direct requests to publishers
hanson2023_strain_publishing.data_description: The data includes millions of papers from leading academic publishers, focusing on the period 2016-2022. The dataset includes metrics such as total articles indexed, share of articles in special issues, article turnaround times, rejection rates, and a new metric called 'impact inflation'.

filters: journals indexed in both Scopus and Web of Science

independent_variables.name: total_articles
independent_variables.definition: Total number of articles indexed in Scopus and Web of Science

independent_variables.name: special_issues
independent_variables.definition: Number of articles published in special issues

independent_variables.name: turnaround_time
independent_variables.definition: Time from submission to acceptance of an article

independent_variables.name: rejection_rate
independent_variables.definition: Proportion of submitted articles that are rejected

independent_variables.name: impact_inflation
independent_variables.definition: A new metric based on citation behaviors indicating inflation in journal impact factors

dependent_variables.name: strain_on_publishing
dependent_variables.formula: Derived from the five metrics (total articles, special issues, turnaround times, rejection rates, impact inflation)

control_variables.name: publisher_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in business models (subscription-based vs. gold open access)

control_variables.name: journal_prestige
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of journal-level prestige metrics such as Impact Factor (IF) and Scimago Journal Rank (SJR)

statistical_method.specification: The analysis is primarily descriptive, using comparative metrics to identify trends and patterns in publishing data.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare how different publisher business models and journal prestige levels contribute to the strain on scientific publishing.

result_claims.value: disproportionate growth in articles published in special issues
reduced turnaround times for articles in special issues
widespread year-over-year inflation of journal impact factors

partial_results.note: The relationship between impact inflation and quality signals is not fully established and requires further investigation.

conclusion_claims: The exponential growth in scientific publications is outpacing the growth in the number of practicing scientists, leading to a strain on the publishing system.
The strain on scientific publishing is not strictly tied to any one publisher business model, although some behaviors are associated with specific gold open access publishers.
The metrics introduced in this study can help inform actionable solutions to address the strain on scientific publishing.
limitations: The study focuses on journals indexed in both Scopus and Web of Science, which may not represent the full spectrum of scientific publishing.
The analysis is primarily descriptive and does not establish causal relationships between the identified metrics and the strain on publishing.

robustness_checks.method: comparison with alternative datasets (e.g., Dimensions, OpenAlex)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the findings are not specific to the Scopus and Web of Science datasets.

lin2023_moprd.title: MOPRD: A multidisciplinary open peer review dataset
lin2023_moprd.research_idea: The paper introduces MOPRD, a multidisciplinary open peer review dataset that includes data covering the entire peer review process and spans multiple disciplines. It also proposes a modular guided review comment generation method and explores other potential applications of the dataset.
lin2023_moprd.research_question: How can a multidisciplinary open peer review dataset be constructed to support a wide range of peer review-related research and applications?
hypotheses: MOPRD will provide a more complete and diverse dataset compared to existing peer review datasets.
The modular guided review comment generation method will outperform existing methods in terms of performance.
lin2023_moprd.data_source: Data were collected from journals across different disciplines using special crawlers with native and auto-labeling labels.
lin2023_moprd.data_description: MOPRD includes paper metadata, initial submissions, revised manuscripts, review comments, meta-reviews, author rebuttal letters, and editorial decisions. The dataset is multidisciplinary and covers the entire peer review process.

independent_variables.name: peer_review_process_data
independent_variables.definition: Includes paper metadata, manuscripts, review comments, meta-reviews, author rebuttal letters, and editorial decisions.

dependent_variables.name: review_comment_generation_performance
dependent_variables.formula: Measured using automatic metrics and human evaluation.

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To ensure the dataset is multidisciplinary and not limited to a single field.

statistical_method.specification: The paper does not describe a specific statistical model but evaluates the performance of the modular guided review comment generation method using automatic metrics and human evaluation.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Not applicable

result_claims.significance: Indicated by both automatic metrics and human evaluation

partial_results.note: The paper explores this application but does not provide specific results.

conclusion_claims: MOPRD is a multidisciplinary open peer review dataset that covers the entire peer review process and supports a wide range of research and applications.
The modular guided review comment generation method based on MOPRD delivers better performance compared to existing methods.
limitations: The paper does not provide specific details on the size of the dataset.
The paper does not provide detailed results for all explored applications, such as editorial decision prediction.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the performance of the modular guided review comment generation method.

sjogarde2023_covid_citations.title: The effect of the rapid growth of covid-19 publications on citation indicators
sjogarde2023_covid_citations.research_idea: The paper investigates how the rapid growth of publications related to the covid-19 pandemic affects bibliometric indicators such as field-normalized citation scores and the journal impact factor. It highlights the risk of drawing misleading conclusions from citation indicators during the early stages of the pandemic.
sjogarde2023_covid_citations.research_question: How does the rapid growth of covid-19 publications influence commonly used bibliometric indicators such as field-normalized citation scores and the journal impact factor?
hypotheses: The burst of publications in the early stage of the covid-19 pandemic affects field-normalized citation scores.
The rapid growth of covid-19 publications will affect the journal impact factor.
sjogarde2023_covid_citations.data_description: The study uses a search query to identify 31,789 publications in 2020 and 74,064 in 2021 related to covid-19 from PubMed/MEDLINE. Citation data is drawn from an in-house version of the Web of Science, including the Science Citation Index Expanded, Social Sciences Citation Index, and Arts & Humanities Citation Index.

filters: restricted to Web of Science publication types 'article' and 'review'

independent_variables.name: covid_19_publication
independent_variables.definition: Whether a publication is related to covid-19 based on a search query.

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the publication was published (2020 or 2021).

independent_variables.name: journal_category
independent_variables.definition: The Web of Science journal category to which the publication belongs.

dependent_variables.name: field_normalized_citation_score
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations to the publication divided by the average number of citations in the same field, year, and publication type.

dependent_variables.name: journal_impact_factor
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations to a journal in a given year divided by the number of citable items published in the previous two years.

control_variables.name: publication_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior between articles and reviews.

control_variables.name: citation_window
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time period over which citations are counted (e.g., two-year window).

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and comparisons between groups to analyze the effects of the rapid growth of covid-19 publications on citation indicators.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation behavior and impact on bibliometric indicators between publications related to the covid-19 pandemic and those unrelated to it.

result_claims.value: Citations to covid-19 publications are significantly higher than to non-covid-19 publications.
Citations from covid-19 publications increase the impact factor of journals in which they are published.
Covid-19 publications cite recent publications to a much higher extent than other medical publications.

partial_results.note: The effect on the field-normalized citation score is particularly pronounced in fields with a high proportion of covid-19 publications.

conclusion_claims: The rapid growth of covid-19 publications affects field-normalized citation scores and the journal impact factor.

There is a considerable risk of drawing misleading conclusions from citation indicators spanning over the beginning of the covid-19 pandemic, especially when time series are used and when the biomedical literature is assessed.
limitations: The study is limited to the biomedical literature and does not consider the impact on other fields.
The analysis is based on a specific time period (2020-2021) and may not generalize to other periods.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison of citation patterns across different Web of Science journal categories
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed effects are not specific to a particular category.

robustness_checks.method: Exclusion of covid-19 publications from the analysis of highly cited publications
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the extent to which the dominance of covid-19 publications affects the overall citation landscape.

fahimifar2022_unreliable_shoulders.title: Are We Standing on Unreliable Shoulders? The Effect of Retracted Papers Citations on Previous and Subsequent Published Papers: A Study of the Web of Science Database
fahimifar2022_unreliable_shoulders.venue: International Journal of Information Science and Management
fahimifar2022_unreliable_shoulders.research_idea: This study investigates the impact of retracted papers on previous and subsequent published papers, focusing on the citation patterns and the potential influence of retracted papers on the scientific community.
fahimifar2022_unreliable_shoulders.research_question: What is the effect of retracted papers on the citations and subsequent publications in the Web of Science database?
hypotheses: There is a significant correlation between journal quartiles and the number of retracted papers.
There is a significant difference between the average number of unretracted and retracted papers citing retracted papers.
There is a significant difference between the average number of retracted and unretracted papers cited by retracted papers.
fahimifar2022_unreliable_shoulders.data_description: The dataset includes 5,693 retracted papers indexed in the Web of Science database from 1975 to 2020. The data includes total records and cited references.

filters: Excluded retraction notices to avoid duplication

independent_variables.name: retraction_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether a paper is retracted or not.

independent_variables.name: journal_quartile
independent_variables.definition: The quartile ranking of the journal in which the paper was published (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4).

independent_variables.name: citation_count
independent_variables.definition: The number of citations a paper receives.

dependent_variables.name: average_citations_to_retracted_papers
dependent_variables.formula: Average number of citations to retracted papers by other papers.

dependent_variables.name: average_citations_from_retracted_papers
dependent_variables.formula: Average number of citations from retracted papers to other papers.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in retraction rates and citation behavior.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in citation practices across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and comparative analysis of citation patterns between retracted and unretracted papers.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation network was built using the citation relationships between retracted and unretracted papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation behavior and impact of retracted papers versus unretracted papers.

partial_results.metric: Correlation between journal quartiles and retraction rates
partial_results.note: The correlation was not explicitly tested in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: Retracted papers are cited differently compared to unretracted papers, indicating a potential influence on subsequent research.
Citations to retracted papers continue despite their retraction status, suggesting a need for improved monitoring by citation databases.
limitations: The study is limited to the Web of Science database and may not represent all retracted papers globally.
The analysis is descriptive and does not establish causal relationships between retraction and citation behavior.

robustness_checks.rationale: To avoid duplication and ensure accurate citation analysis.

momeni2023_factors_oa.title: Which Factors are associated with Open Access Publishing? A Springer Nature Case Study
momeni2023_factors_oa.research_idea: The paper investigates the factors associated with Open Access (OA) publishing, particularly focusing on how financial support, country income levels, and other author characteristics influence OA publishing decisions and citation impact.
momeni2023_factors_oa.research_question: What factors are associated with Open Access publishing, and how do these factors influence the choice of publishing model and citation impact?
hypotheses: Researchers with better access to financial resources have better access to publications in terms of both reading and publishing openly.
OA publishing may perpetuate existing inequalities rather than resolve marginalization in the scholarly communication system.
momeni2023_factors_oa.data_description: The study analyzed 522,411 articles published by Springer Nature. The dataset includes information on authors' affiliations, country income levels, publishing models (gold OA, hybrid, closed access), and citation impact.

independent_variables.name: country_income_level
independent_variables.definition: The economic status of the country where the author is affiliated (e.g., low-income, upper-middle-income, high-income)

independent_variables.name: APC_waiver_eligibility
independent_variables.definition: Whether the author is eligible for an article processing charge (APC) waiver

independent_variables.name: APC_discount_eligibility
independent_variables.definition: Whether the author is eligible for an APC discount

independent_variables.name: journal_rank
independent_variables.definition: The prestige or ranking of the journal in which the article is published

independent_variables.name: author_seniority
independent_variables.definition: The career stage of the author (e.g., early-career, mid-career, senior)

independent_variables.name: experience_with_OA
independent_variables.definition: Whether the author has previously published in an OA format

dependent_variables.name: OA_publishing_choice
dependent_variables.definition: The publishing model chosen by the author (gold OA, hybrid, closed access)

dependent_variables.name: citation_impact
dependent_variables.definition: The number of citations received by the article

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: Different disciplines may have different publishing norms and citation practices

control_variables.name: language
control_variables.rationale: Language can influence the visibility and citation impact of an article

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: The type of institution (e.g., public, private) may influence access to resources and OA publishing options

statistical_method.specification: The study employed correlation and regression analyses to describe the relationship between authors' affiliations, publishing models, and citation impact. A machine learning classification method was used to explore the importance of different features in predicting the publishing model.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare how different financial and institutional factors influence OA publishing decisions and citation impact.

result_claims.value: Authors eligible for APC waivers publish more in gold-OA journals than others
Authors eligible for an APC discount have the lowest ratio of OA publications
There is a strong correlation between journal rank and the publishing model in gold-OA journals
The OA option is mostly avoided in hybrid journals
Countries' income level, seniority, and experience with OA publications are the most predictive factors for OA publishing in hybrid journals

partial_results.note: The study notes that OA articles have a citation advantage, but the extent to which this is due to OA versus other factors (e.g., quality bias) is not fully resolved.

conclusion_claims: Authors eligible for APC waivers are more likely to publish in gold-OA journals, suggesting that financial support is a key factor in OA publishing decisions.

The study highlights the potential for OA publishing to exacerbate existing inequalities in the scholarly communication system, particularly for authors from low-income countries or those without financial support.
limitations: The study is limited to articles published by Springer Nature, which may not be representative of the broader scholarly publishing landscape.
The study does not fully disentangle the effects of OA from other factors that may influence citation impact, such as article quality or journal prestige.

robustness_checks.rationale: To explore the importance of different features in predicting the publishing model and validate the results of the regression analysis.

tiyavorabun2022_badder_seeds.title: [Re] Badder Seeds: Reproducing the Evaluation of Lexical Methods for Bias Measurement
tiyavorabun2022_badder_seeds.research_idea: This paper replicates the study by Antoniak and Mimno to evaluate the reproducibility of their findings on the fragility of seed sets used in bias measurement in NLP. The core contribution is verifying the claim that seed sets themselves can be biased and induce instability in bias measurements.
tiyavorabun2022_badder_seeds.research_question: Can the findings of Antoniak and Mimno regarding the fragility of seed sets in bias measurement be reproduced, and are their conclusions valid?
hypotheses: Seed sets used for bias measurement often suffer from biases that affect their performance as a baseline for bias metrics.
Features of seed sets such as pairing order, set similarity, and frequency can cause instability in bias measurements.
tiyavorabun2022_badder_seeds.data_source: New York Times articles, WikiText-103, Goodreads book reviews, GoogleNews word2vec model
tiyavorabun2022_badder_seeds.data_description: The study uses four corpora: New York Times articles from April 15th-June 30th, 2016; WikiText-103 training set; Goodreads book reviews for the romance and history and biography genres; and the GoogleNews word2vec model. The data is preprocessed by lowercasing, removing special characters, and tokenizing with spaCy.

filters: filtered reviews with at least 20 characters

independent_variables.name: seed_set
independent_variables.definition: A set of words specifying stereotypes or dimensions of interest used for bias measurement.

independent_variables.name: pairing_order
independent_variables.definition: The order in which seed terms are paired for bias measurement.

independent_variables.name: set_similarity
independent_variables.definition: The degree of semantic similarity between seed terms in a set.

independent_variables.name: seed_frequency
independent_variables.definition: The frequency of occurrence of seed terms in the corpus.

dependent_variables.name: bias_measurement
dependent_variables.formula: Computed using bias measurement metrics such as WEAT, PCA, and custom metrics proposed by the authors.

control_variables.name: corpus
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in bias measurements are not due to differences in the underlying corpora.

control_variables.name: embedding_model
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in bias measurements are not due to differences in the word embedding models used.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and qualitative analysis to evaluate the reproducibility of the original findings.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of semantic similarity on the stability of bias measurements.

result_claims.value: Seed sets often suffer from biases that affect their performance as a baseline for bias metrics.
Bias measurements are unstable due to factors such as pairing order, set similarity, and seed frequency.

partial_results.note: Unable to reproduce vocabulary size accurately due to implementation differences.

conclusion_claims: Seed sets used for bias measurement often suffer from biases that affect their performance as a baseline for bias metrics.
Bias measurements are unstable due to factors such as pairing order, set similarity, and seed frequency, which highlights the need for better justification and analysis of seed sets in future work.
limitations: Lack of publicly available code and documentation from the original paper made the replication process challenging.
Minor discrepancies in preprocessing and vocabulary size reproduction.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not due to implementation-specific issues.
To ensure that the findings are robust across different datasets.

ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.title: Retracted papers by Iranian authors: Causes, journals, time lags, affiliations, collaborations
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.research_idea: This study investigates the causes, journals, time lags, affiliations, and collaborations of retracted papers by Iranian authors indexed in Scopus from 2001 to 2019. The findings highlight the prevalence of fake peer review and plagiarism as major causes of retractions.
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.research_question: What are the causes, journals, time lags, affiliations, and collaborations associated with retracted papers by Iranian authors?
hypotheses: The majority of retracted papers by Iranian authors are due to fake peer review and plagiarism.
Islamic Azad University is the leading institution in terms of retracted papers.
International collaborations involving Iranian authors are associated with a significant number of retractions.
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.data_source: Scopus database and Retraction Watch database
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.data_description: The study analyzed 343 retraction notices indexed in Scopus from 2001 to 2019, related to scientific articles (co-)written by at least one author affiliated with an Iranian institution. The data were merged with the Retraction Watch database to determine the reasons for retractions.

filters: articles with at least one Iranian author affiliation

independent_variables.name: retraction_reason
independent_variables.definition: The reason for the retraction, such as fake peer review, plagiarism, duplicate publication, etc.

independent_variables.name: journal
independent_variables.definition: The journal in which the retracted paper was published.

independent_variables.name: institution
independent_variables.definition: The institution affiliated with the author(s) of the retracted paper.

independent_variables.name: collaboration
independent_variables.definition: Whether the paper was the result of an international collaboration.

dependent_variables.name: retraction_count
dependent_variables.formula: The number of retracted papers associated with a given reason, journal, institution, or collaboration.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in retraction rates.

statistical_method.specification: The data were analyzed using Excel 2016 and IBM-SPSS version 24.0. Descriptive statistics were used to summarize the data, and VOSviewer software was used for visualization.

groups: papers retracted due to fake peer review
papers retracted due to duplicate publication
grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the time lags between publication and retraction for different retraction reasons.

partial_results.note: The study does not specify who initiated the retraction for 53% of the retracted papers.

conclusion_claims: The peer review system appears to be a weak point in the submission/publication process, as most retractions were due to fake peer review and plagiarism.
Improving the peer review system and increasing editorial control could likely reduce the number of retractions.
limitations: The study is limited to retracted papers indexed in Scopus and Retraction Watch, which may not capture all retracted papers.
The study does not provide a causal analysis of the factors leading to retractions.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the findings, the study used descriptive statistics and visualizations to summarize the data.

rudnicka2021_in_order_that.title: In order that – a data driven study of symptoms and causes of obsolescence
rudnicka2021_in_order_that.research_idea: This paper presents an empirical case study of grammatical obsolescence in progress, focusing on the purpose subordinator 'in order that'. It applies a data-driven approach to investigate symptoms and causes of obsolescence.
rudnicka2021_in_order_that.research_question: What are the symptoms and causes of the obsolescence of the purpose subordinator 'in order that' in English?
hypotheses: The frequency of use of 'in order that' is negatively correlated with time.
The obsolescence of 'in order that' is influenced by higher-order processes such as socio-cultural changes and the rise of the to-infinitive.
rudnicka2021_in_order_that.data_source: Corpus of Historical American English (COHA), Corpus of Contemporary American English (COCA)
rudnicka2021_in_order_that.data_description: COHA contains 400 million words of American English from 1810 to 2009. COCA contains 560 million words of American English from 1990 to 2017.

independent_variables.name: time
independent_variables.definition: The year of the text, used to measure the temporal trend in the frequency of 'in order that'.

independent_variables.name: genre
independent_variables.definition: The genre of the text (e.g., fiction, non-fiction, spoken), used to analyze distributional fragmentation.

dependent_variables.name: frequency_of_use
dependent_variables.formula: The number of occurrences of 'in order that' per million words.

control_variables.name: corpus_size
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the size of the corpus across time periods.

control_variables.name: genre_distribution
control_variables.rationale: To account for changes in the distribution of genres over time.

statistical_method.specification: Kendall’s tau test is used to assess the correlation between time and the frequency of use of 'in order that'.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze the distributional fragmentation of 'in order that' across different genres.

partial_results.note: The presence of 'in order that' in spoken language is observed, but the analysis of increasing restriction is limited due to the rarity of the construction.

conclusion_claims: The purpose subordinator 'in order that' is showing clear symptoms of obsolescence, including a strong negative correlation between time and frequency of use.
The obsolescence of 'in order that' is influenced by higher-order processes, such as socio-cultural changes and the rise of the to-infinitive.
limitations: The analysis is limited to American English and may not generalize to other varieties of English.
The rarity of 'in order that' in later decades may introduce bias in the analysis of distributional fragmentation.

robustness_checks.rationale: To mitigate the bias introduced by changes in genre distribution over time.

_source.title: "In order that" -- a data driven study of symptoms and causes of obsolescence

okamura2022_scientometric_engineering.title: Scientometric engineering: Exploring citation dynamics via arXiv eprints
okamura2022_scientometric_engineering.research_idea: The paper investigates the citation dynamics of arXiv eprints to understand how the shift from traditional journal publishing to preprint servers has affected the growth and obsolescence of scientific attention. It introduces a normalized citation index and a stochastic model to explain these dynamics.
okamura2022_scientometric_engineering.research_question: How has the shift to preprint servers like arXiv affected the citation dynamics and attention patterns in different scientific disciplines?
hypotheses: The growth and obsolescence patterns of citations vary significantly across disciplines.
The citation dynamics of arXiv eprints differ from those of traditional journal publications.
A stochastic model can capture the observed citation growth and obsolescence patterns.
okamura2022_scientometric_engineering.data_description: The dataset includes over 1.5 million eprints from arXiv, covering multiple scientific disciplines. The data includes citation information and temporal metadata.

independent_variables.name: discipline
independent_variables.definition: The scientific discipline to which the eprint belongs (e.g., physics, computer science)

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the eprint was published

independent_variables.name: citation_age
independent_variables.definition: The time elapsed since the eprint was published

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by the eprint over time

dependent_variables.name: normalized_citation_index
dependent_variables.formula: A quantitatively-and-temporally normalized index of citational attention (‘𝛾-index’)

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for discipline-specific citation practices

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and regression models to analyze citation trends and fit curves to the data.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation network is built using the citation relationships between arXiv eprints.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation dynamics across disciplines with different publication and communication practices.

partial_results.note: Results may vary depending on the specific subfield within a discipline.

conclusion_claims: The citation dynamics of arXiv eprints vary significantly across disciplines, reflecting different publication and communication practices.
The development of a quantitatively-and-temporally normalized citation index and a stochastic model provides a new framework for understanding citation dynamics in the digital age.
limitations: The study is limited to arXiv eprints and may not generalize to other preprint servers or traditional journal publications.
The citation data may not fully capture the attention dynamics of all scientific fields, especially those with limited preprint activity.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the observed patterns are consistent across different modeling approaches
To validate the robustness of the findings within and across disciplines

kerzendorf2020_distributed_peer_review.title: Distributed peer review enhanced with natural language processing and machine learning
kerzendorf2020_distributed_peer_review.research_idea: The paper introduces and evaluates a distributed peer review (DPR) system enhanced with natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning for the allocation of telescope time at the European Southern Observatory (ESO). The study demonstrates that DPR is statistically equivalent to traditional peer review while reducing reviewer load and introducing automated referee selection.
kerzendorf2020_distributed_peer_review.research_question: Does the distributed peer review system, enhanced with NLP and machine learning, produce outcomes comparable to traditional peer review, and how do factors like reviewer expertise and seniority influence review quality?
hypotheses: The distributed peer review system will produce outcomes statistically equivalent to traditional peer review.
The machine learning algorithm will effectively predict reviewer expertise.
Seniority and reviewer expertise will influence the quality of reviews.
kerzendorf2020_distributed_peer_review.data_source: European Southern Observatory (ESO) proposal data from Period 103 (2018), including volunteer submissions and reviews.
kerzendorf2020_distributed_peer_review.data_description: The dataset includes 172 volunteer proposals and their reviews, with each proposal reviewed by 8 referees. The data was collected during the ESO Period 103 call for proposals issued on 30 August 2018.

independent_variables.name: reviewer_selection_method
independent_variables.definition: The method used to select reviewers for proposals (traditional vs. machine learning-based)

independent_variables.name: reviewer_expertise
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the reviewer's expertise predicted by the machine learning algorithm

independent_variables.name: reviewer_seniority
independent_variables.definition: The career stage or experience level of the reviewer

dependent_variables.name: proposal_quartile_ranking
dependent_variables.formula: The quartile ranking of a proposal based on the average grade from reviewers

dependent_variables.name: review_helpfulness
dependent_variables.formula: A binary or ordinal measure of whether the proposer found the review helpful

control_variables.name: proposal_content_similarity
control_variables.rationale: To account for the similarity between proposals and how it might influence reviewer ratings

control_variables.name: number_of_reviews_per_proposal
control_variables.rationale: To account for the number of reviews a proposal received, which could influence the final ranking

statistical_method.specification: The study uses quartile agreement matrices (QAM) to compare the consistency of rankings between the DPR and OPC processes. Bootstrapping is used to generate sub-panels for comparison.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the outcomes of the distributed peer review system with the traditional Observing Programmes Committee (OPC) process.

result_claims.value: 40% for first quartile, 30% for second and third quartiles

partial_results.note: The experiment did not influence telescope allocation, so long-term effects are not yet known.

conclusion_claims: Distributed peer review is statistically equivalent to traditional peer review in terms of proposal ranking consistency.
Machine learning can effectively predict reviewer expertise, potentially reducing biases in the review process.
Seniority and reviewer expertise influence the quality of reviews, as judged by proposer feedback.
limitations: The experiment was voluntary and did not influence actual telescope time allocation.
The sample size was relatively small (172 proposals).
The results are specific to the astronomy community and may not generalize to other fields.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the consistency of results by comparing sub-panels of reviewers.

chaieb2022_data_obsolescence.title: Data Obsolescence Detection in the Light of Newly Acquired Valid Observations
chaieb2022_data_obsolescence.research_idea: This paper proposes a novel causation-based system for detecting and explaining obsolete information in a database when new valid observations contradict existing ones. The approach uses a Causal Bayesian Network (CBN) to detect contradictions and provide explanations in the form of AND-OR trees.
chaieb2022_data_obsolescence.research_question: How can we detect and explain obsolete information in a database when new valid observations contradict existing ones?
hypotheses: The proposed causation-based system can effectively detect contradictions between new and existing observations.
The AND-OR tree explanation method can provide reliable recommendations for updating obsolete information.
chaieb2022_data_obsolescence.data_source: Elderly database with sensor data for fall-prevention monitoring
chaieb2022_data_obsolescence.data_description: The data includes observations on elderly individuals' behaviors, such as walking ability, driving, shopping, and sensor network status. The dataset is used to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in a real-life medical application.

independent_variables.name: new_observations
independent_variables.definition: New valid observations that may contradict existing ones in the database

independent_variables.name: existing_observations
independent_variables.definition: Previously stored observations in the database

dependent_variables.name: obsolete_information
dependent_variables.formula: Identified based on contradictions between new and existing observations using a CBN model

control_variables.name: CBN_model
control_variables.rationale: The Causal Bayesian Network is used as a representation model to reason about dependencies and contradictions between observations.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a Causal Bayesian Network (CBN) to model dependencies and detect contradictions between observations.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Not applicable

partial_results.note: The optimal threshold for detecting contradictions is discussed but not fully quantified in the paper.

conclusion_claims: The proposed causation-based system effectively detects contradictions between new and existing observations.
The AND-OR tree explanation method provides reliable recommendations for updating obsolete information in a database.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed quantitative results or statistical significance tests.
The approach is demonstrated in a single real-life application (elderly fall-prevention), and its generalizability to other domains is not fully explored.

robustness_checks.method: application in a real-life medical context
robustness_checks.rationale: The approach is tested in a real-world scenario to demonstrate its practical effectiveness.

xia2019_function_point.title: Updating Weight Values for Function Point Counting
xia2019_function_point.venue: International Journal of Hybrid Intelligent Systems
xia2019_function_point.research_idea: The paper proposes a neuro-fuzzy model to update the outdated weight values used in Function Point (FP) counting to improve software effort estimation accuracy.
xia2019_function_point.research_question: How can the outdated weight values in Function Point counting be updated to improve the accuracy of software effort estimation?
hypotheses: The proposed neuro-fuzzy model will provide more accurate software effort estimation compared to traditional FP methods.
The integration of neural networks and fuzzy logic will better capture the uncertainties and imprecisions in software development.
xia2019_function_point.data_description: The dataset includes historical software project data used for training and validating the neuro-fuzzy model.

independent_variables.name: Function Point components
independent_variables.definition: Counts of Internal Logical Files (ILF), External Interface Files (EIF), External Inputs (EI), External Outputs (EO), and External Inquiries (EQ).

independent_variables.name: Complexity weights
independent_variables.definition: Weights assigned to each function component based on its complexity (low, average, high).

dependent_variables.name: Software effort
dependent_variables.formula: Estimated effort derived from the neuro-fuzzy model.

control_variables.name: Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)
control_variables.rationale: Accounts for the contribution of technical and quality requirements to the overall complexity.

statistical_method.specification: A neuro-fuzzy system that combines the learning ability of neural networks with the capability of fuzzy logic to capture human knowledge.
statistical_method.estimation: Neural network training and fuzzy logic inference

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the accuracy of software effort estimation between the traditional FP method and the proposed neuro-fuzzy model.

result_claims.significance: Empirical validation shows an average improvement of 22% in the accuracy of software effort estimations.

partial_results.note: The correlation is still unsatisfactory due to the wide variation of data points with many outliers.

conclusion_claims: The proposed neuro-fuzzy model significantly improves the accuracy of software effort estimation compared to traditional FP methods.
The integration of neural networks and fuzzy logic provides a more robust approach to handling uncertainties and imprecisions in software development.
limitations: The calibration was done using a limited dataset, which may affect the generalizability of the results.
The model's performance may vary depending on the specific context and characteristics of the software projects.

robustness_checks.method: Empirical validation using ISBSG Data Repository
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the model's effectiveness across a diverse set of software projects.

arroyomachado2020_science_wikipedia.title: Science through Wikipedia: a novel representation of open knowledge through co-citation networks
arroyomachado2020_science_wikipedia.research_idea: This study explores how Wikipedia represents scientific knowledge by analyzing co-citation networks of scientific articles cited in Wikipedia. It provides a novel approach to understanding the consumption and representation of scientific literature through open knowledge platforms.
arroyomachado2020_science_wikipedia.research_question: How does Wikipedia represent scientific knowledge, and what patterns emerge in the citation of scientific literature through this open encyclopaedia?
hypotheses: Wikipedia citations will show a significant presence of certain scientific fields such as Medicine and Biochemistry, Genetics and Molecular Biology.
High-impact factor journals are more likely to be cited in Wikipedia.
Co-citation networks will reveal distinct groupings of journals and fields that reflect the structure of scientific knowledge.
arroyomachado2020_science_wikipedia.data_source: Altmetric.com, Elsevier's CiteScore Metrics, Scopus ASJC
arroyomachado2020_science_wikipedia.data_description: The study analyzed 1,433,457 references from Wikipedia to scientific articles, which were linked to 14,149 journals indexed in Scopus. After preprocessing, the sample included 847,512 references to 598,746 scientific articles from 193,802 Wikipedia articles.

independent_variables.name: scientific_field
independent_variables.definition: The field of the scientific article as classified by Scopus ASJC

independent_variables.name: journal_impact_factor
independent_variables.definition: The impact factor of the journal in which the cited article was published

independent_variables.name: open_access_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the cited article is published in an open access journal

dependent_variables.name: citation_frequency
dependent_variables.formula: Number of times a scientific article is cited in Wikipedia

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for the age of the article and its potential obsolescence

control_variables.name: journal_citation_count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the overall citation impact of the journal

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and co-citation network analysis to explore the structure and patterns of citations in Wikipedia.

network_construction.construction_method: Co-citation networks were constructed by analyzing the co-occurrence of journals cited in the same Wikipedia article. Pathfinder networks (PFNET) were used to simplify and highlight the most relevant connections.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze the structure of scientific knowledge at different levels of aggregation and to identify patterns of citation and interrelation between journals and fields.

partial_results.note: Only 5.49% of Wikipedia citations correspond to the Humanities, which is not the focus of this study.

conclusion_claims: Wikipedia provides a significant representation of scientific knowledge, with a strong presence of Medicine and Biochemistry, Genetics and Molecular Biology.
High-impact factor journals are more likely to be cited in Wikipedia, suggesting that visibility and impact are key factors in citation practices.
limitations: The study focuses on English Wikipedia, which may not represent the global perspective of scientific knowledge.
The use of Altmetric.com data may not capture all citations made in Wikipedia.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the quality and relevance of the dataset by removing duplicates and incomplete references.

zhao2023_effect_of_gender_diversity.title: The Effect of Gender Diversity on Scientific Team Impact: A Team Roles Perspective
zhao2023_effect_of_gender_diversity.research_idea: This paper investigates how gender diversity in different team roles (leadership and support) affects the impact of scientific teams, and whether team size moderates this relationship. It contributes by analyzing role-specific gender diversity and its nuanced effects on team performance.
zhao2023_effect_of_gender_diversity.research_question: How does gender diversity within different team roles affect the team’s impact, and can team size moderate this relationship?
hypotheses: The relationship between gender diversity and team impact follows an inverted U-shape for both leadership and support groups.
The effect of leadership-group gender diversity is significantly negative for small teams but becomes positive and statistically insignificant in large teams.
zhao2023_effect_of_gender_diversity.data_description: More than 130,000 papers from PLOS journals, primarily in biomedical-related disciplines.

independent_variables.name: gender_diversity_leadership
independent_variables.definition: Gender diversity within the leadership roles in a scientific team.

independent_variables.name: gender_diversity_support
independent_variables.definition: Gender diversity within the support roles in a scientific team.

independent_variables.name: team_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of authors in a scientific team.

dependent_variables.name: team_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Five-year citation counts of a paper.

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across disciplines.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To control for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: team_experience
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of team members' experience on team impact.

statistical_method.specification: Multivariable regression to examine the association between gender diversity in leadership and support roles and team impact. Threshold regression model to investigate how team size moderates this relationship.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze the distinct effects of gender diversity in leadership and support roles on team impact.

key_quantities: coefficient of gender diversity in leadership roles
coefficient of gender diversity in support roles

result_claims.value: negative for small teams, positive for large teams

partial_results.note: The effect of leadership-group gender diversity becomes positive and statistically insignificant in large teams.

conclusion_claims: The relationship between gender diversity and team impact follows an inverted U-shape for both leadership and support groups.
Teams with an all-female leadership group and an all-male support group achieve higher impact than other team types.
limitations: The study focuses primarily on biomedical-related disciplines, which may limit generalizability to other fields.
The use of author contribution statements may not fully capture the complexity of role differentiation in all teams.

robustness_checks.rationale: To investigate how team size moderates the relationship between gender diversity and team impact.

Ribeiro2026_GenderProductivityPrestige.title: Gender shapes the relationship between productivity and journal prestige in science
Ribeiro2026_GenderProductivityPrestige.research_idea: This study investigates how gender influences the relationship between research productivity and journal prestige in science, revealing distinct gendered strategies in the quantity-versus-quality trade-off.
Ribeiro2026_GenderProductivityPrestige.research_question: How does gender shape the relationship between research productivity and journal prestige in science?
hypotheses: Male researchers are more likely to adopt productivity-driven strategies compared to female researchers.
Female researchers tend to achieve comparable or higher average journal prestige despite lower productivity rates.
Productivity and career age are negatively associated with average journal prestige for both genders, with stronger effects observed among female researchers.
Ribeiro2026_GenderProductivityPrestige.data_description: Publication records of 6,028 Brazilian researchers (1,748 females) holding a nationally prestigious fellowship awarded by the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) from the 1970s onward.
available_fields: Average journal prestige (Journal Impact Factor and SCImago Journal Rank)

filters: Researchers holding a nationally prestigious fellowship (Research Productivity Fellowship)
Active researchers required to update their publications and research achievements

independent_variables.name: Gender
independent_variables.definition: Binary variable indicating whether the researcher is male or female.

independent_variables.name: Productivity
independent_variables.definition: Annual publication count of the researcher.

independent_variables.name: Career age
independent_variables.definition: The number of years since the researcher began their academic career.

independent_variables.name: Discipline
independent_variables.definition: The academic discipline in which the researcher is active.

dependent_variables.name: Average journal prestige
dependent_variables.formula: Computed as the average of the Journal Impact Factor (JIF) and SCImago Journal Rank (SJR) of the journals in which the researcher publishes.

control_variables.name: Discipline-specific publication practices
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in publication norms across disciplines.

control_variables.name: Inflation-like effects
control_variables.rationale: To adjust for changes in journal prestige metrics over time.

control_variables.name: Size-dependent biases
control_variables.rationale: To correct for biases in average journal prestige evaluation based on the number of publications.

statistical_method.specification: A Bayesian hierarchical model is applied to project individual career years onto a standardized productivity-prestige plane and analyze gendered patterns.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the distinct strategies and outcomes of male and female researchers in terms of productivity and journal prestige.

key_quantities: Gender effect on productivity and journal prestige
Interaction between productivity and career age on journal prestige

result_claims.value: Comparable or higher for female researchers, particularly in later career stages and among outlier individuals

result_claims.metric: Temporal persistence in productivity and impact
Negative association between productivity and average journal prestige
result_claims.value: Observed for both genders, with slightly stronger effects among female researchers

partial_results.note: Patterns vary across disciplines, highlighting the complexity and heterogeneity of academic careers.

conclusion_claims: Male researchers tend to adopt productivity-driven strategies, while female researchers more often pursue impact-oriented trajectories.
Gender-sensitive approaches in research evaluation are essential to foster a more equitable, fair, and efficient academic environment.
limitations: The study focuses on a specific national context (Brazil), which may limit generalizability to other academic systems.
The dataset is based on self-reported information from the Lattes platform, which may introduce potential biases.

robustness_checks.rationale: Results are consistent across both Journal Impact Factor (JIF) and SCImago Journal Rank (SJR) metrics.
A recently introduced standardization approach is applied to account for outlier individuals, discipline-specific practices, and biases.

banerjee2025_citation_diversity.title: Exploring Citation Diversity in Scholarly Literature: An Entropy-Based Approach
banerjee2025_citation_diversity.research_idea: This study introduces a novel entropy-based measure of citation diversity to analyze patterns of citations across countries, academic disciplines, and award-winning scientists, revealing insights beyond traditional citation counts.
banerjee2025_citation_diversity.research_question: How does citation diversity vary across countries, academic disciplines, and demographic factors such as gender, and what does this reveal about the structure of scholarly influence?
hypotheses: Citation diversity varies significantly across countries with different economic sizes.
Citation diversity differs among scientific disciplines.
Citation diversity varies between male and female scientists.
banerjee2025_citation_diversity.data_description: The study uses citation data from the Webometrics ranking of top higher education institutions and Scopus for publication and citation data of award-winning scientists. The data includes top institutions across countries and citation counts for 21 scientists per discipline, including Nobel laureates, Abel prize winners, and Turing award winners.

independent_variables.name: country_economic_size
independent_variables.definition: Categorization of countries based on their economic size (small vs. large)

independent_variables.name: discipline
independent_variables.definition: Scientific discipline (e.g., Physics, Chemistry, etc.)

independent_variables.name: gender
independent_variables.definition: Gender of the scientist (male vs. female)

independent_variables.name: number_of_top_institutions
independent_variables.definition: Number of top institutions included in the analysis

dependent_variables.name: citation_diversity
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated using the two-parameter generalization of the Renyi entropy (logarithmic norm entropy) to measure the evenness of citation distribution

control_variables.name: citation_count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the total number of citations, which may influence the diversity measure

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis and entropy-based measures to quantify citation diversity across different categories.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation diversity patterns between countries with different economic sizes.

result_claims.value: Varies significantly across countries and disciplines
Countries with small and large economies cluster similarly
Varies between male and female scientists

partial_results.note: The composition of countries within each group changes as the number of top institutions considered in the analysis varies.

conclusion_claims: The entropy-based citation diversity measure provides deeper insights into the unevenness of citation distributions compared to traditional citation counts.
Citation diversity varies significantly across countries, disciplines, and genders, highlighting the need for a more equitable view of academic achievements.
limitations: The study is limited to top-ranked institutions and award-winning scientists, which may not represent the broader academic population.
The analysis is based on a single year of data, which may not capture long-term trends in citation diversity.

robustness_checks.method: Varying the number of top institutions included in the analysis
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the sensitivity of the citation diversity measure to the sample size.

silvello2017_theory_practice_data_citati.title: Theory and Practice of Data Citation
silvello2017_theory_practice_data_citati.venue: Journal of the Association for Information Science and Technology (JASIST)
silvello2017_theory_practice_data_citati.research_idea: The paper explores the theoretical and practical aspects of data citation, emphasizing the need for a consistent and established methodology to cite datasets as first-class research objects.
silvello2017_theory_practice_data_citati.research_question: What is the current state of data citation, and what are the theoretical and practical challenges and solutions for treating data as first-class research objects?

statistical_method.specification: The paper provides a descriptive analysis of the current state of data citation and outlines theoretical and practical challenges.

conclusion_claims: Data citation is essential for recognizing datasets as first-class research objects and ensuring proper credit attribution and scientific reproducibility.
A consistent and persistent mechanism for data citation is lacking, and current citation systems do not adequately account for datasets.
limitations: The paper is a review and does not present original empirical data.
It does not propose a specific solution or framework for data citation.

nakajima2023_quantifying_gender_imbalanc.title: Quantifying gender imbalance in East Asian academia: Research career and citation practice
nakajima2023_quantifying_gender_imbalanc.research_idea: This study quantifies gender imbalance in East Asian academia, focusing on China, Japan, and South Korea, by analyzing the number of researchers, their research careers, and citation practices. It highlights the larger gender imbalance in these countries compared to other research-active countries.
nakajima2023_quantifying_gender_imbalanc.research_question: What is the extent of gender imbalance in East Asian academia in terms of the number of researchers, their research careers, and citation practices, and how does it compare to other countries?
hypotheses: China, Japan, and South Korea are exposed to larger gender imbalance than other research-active countries.
Japanese academia is exposed to larger gender imbalance than Chinese and South Korean academia.
nakajima2023_quantifying_gender_imbalanc.data_description: Publication data between 1950 and 2020, including 104,169,109 papers, with information on publication date, research fields, author IDs, citations, and author positions.

filters: researchers from China, Japan, South Korea, and other countries

independent_variables.name: country
independent_variables.definition: The country where the researcher is affiliated (China, Japan, South Korea, or other countries).

independent_variables.name: gender
independent_variables.definition: The gender of the researcher, inferred from the author's name.

dependent_variables.name: number_of_researchers
dependent_variables.formula: Count of researchers by gender and country.

dependent_variables.name: research_career_length
dependent_variables.formula: Duration of the researcher's career based on the time between their first and last publication.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
