dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by the researcher's publications.

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices and research career dynamics across disciplines.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in gender imbalance and research practices.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive analysis of gender imbalance in terms of the number of researchers, research career length, and citation counts.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare gender imbalance in East Asian countries with other research-active countries.

result_claims.value: Higher gender imbalance in China, Japan, and South Korea compared to other countries.
Higher gender imbalance in Japan compared to China and South Korea.
Higher gender imbalance in China, Japan, and South Korea compared to other countries, with Japan having the largest imbalance.

partial_results.note: The analysis is limited by the difficulty of inferring gender from Asian names.

conclusion_claims: China, Japan, and South Korea exhibit larger gender imbalance in academia compared to other research-active countries in terms of the number of researchers and citation practices.
Japan has the largest gender imbalance among the three East Asian countries in terms of research career and citation practice.
limitations: The difficulty of inferring gender from Asian names may affect the accuracy of the analysis.
The study focuses only on publication data and does not consider other aspects of gender imbalance in academia.

robustness_checks.method: Exclusion of researchers from small countries like Singapore
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the reliability of the analysis by focusing on countries with a sufficient number of researchers.

gong2023_citation_advantage.title: The citation advantage of foreign language references for Chinese social science papers
gong2023_citation_advantage.research_idea: This study investigates the citation advantage of incorporating foreign language references in Chinese social science papers, exploring how the language diversity of cited references affects the citation impact of the citing paper.
gong2023_citation_advantage.research_question: Does the inclusion of foreign language references in Chinese social science papers lead to a citation advantage, and how does this advantage evolve over time?
hypotheses: Papers with foreign language references receive more citations than those without.
The citation advantage of papers with foreign language references decreases as their proportion increases.
The citation advantage of papers with foreign language references remains after controlling for characteristics of citing papers.
gong2023_citation_advantage.data_description: 950,302 papers published between 1998 and 2013 with 8,151,327 cited references collected from the CSSCI database.

independent_variables.name: foreign_language_references
independent_variables.definition: Whether the paper includes foreign language references.

independent_variables.name: proportion_foreign_language_references
independent_variables.definition: The proportion of foreign language references among all cited references in a paper.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by the paper.

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: Different disciplines may have different citation practices.

control_variables.name: journal_prestige
control_variables.rationale: Prestigious journals tend to receive more citations.

control_variables.name: institution_prestige
control_variables.rationale: Prestigious institutions may produce higher-impact research.

control_variables.name: scientific_collaboration
control_variables.rationale: Collaborative papers may have higher citation rates.

statistical_method.specification: Regression analysis to examine the relationship between the language diversity of cited references and the number of citations received by the citing paper.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation impact of papers based on the inclusion of foreign language references.

result_claims.value: Papers with foreign language references receive significantly more citations than those without
The citation advantage decreased from 1998 to 2008 but stabilized after 2008

partial_results.note: The decline of the citation advantage from 1998 to 2008 is largely an artifact of the increased number of papers citing foreign language material.

conclusion_claims: Incorporating foreign language literature continues to increase scientific impact in Chinese social sciences, even as the scientific community becomes increasingly international.

The citation advantage of foreign language references remains significant after controlling for paper characteristics such as discipline, journal prestige, institution prestige, and scientific collaboration.
limitations: The study is limited to Chinese social science papers indexed in CSSCI and does not include international publications.
The analysis does not account for the quality of the cited foreign language references.

robustness_checks.method: Controlling for discipline, journal prestige, institution prestige, and scientific collaboration
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed citation advantage is not due to other confounding factors.

doukhan2024_voice_passing.title: Voice Passing: a Non-Binary Voice Gender Prediction System for evaluating Transgender voice transition
doukhan2024_voice_passing.research_idea: This paper presents a non-binary voice gender prediction system that evaluates transgender voice transition using a continuous Voice Femininity Percentage (VFP) index, calibrated to human perception in the French language and culture.
doukhan2024_voice_passing.research_question: How can a non-binary voice gender prediction system be developed and evaluated to better reflect human perception of gender in transgender voices?
hypotheses: Machine learning models can be trained to predict a continuous VFP index that better reflects human perception of voice gender than traditional binary classification.
The accuracy of VFP estimation is influenced by factors such as speaking style, age, and the conception of gender as binary or non-binary.
doukhan2024_voice_passing.data_source: A corpus of 41 French cis- and transgender speakers, as well as external gender-balanced data for training models.
doukhan2024_voice_passing.data_description: The corpus includes 8 cisgender males, 12 cisgender females, and 21 transgender females, with ages ranging from 20 to 69 years (mean: 39). Recordings were made of participants reading a French text, with an average duration of 39 seconds.

independent_variables.name: Voice Femininity Percentage (VFP)
independent_variables.definition: A continuous index derived from perceptual evaluations of voice gender, calculated as the number of 'Female' answers plus half of the 'IDK' answers divided by the total number of answers.

independent_variables.name: Fundamental Frequency (F0)
independent_variables.definition: The fundamental frequency of the voice, expressed in semitones relative to 1 Hz.

independent_variables.name: Vocal Tract Length (VTL)
independent_variables.definition: An estimate of the vocal tract length based on the first four formants measured on the vocalic part of the recordings.

independent_variables.name: Speaking Style
independent_variables.definition: The style in which the speaker reads the text, which may influence the perception of voice gender.

independent_variables.name: Age
independent_variables.definition: The age of the speaker, which was found to affect the accuracy of the models.

dependent_variables.name: Perceived Gender
dependent_variables.formula: The gender perceived by listeners, categorized as 'Female', 'Male', or 'IDK' (I don't know).

dependent_variables.name: Reaction Time (RT)
dependent_variables.formula: The time taken by participants to respond to the gender perception question, measured in seconds.

control_variables.name: Language
control_variables.rationale: The study focuses on the French language and culture, so language is controlled to ensure the results are relevant to this context.

control_variables.name: Cultural Context
control_variables.rationale: The study is conducted within the French cultural context, which is controlled to ensure the results reflect gender perception in this specific setting.

statistical_method.specification: Wilcoxon rank sum test was used to compare the mean gender judgments between female and male listeners.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the perception of voice gender across different speaker categories and to evaluate the effectiveness of the VFP index in capturing gender perception for transgender voices.

result_claims.metric: Proportion of 'Female' answers for Cisgender Females (CF)
Proportion of 'Male' answers for Cisgender Males (CM)
Proportion of 'IDK' answers for Transgender Females (TF)
Mean Reaction Time (RT) for Transgender Females (TF)

result_claims.value: Higher than F0 or VTL-based models

result_claims.metric: Effect of age on model accuracy

partial_results.metric: Effect of speaking style on VFP estimation
partial_results.note: The impact of speaking style on VFP estimation is mentioned but not quantified in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: The VFP index provides a continuous measure of voice femininity that better reflects human perception of gender than traditional binary classification.
The accuracy of VFP estimation is influenced by factors such as speaking style, age, and the conception of gender as binary or non-binary.
limitations: The study is limited to the French language and culture, so the results may not generalize to other contexts.
The sample size is relatively small, with only 41 speakers, which may limit the statistical power of the analysis.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the reliability of the VFP index by comparing it to the judgments of 57 French-speaking participants.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with F0 and VTL-based models
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the VFP index by comparing it to traditional acoustic features used in gender prediction.

abramo2015_research_performance.title: Should the research performance of scientists be distinguished by gender?
abramo2015_research_performance.research_idea: The paper investigates whether distinguishing research performance by gender leads to significantly different ranking outcomes compared to evaluations without gender distinction, particularly in the context of potential structural penalties for female researchers.
abramo2015_research_performance.research_question: Does separating the measurement of research performance by gender produce notably different results compared to measurement without such distinction?
hypotheses: If there is a structural penalty for female researchers, ranking without gender distinction would distort judgments in favor of men.
Ranking lists with gender distinction would show different relative positions of female researchers compared to evaluations without gender distinction.
abramo2015_research_performance.data_source: Italian Ministry of Education, Universities and Research (MIUR) data on academic staff and research performance in hard sciences.
abramo2015_research_performance.data_description: The dataset includes Italian university professors in hard sciences, with gender information and research performance metrics for the period 2006-2010. The data is structured by Scientific Disciplinary Sectors (SDSs) and grouped into University Disciplinary Areas (UDAs).

filters: classified in a single research field

independent_variables.name: gender
independent_variables.definition: Binary variable indicating whether the researcher is male or female.

dependent_variables.name: research_performance
dependent_variables.formula: Measured using bibliometric indicators such as publication count and citation impact.

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in publication intensity and citation practices across fields.

statistical_method.specification: Comparative analysis of distributions and ranking lists with and without gender distinction.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the distributions of research performance and ranking outcomes between genders.

result_claims.value: Female researchers show lower productivity compared to male researchers.
Ranking lists with gender distinction show different relative positions for female researchers compared to evaluations without gender distinction.

partial_results.note: The extent of differences may vary across research fields and career stages, which is not fully explored in this study.

conclusion_claims: Distinguishing research performance by gender can lead to different ranking outcomes compared to evaluations without gender distinction.
In contexts where gender discrimination or family roles affect research productivity, evaluations without gender distinction may penalize female researchers.
limitations: The study is limited to the Italian academic system and hard sciences, which may not generalize to other contexts.
The analysis does not account for personal and environmental factors that may influence research productivity.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing ranking lists at the aggregated level of the university
robustness_checks.rationale: To verify the consistency of results across different levels of analysis.

schueler2023_platform_research.title: Objectives of platform research: A co-citation and systematic literature review analysis
schueler2023_platform_research.research_idea: The paper aims to summarize the key findings of scientific platform research by applying a mixed methods design combining co-citation bibliometric analysis with a systematic qualitative content analysis. It identifies research fields and objectives in platform research to enhance understanding and reduce conceptual ambiguity.
schueler2023_platform_research.research_question: What are the key research fields and objectives in platform research, and how have they evolved over time?
schueler2023_platform_research.data_description: 10,357 research papers related to digital platforms from the Web of Science database. A subset of 107 papers was analyzed in the platform ecosystem research field.

statistical_method.specification: Mixed methods design combining co-citation bibliometric analysis with qualitative content analysis.

network_construction.construction_method: Co-citation analysis was used to identify essential papers and cluster them into research fields.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To identify and analyze the different research fields and their evolution, particularly focusing on the platform ecosystem field.

conclusion_claims: The study identifies 14 platform-related research fields and three primary research objectives in platform ecosystems.
The mixed methods approach provides a holistic knowledge structure of platform research, helping to reduce conceptual ambiguity and offering an overall picture of platform research.
limitations: The study does not explicitly mention the time period of the analyzed papers.
The focus on Web of Science may exclude relevant research from other databases.

zhao2023_academic_industry_knowledge_pro.title: Quantifying the Knowledge Proximity Between Academic and Industry Research: An Entity and Semantic Perspective
zhao2023_academic_industry_knowledge_pro.research_idea: This study proposes a fine-grained method to quantify knowledge proximity between academia and industry using knowledge entities and semantic space, addressing the limitations of traditional macro-level indicators.
zhao2023_academic_industry_knowledge_pro.research_question: How to construct a fine-grained measurement indicator to characterize knowledge proximity, and how does it change over time and with technological paradigm shifts?
hypotheses: Knowledge proximity between academia and industry increases over time, especially following technological change.
Academia's knowledge dominance weakens during technological paradigm shifts.
There is a dynamic relationship between knowledge flow and knowledge proximity, varying across stages of technological development.
zhao2023_academic_industry_knowledge_pro.data_source: Not explicitly mentioned, but likely includes academic and industry publications in the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) from 2000–2022.
zhao2023_academic_industry_knowledge_pro.data_description: The study uses academic and industry publications in the NLP field from 2000 to 2022. The dataset is not explicitly described in terms of size or source, but it is implied to include textual data from both sectors.

independent_variables.name: Knowledge entities
independent_variables.definition: Fine-grained knowledge units extracted from academic and industry publications using pre-trained models.

independent_variables.name: Semantic similarity
independent_variables.definition: Cross-institutional textual similarity measured using unsupervised contrastive learning.

independent_variables.name: Technological change
independent_variables.definition: Indicators of shifts in the technological paradigm in the NLP field.

dependent_variables.name: Knowledge proximity
dependent_variables.formula: Measured via cosine similarity of knowledge entity sequences and semantic space convergence.

control_variables.name: Institutional logic divergence
control_variables.rationale: To account for inherent differences in the institutional logics of academia and industry.

control_variables.name: Collaboration frequency
control_variables.rationale: To control for the potential influence of collaboration frequency on knowledge proximity.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis and network analysis to examine knowledge proximity and its evolution over time.

network_construction.construction_method: Knowledge entities are extracted from academic and industry publications, and semantic similarity is computed using unsupervised contrastive learning.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the knowledge proximity between academic and industry research outputs.

key_quantities: Semantic similarity between academic and industry texts

result_claims.value: Rises over time, particularly following technological change

partial_results.note: The relationship between knowledge flow and proximity is described as dynamic and potentially varying across stages of technological development, but specific quantitative results are not provided.

conclusion_claims: Knowledge proximity between academia and industry increases over time, especially following technological change.
The transformation of academia-industry relations from institutional logic divergence to knowledge production synergy is supported by the observed patterns of knowledge proximity.
limitations: The study does not explicitly describe the dataset size or source, which limits reproducibility.
The dynamic relationship between knowledge flow and proximity is described qualitatively, with limited quantitative analysis.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the reliability of the semantic space analysis.

robustness_checks.method: Network analysis of knowledge entity overlaps
robustness_checks.rationale: To confirm the stability of the knowledge proximity measurements.

bezerra2025_leveraging_gans.title: Leveraging GANs for citation intent classification and its impact on citation network analysis
bezerra2025_leveraging_gans.research_idea: This paper proposes a GAN-based method for citation intent classification and investigates how filtering citations by intent affects centrality metrics in citation networks.
bezerra2025_leveraging_gans.research_question: How does leveraging GANs for citation intent classification impact the performance of intent classification and the centrality of papers in citation networks?
hypotheses: The proposed GAN-based method will achieve competitive classification performance with fewer parameters compared to state-of-the-art models.
Filtering citations by intent will significantly affect the centrality of papers in citation networks.
bezerra2025_leveraging_gans.data_description: The unArXiv dataset is used for constructing and analyzing citation networks. It contains a large collection of preprints from arXiv, including citation relationships and metadata.

independent_variables.name: citation_intent
independent_variables.definition: The purpose or function of a citation, such as background, method, or result.

independent_variables.name: network_centrality
independent_variables.definition: A measure of a paper's importance in the citation network, such as degree, PageRank, closeness, or betweenness centrality.

dependent_variables.name: classification_performance
dependent_variables.formula: Measured using F1 score on benchmark datasets like SciCite and ACL.

dependent_variables.name: ranking_sensitivity
dependent_variables.formula: Measured by the change in centrality rankings when specific citation intents are removed.

control_variables.name: citation_count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the overall number of citations a paper receives, which may influence centrality metrics.

control_variables.name: paper_publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To control for the age of the paper, which may affect its centrality in the network.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis to evaluate the performance of the GAN-based model and the impact of citation intent filtering on centrality metrics.

network_construction.construction_method: The network is constructed from the unArXiv dataset, where nodes represent papers and edges represent citation relationships.

groups: papers with specific citation intents removed
grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare how the removal of specific citation intents affects the centrality of papers in the network.

result_claims.value: Most sensitive to citation intent filtering

partial_results.note: Lower performance on the 3C dataset compared to other benchmarks.

conclusion_claims: The proposed GAN-based method achieves competitive performance in citation intent classification with fewer parameters.
Filtering citations by intent significantly affects the centrality of papers in citation networks, with betweenness centrality being the most sensitive.
limitations: Performance on the 3C dataset was lower, indicating potential challenges in more complex classification tasks.
The study focuses on a specific dataset (unArXiv), which may limit generalizability to other citation networks.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed GAN-based method.
To ensure the robustness of the findings across multiple network metrics.

li2023_two_stage.title: Two-stage dynamic creative optimization under sparse ambiguous samples for e-commerce advertising
li2023_two_stage.research_idea: The paper proposes a two-stage dynamic creative optimization framework for e-commerce advertising to address the challenges of sparse and ambiguous samples. The first stage uses an autoco-based model to simulate creative element interactions, while the second stage employs a bandit model for real-time ad delivery.
li2023_two_stage.research_question: How can dynamic creative optimization be effectively implemented in e-commerce advertising when dealing with sparse and ambiguous samples?
hypotheses: A two-stage framework combining autoco and rerank models can improve the accuracy of creative ranking.
The proposed distillation method can enhance the model's ability to handle ambiguous samples.
li2023_two_stage.data_description: The data includes historical ad creative materials and real-time feedback from e-commerce advertising. The data is sparse and contains ambiguous samples due to limited exposure and varying user behavior.

independent_variables.name: creative_elements
independent_variables.definition: The combination of elements such as template, text color, picture size, and font used in ad creatives.

independent_variables.name: autoco_rank
independent_variables.definition: The ranking score generated by the autoco model for each ad creative.

independent_variables.name: rerank_score
independent_variables.definition: The ranking score generated by the transformer-based rerank model for each ad creative.

dependent_variables.name: click_through_rate
dependent_variables.formula: Number of clicks divided by the number of impressions for each ad creative.

control_variables.name: ad_delivery_time
control_variables.rationale: To account for variations in user behavior and ad performance over different time periods.

control_variables.name: product_category
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in user preferences across product categories.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a combination of autoco and rerank models to simulate creative element interactions and generate ranking scores. A bandit model is used for real-time ad delivery.
statistical_method.estimation: The models are trained using historical data and validated through online A/B testing.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of the proposed two-stage framework with existing methods in terms of click-through rate.

partial_results.note: The ranking accuracy of the autoco model is limited by the ambiguity of the samples.

conclusion_claims: The proposed two-stage dynamic creative optimization framework outperforms existing methods in terms of click-through rate.
The use of a transformer-based rerank model and distillation method improves the model's ability to handle ambiguous samples.
limitations: The model's performance is dependent on the quality and quantity of historical data.
The framework may not generalize well to other domains outside of e-commerce advertising.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in a real-world e-commerce advertising scenario.

baccini2025_similarity_matrix.title: Similarity matrix average for aggregating multiplex networks
baccini2025_similarity_matrix.research_idea: The paper introduces a methodology based on averaging similarity matrices to integrate the layers of a multiplex network into a single monoplex network. This approach is applied to a multiplex similarity network of statistical journals.
baccini2025_similarity_matrix.research_question: How can the layers of a multiplex network be aggregated into a single monoplex network using similarity matrix averaging?
hypotheses: The proposed similarity matrix averaging approach provides a valid representation of all layers in a multiplex network.
The Fréchet mean criterion with different metrics (Frobenius, Riemannian, Wasserstein) can be used to define appropriate averages for positive definite matrices in multiplex networks.
baccini2025_similarity_matrix.data_source: Multiplex similarity network of statistical journals
baccini2025_similarity_matrix.data_description: The data consists of a multiplex network of statistical journals with three layers representing similarity based on co-citations, common authors, and common editors.

independent_variables.name: layer_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between journals based on co-citations, common authors, and common editors.

dependent_variables.name: aggregated_similarity
dependent_variables.formula: Average of similarity matrices using the Fréchet mean criterion with different metrics.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses the Fréchet mean criterion with three different metrics (Frobenius, Riemannian, Wasserstein) to compute the average of similarity matrices.

network_construction.construction_method: Similarity matrices are constructed based on co-citations, common authors, and common editors. These matrices are then aggregated using the Fréchet mean criterion.

partial_results.note: The specific values of similarity for each layer are not provided in the abstract.

conclusion_claims: The proposed methodology based on similarity matrix averaging provides a valid way to aggregate the layers of a multiplex network into a single monoplex network.
The Fréchet mean criterion with different metrics can be used to define appropriate averages for positive definite matrices in multiplex networks.
limitations: The paper does not provide specific numerical results or empirical validation of the proposed methodology.
The application to the multiplex network of statistical journals is not detailed in the abstract.

diBona2023_decentralization.title: The Concept of Decentralization Through Time and Disciplines: A Quantitative Exploration
diBona2023_decentralization.research_idea: The paper presents a general pipeline to investigate the scholarly history of the term (de)centralization, analyzing 425,144 academic publications. It reveals the independent emergence of the concept in different fields and the increasing influence of blockchain in recent years.
diBona2023_decentralization.research_question: Have different disciplines independently developed the concept of (de)centralization, and how have they influenced each other over time?
hypotheses: Different academic fields have independently contributed to the concept of (de)centralization.
Blockchain has become the most influential field regarding (de)centralization in the past 10 years.
diBona2023_decentralization.data_description: The dataset includes 425,144 academic publications with the root string 'centrali' in their title or abstract, spanning multiple disciplines and languages. The data includes titles, abstracts, authors, citations, and fields of study.

filters: Papers containing the root string 'centrali' in title or abstract
Papers with at least one citation or reference

independent_variables.name: field_of_study
independent_variables.definition: The academic field to which the paper is classified (e.g., Economics, Political Science, Computer Science).

independent_variables.name: year_of_publication
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the paper was published, used to analyze temporal trends in the use of (de)centralization.

dependent_variables.name: mention_of_decentralization
dependent_variables.definition: Whether the paper mentions (de)centralization in its title or abstract.

control_variables.name: number_of_citations
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of citation patterns on the spread of the concept across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a multilayer hierarchical stochastic block model (hSBM) to cluster papers based on semantic and citation information. It also performs a time-based analysis of knowledge flows between clusters.
statistical_method.estimation: Monte Carlo partitioning with consensus clustering

network_construction.construction_method: A bipartite network is constructed between documents and words in their titles, and a document layer is built using citations between papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the influence and productivity of different fields in the evolution of the concept of (de)centralization.

key_quantities: Temporal trends in the use of (de)centralization

result_claims.value: Most influential field in the past 10 years

partial_results.note: Not explicitly quantified in the paper, but inferred from clustering results.

conclusion_claims: The concept of (de)centralization has independently emerged in different academic fields with minimal cross-disciplinary influence.
Blockchain has become the most influential and productive field regarding (de)centralization in the past 10 years.
limitations: The dataset includes some unrelated articles due to the use of the root string 'centrali'.
The clustering method may not capture all nuances of the concept's evolution.

robustness_checks.method: Multiple iterations of the hSBM algorithm
robustness_checks.rationale: To capture the inherent variability and uncertainty in the clustering process and ensure the robustness of the results.

asatani2025_influential_scientists.title: Influential scientists shape knowledge flows between science and IGO policy
asatani2025_influential_scientists.research_idea: The paper investigates how scientific research influences intergovernmental organization (IGO) policy by identifying a small group of policy-influential scientists (PI-Sci) who dominate the knowledge flow from science to policy. It reveals how network structure and elite brokerage shape the translation of research into global policy.
asatani2025_influential_scientists.research_question: What are the mechanisms through which scientific research is translated into IGO policy, and who are the key individuals and networks that dominate this process?
hypotheses: A small group of scientists (PI-Sci) disproportionately influences IGO policy through their co-authorship networks and prior policy citations.
The concentration of influence varies by scientific field, with more established fields showing tighter clustering of influence.
Policy citations are influenced by factors such as journal selection, citation of policy-relevant research, and authors' prior policy citations.
asatani2025_influential_scientists.data_source: Overton, Scopus, and IGO policy documents
asatani2025_influential_scientists.data_description: The study analyzes 230,737 scientific papers cited in IGO policy documents from 2015 to 2023. These papers are linked to their authors and co-authorship networks using Scopus author identifiers and Overton records.

filters: Papers cited in IGO policy documents

independent_variables.name: journal_of_publication
independent_variables.definition: The journal in which the paper was published, used as a proxy for academic impact and policy relevance.

independent_variables.name: policy_cited_references
independent_variables.definition: The number of references in a paper that have been previously cited in IGO policy documents.

independent_variables.name: authors_past_policy_citations
independent_variables.definition: The number of policy-cited papers previously authored by any co-author of the paper.

independent_variables.name: country_of_origin
independent_variables.definition: The country of the author's affiliation, used to analyze regional disparities in policy citation rates.

dependent_variables.name: policy_citation
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the paper is cited in an IGO policy document, 0 otherwise.

dependent_variables.name: citation_distance
dependent_variables.formula: The shortest citation-path distance from the paper to an IGO policy document.

control_variables.name: academic_citation_count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the general academic impact of a paper, which may influence its likelihood of being cited in policy.

control_variables.name: number_of_authors
control_variables.rationale: To control for the potential influence of collaboration size on policy citation likelihood.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and network analysis to examine the distribution of policy citations and the structure of co-authorship networks among policy-influential scientists.

network_construction.construction_method: Co-authorship networks are constructed based on the authors of policy-cited papers, with edges representing collaborative relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics and influence of scientists who receive a disproportionate share of policy citations with those who do not.

key_quantities: Policy citation probability by journal volume
Policy citation probability by number of policy-cited references

result_claims.value: 93.3% of papers are connected to IGO policy documents within 3 or 4 steps in the citation network.
Papers citing more than eight policy-cited references have a 30% chance of subsequent policy citation.
A small group of scientists (PI-Sci) receive a disproportionate share of policy citations and collaborate within dense international networks.

partial_results.metric: Concentration of influence in emerging fields
partial_results.note: The concentration of influence is weaker in rapidly evolving areas like data science and AI compared to established fields like climate modeling.

conclusion_claims: A small group of policy-influential scientists (PI-Sci) dominate the flow of scientific knowledge into IGO policy, particularly in established fields.
The structure of co-authorship networks and the prior policy influence of authors are key factors in determining the likelihood of policy citation.
limitations: The study focuses on IGO policy documents and may not capture the full scope of science-policy interactions in other contexts.
The analysis is based on citation data, which may not fully reflect the actual impact of scientific research on policy decisions.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed patterns in co-authorship networks are not artifacts of the network construction method.
To confirm that the identified factors (journal selection, policy-cited references, and authors' prior policy citations) consistently predict policy citation likelihood.

fitzgerald2021_localization.title: Is academia becoming more localised? The growth of regional knowledge networks within international research collaboration
fitzgerald2021_localization.research_idea: The paper investigates whether international research collaboration is becoming more localized over time, with a focus on the formation of regional knowledge networks and their alignment with historical geopolitical or colonial ties.
fitzgerald2021_localization.research_question: Is there an increasing trend in regional knowledge networks within international research collaboration, and how do these networks align with historical or geographical factors?
hypotheses: International research collaboration is becoming more localized over time.
Regional knowledge networks are increasingly aligned with geographical regions rather than historical geopolitical or colonial lines.
fitzgerald2021_localization.data_description: A dataset of international collaboratively authored scientific publications covering a range of disciplines published between 1970 and 2018.

independent_variables.name: time_period
independent_variables.definition: The year of publication, used to analyze trends over time.

dependent_variables.name: collaboration_intensity
dependent_variables.formula: The number of co-authorship collaborations between countries within a region versus between regions.

control_variables.name: historical_geopolitical_alliances
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of historical ties on collaboration patterns.

statistical_method.specification: Application of network science methods including historical profile clustering, entropy of collaborations, community detection, and mutual information comparisons.

network_construction.construction_method: Based on co-authorship data from Scopus, with edges representing collaborative publications between countries.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the alignment of collaboration networks with historical geopolitical lines versus geographical regions.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide detailed disciplinary breakdowns of the results.

conclusion_claims: International research collaboration has become more localized over the past five decades, with an increase in within-region collaboration relative to international collaboration.
The alignment of collaboration networks with geographical regions has increased since 2000, while historical geopolitical or colonial lines have become less relevant.
limitations: The study is based on Scopus data, which may not capture all scientific publications, especially from non-English or non-Western sources.
The analysis does not provide a detailed breakdown of collaboration patterns by specific disciplines.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the stability of detected communities over time.
To quantify the diversity and localization of collaboration patterns.

nikolentzos2021_author_collaboration.title: Can Author Collaboration Reveal Impact? The Case of ℎ-index
nikolentzos2021_author_collaboration.research_idea: This paper investigates whether the collaboration patterns of an author are good predictors of the author’s future ℎ-index. It leverages graph neural networks to predict the ℎ-index based on the author’s collaboration network and the textual content of their papers.
nikolentzos2021_author_collaboration.research_question: Can the collaboration patterns of an author predict their future ℎ-index?
hypotheses: An author's structural role in the co-authorship network is related to their future ℎ-index.
Graph neural networks can outperform traditional machine learning methods in predicting the ℎ-index.
nikolentzos2021_author_collaboration.data_description: A large-scale network consisting of more than 1 million authors and over 37 million edges, representing co-authorship relationships in computer science publications.

independent_variables.name: co_authorship_network
independent_variables.definition: The structural role of an author in the co-authorship network.

independent_variables.name: textual_content
independent_variables.definition: The textual content of a subset of the author's papers.

dependent_variables.name: future_h_index
dependent_variables.formula: ℎ-index after six months.

control_variables.name: publication_history
control_variables.rationale: To account for the author's past publication record.

statistical_method.specification: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are used to predict the future ℎ-index of authors based on their co-authorship network and textual content.

network_construction.construction_method: A co-authorship network is built where nodes represent authors and edges represent co-authorship relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: No explicit grouping strategy is described.

result_claims.metric: relationship between structural role and future ℎ-index

partial_results.note: Textual features generated from the author's published papers do not provide much information.

conclusion_claims: The structural role of an individual in the co-authorship network is empirically related to their future ℎ-index.
Graph neural networks outperform traditional machine learning methods in predicting the ℎ-index.
limitations: The study is limited to the field of computer science.
The predictive power of textual features is limited.

robustness_checks.method: comparison with standard machine learning techniques
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed GNN approach.

mongeon2023_concentration.title: Concentration of research funding leads to decreasing marginal returns
mongeon2023_concentration.research_idea: This study investigates whether the concentration of research funding in the hands of a few researchers leads to diminishing marginal returns in terms of scientific output and impact.
mongeon2023_concentration.research_question: How do research productivity and scientific impact of individual researchers vary with the amount of funding they receive, and is this variation similar across different scientific fields?
hypotheses: Higher amounts of funding will lead to higher scientific output and impact.
The relationship between funding and scientific output/impact will exhibit diminishing marginal returns.
mongeon2023_concentration.data_source: Funding data from research councils in Québec, combined with publication and citation data.
mongeon2023_concentration.data_description: The study analyzes data on 12,720 researchers in Québec over a 15-year period (1998-2012), with scientific output and impact measured from 2000 to 2013.

filters: Researchers in Québec who received funding from national and provincial research councils

independent_variables.name: funding_amount
independent_variables.definition: The total amount of research funding received by each researcher over the 15-year period.

dependent_variables.name: scientific_output
dependent_variables.formula: Number of scientific papers produced by each researcher from 2000 to 2013.

dependent_variables.name: scientific_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Citation impact of each researcher's publications from 2000 to 2013.

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in output and impact across scientific fields.

control_variables.name: career_stage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in productivity and impact based on the researcher's career stage.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and regression analysis to examine the relationship between funding and scientific output and impact.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the scientific output and impact of researchers who received the most funding versus those who received less funding.

result_claims.value: do not stand out in terms of output and impact

partial_results.note: The study does not provide detailed results on how the relationship between funding and output/impact varies across specific fields.

conclusion_claims: The concentration of research funding in the hands of a few researchers generally produces diminishing marginal returns in terms of scientific output and impact.
The most funded researchers do not necessarily produce the highest scientific output or impact.
limitations: The study is limited to researchers in Québec and may not be generalizable to other regions or countries.
The study does not account for all potential confounding factors that may influence scientific output and impact.

robustness_checks.rationale: To verify whether the results hold across different scientific fields and career stages.

nakajima2023_higher_order_rich_club.title: Higher-order rich-club phenomenon in collaborative research grant networks
nakajima2023_higher_order_rich_club.research_idea: The paper investigates higher-order rich-club phenomena in networks of collaborative research grants among institutions and their associations with research impact. It uses a bipartite network representation to analyze the structure and productivity of these collaborations.
nakajima2023_higher_order_rich_club.research_question: Do institutions participating in many collaborative grants gain advantages in their per-dollar research impact when they densely collaborate with each other (i.e., they form a rich club) in research grants?
hypotheses: Institutions that form rich clubs in collaborative grant networks will have higher per-dollar research impact compared to control groups.
Collaborative grants within rich clubs will be more productive in a per-dollar sense than those outside of rich clubs.
nakajima2023_higher_order_rich_club.data_description: The study uses publicly available data from the National Science Foundation (NSF) in the US, covering collaborative grants and single-institution grants awarded between January 1, 2000, and December 31, 2020. The Web of Science Core Collection is used to quantify research outputs, including papers published between 2000 and 2020 with acknowledgments of NSF funding.

filters: Collaborative grants with at least two awards (institutions)
Papers with at least five citations or acknowledgments
Institutions categorized as universities, colleges, or schools

independent_variables.name: rich_club_participation
independent_variables.definition: Whether an institution is part of a rich club in the collaborative grant network.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of institutions involved in a collaborative grant.

independent_variables.name: grant_amount
independent_variables.definition: The monetary amount of a grant awarded to an institution.

dependent_variables.name: per_dollar_productivity
dependent_variables.formula: Number of published papers per dollar of grant funding.

dependent_variables.name: per_dollar_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations per dollar of grant funding.

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in research output and impact based on the type of institution.

control_variables.name: grant_duration
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time available for research output and impact.

control_variables.name: research_discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices and research output across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses bipartite network analysis to identify rich clubs and their properties. It compares the productivity and impact of grants within and outside of rich clubs.

network_construction.construction_method: A bipartite network is constructed with institutions and collaborative grants as nodes. Edges represent the participation of an institution in a grant. The network is analyzed to identify rich clubs based on the number of collaborations and the density of connections among institutions.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the research productivity and impact of institutions that are part of dense collaboration networks (rich clubs) with those that are not.

key_quantities: Per-dollar productivity of grants within and outside of rich clubs
Per-dollar impact of grants within and outside of rich clubs

partial_results.note: The relationship between collaboration size and productivity/impact is not fully quantified in the summary.

conclusion_claims: Institutions that form rich clubs in collaborative grant networks tend to have higher per-dollar research productivity and impact.
Collaborative grants within rich clubs are more productive and impactful in a per-dollar sense compared to those outside of rich clubs.
limitations: The study is limited to US-based institutions and NSF-funded grants, which may not generalize to other regions or funding agencies.
The analysis does not account for unobserved factors that may influence research productivity and impact, such as institutional resources or researcher expertise.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed effects are specific to collaborative grants and not due to general differences in funding or research practices.

robustness_checks.method: Exclusion of grants with fewer than five published papers
robustness_checks.rationale: To reduce the influence of outliers and ensure reliable impact measurements.

tohalino2021_predicting_grants.title: On predicting research grants productivity
tohalino2021_predicting_grants.research_idea: The study investigates whether bibliometric features can predict the success of research grants, focusing on the academic history of researchers and the role of publication and institutional factors.
tohalino2021_predicting_grants.research_question: Can bibliometric features extracted from researchers, institutions, and publications predict the productivity of research grants?
hypotheses: Research subject and publication history are significant predictors of grant productivity.
Institution-based features, when combined with other features, contribute to predicting grant success.
Machine learning models can outperform text-based attributes in predicting grant productivity.
tohalino2021_predicting_grants.data_source: São Paulo Research Foundation (FAPESP) dataset, retrieved from Biblioteca Virtual website
tohalino2021_predicting_grants.data_description: The dataset includes metadata on research grants, including the number of publications derived from the grant, titles, abstracts, and information about PIs and institutions. The dataset contains approximately 31,000 regular grants with a duration of 23-24 months, starting before 2016.

filters: grants with at least 3 years to produce at least one publication

independent_variables.name: research_subject
independent_variables.definition: The topic or field of the research grant.

independent_variables.name: publication_history
independent_variables.definition: The number of publications and citations yielded by the researcher’s previous grants.

independent_variables.name: institution_relevance
independent_variables.definition: The relevance or prestige of the PI’s institution.

independent_variables.name: diversity_of_subfields
independent_variables.definition: The diversity of research topics or subfields studied by the researcher.

dependent_variables.name: grant_productivity
dependent_variables.formula: Whether the grant resulted in at least one scientific publication.

control_variables.name: grant_duration
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time available to produce publications.

control_variables.name: research_area
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in publication patterns across fields.

statistical_method.specification: Binary classification using machine learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP).

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable, as the study does not involve network analysis.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics of grants that resulted in at least one publication versus those that did not.

result_claims.value: research subject and publication history were the most effective predictors
SVM and MLP yielded the highest accuracy rates

partial_results.note: Typical prediction accuracy was not very high, though higher than approaches based on textual features alone.

conclusion_claims: Research subject and publication history are significant predictors of grant productivity.
Institution-based features contribute to predicting grant success when combined with other features.
Machine learning models outperformed text-based attributes in predicting grant productivity, but the overall accuracy was not very high.
limitations: The evaluated features were not highly discriminative.
The study focused on a specific set of bibliometric features, which may not capture all relevant factors influencing grant success.
The dataset is limited to grants from FAPESP in Brazil, which may limit generalizability.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess whether combining different features improves prediction accuracy.
To ensure the robustness of the machine learning models across different subsets of the data.

gross2019_contest_models.title: Contest models highlight inherent inefficiencies of scientific funding competitions
gross2019_contest_models.research_idea: The paper uses contest theory to analyze the efficiency of scientific funding competitions and compares them with partially randomized alternatives like lotteries. It finds that the effort wasted on proposal writing can be comparable to the scientific value of funded research, especially when few proposals are funded.
gross2019_contest_models.research_question: To what extent does the community’s investment in proposal preparation negate the scientific impact of the funding program? Are there alternative mechanisms for awarding funds that advance science more efficiently?
hypotheses: The effort researchers waste in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports.

When professional pressures motivate investigators to seek funding for reasons beyond the value of the proposed science, the entire program can hamper scientific progress when the number of awards is small.

independent_variables.name: proposal_strength
independent_variables.definition: The strength of a proposal, denoted x ≥ 0, which corresponds to how much effort is invested in writing the proposal.

independent_variables.name: scientific_value
independent_variables.definition: The intrinsic scientific value of a project idea, denoted v ≥ 0, which combines the ability of the investigator and the promise of the idea itself.

independent_variables.name: extra_scientific_reward
independent_variables.definition: An additional reward for investigators, denoted v0 ≥ 0, such as prestige or promotion, which is private and not shared with the funder.

independent_variables.name: number_of_awards
independent_variables.definition: The number of proposals that can be funded, which affects the competition and the effort required to win.

dependent_variables.name: net_scientific_progress
dependent_variables.formula: The total scientific value of funded projects minus the scientific waste from proposal writing.

dependent_variables.name: proposal_effort
dependent_variables.formula: The effort (disutility cost) invested in writing proposals, denoted c(v, x) = g(v)h(x).

control_variables.name: intrinsic_scientific_value_of_proposal_writing
control_variables.rationale: A proportion k ∈ [0, 1) of the disutility cost c(v, x) is recouped by honing ideas, which is considered as the intrinsic scientific value of writing a proposal.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a contest model based on economic theory to analyze the efficiency of funding competitions and compare them with lotteries.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the efficiency of traditional proposal competitions with partially randomized alternatives like lotteries.

result_claims.value: The effort wasted in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports.

When professional pressures motivate investigators to seek funding for reasons beyond the value of the proposed science, the entire program can hamper scientific progress when the number of awards is small.

partial_results.note: The results depend on the number of proposals funded and the presence of extra-scientific incentives.

conclusion_claims: The effort researchers waste in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports, especially when only a few proposals can be funded.
Lost efficiency may be restored either by partial lotteries for funding or by funding researchers based on past scientific success instead of proposals for future work.
limitations: The model assumes a large number of scientists and predictable competition strength, which may not always hold in practice.
The model does not account for variability in reviewer opinions or other real-world complexities in the funding process.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing the model with alternative funding mechanisms like lotteries
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the robustness of the findings to different assumptions about the funding process.

mryglod2014_is_your_epl_attractive.title: Is your EPL attractive? Classification of publications through download statistics
mryglod2014_is_your_epl_attractive.research_idea: The paper analyzes download statistics of EPL publications to classify papers based on their attractiveness and to understand the dynamics of download activity over time.
mryglod2014_is_your_epl_attractive.research_question: How can the attractiveness of EPL publications be classified and modeled using download statistics?
hypotheses: Papers exhibit different download patterns over time, which can be modeled and classified.
Open-access papers have different download dynamics compared to non-open-access papers.
mryglod2014_is_your_epl_attractive.data_description: Full-text download statistics for EPL papers published between January 2007 and June 2013, with one month resolution. The dataset includes 4,986 non-OA papers and 377 OA papers.

independent_variables.name: paper_age
independent_variables.definition: The number of months since the paper was published online.

independent_variables.name: open_access_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the paper is open access (OA) or non-open access (non-OA).

dependent_variables.name: download_density
dependent_variables.formula: Mean number of downloads per paper as a function of paper age.

control_variables.name: publication_month
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in download activity.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a two-factor exponential decay model to fit the download density as a function of paper age.
statistical_method.estimation: Model fitting using exponential decay functions.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare download patterns between open-access and non-open-access papers.

result_claims.value: 2% of non-OA papers show intense bursts of download activity
18% of non-OA papers have an especially high degree of attractiveness
20% of papers exhibit delayed recognition with late spurs in download activity
OA papers have more downloads on average than non-OA papers

partial_results.note: The model predicts long-term download proportions, but actual data may vary.

conclusion_claims: EPL papers exhibit distinct download patterns that can be classified into categories such as intense bursts of activity, high attractiveness, and delayed recognition.
Open-access papers accumulate downloads faster than non-open-access papers, but the proportions of papers in each category are similar.
limitations: Download statistics may not fully capture the impact or quality of a paper.
The analysis is limited to EPL and may not generalize to other journals.

robustness_checks.method: model fitting with different decay constants
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the exponential decay model in capturing download patterns.

zhao2020_delayed_recognition.title: Delayed Recognition; the Co-citation Perspective
zhao2020_delayed_recognition.research_idea: The paper investigates the phenomenon of delayed recognition in scientific literature, focusing on co-citations as a lens to study delayed recognition of combined ideas. It extends prior work by analyzing a much larger dataset of co-cited article pairs.
zhao2020_delayed_recognition.research_question: Can delayed recognition be observed in co-citations, and what are the characteristics of such delayed co-citations?
hypotheses: Co-citations can exhibit delayed recognition patterns similar to single publications.
Delayed co-citations are more likely to originate from 'Sleeping Beauty' publications.
zhao2020_delayed_recognition.data_description: The study uses a dataset of over 940 million unique co-cited article pairs from Scopus, covering publications from 1985 to 1995. The dataset includes citation data up to 2018.

filters: Publications with at least 5 cited references
Cited references published no earlier than 1970

independent_variables.name: sleeping_duration
independent_variables.definition: The number of years between the first possible year of co-citation and the year of awakening.

independent_variables.name: peak_co_citation_frequency
independent_variables.definition: The highest annual co-citation frequency observed for a pair.

independent_variables.name: total_co_citation_frequency
independent_variables.definition: The total number of co-citation events for a pair across all years.

dependent_variables.name: delayed_recognition
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the pair meets the criteria for delayed co-citation, 0 otherwise.

dependent_variables.name: awakening_intensity
dependent_variables.formula: The average co-citation frequency during the awakening period.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for disciplinary differences in citation practices.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and frequency analysis to identify and characterize delayed co-citations.

network_construction.construction_method: Co-citation pairs are generated by identifying all combinations of cited references in each article and counting the frequency of co-citation events over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics of co-citation pairs that exhibit delayed recognition with those that do not.

partial_results.note: The study does not provide a definitive answer on whether delayed co-citations are derived from Sleeping Beauty publications.

conclusion_claims: Delayed recognition can be observed in co-citations, with 1,196 cases identified in the dataset.
The characteristics of delayed co-citations include a long sleeping duration and a sharp increase in co-citation frequency during the awakening period.
limitations: The study is limited to co-citations in the Scopus database and may not generalize to other citation databases.
The criteria for identifying delayed co-citations are relatively conservative and may exclude some valid cases.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the findings by comparing them with results from smaller datasets and prior studies on delayed recognition.

ke2018_comparing_scientific_technologica.title: Comparing scientific and technological impact of biomedical research
ke2018_comparing_scientific_technologica.research_idea: The paper investigates the technological impact of biomedical research by analyzing how papers are cited by patents, and compares it with their scientific impact as measured by citations from other papers.
ke2018_comparing_scientific_technologica.research_question: How do the scientific and technological impacts of biomedical research compare in terms of citation patterns, overlap, and temporal dynamics?
hypotheses: There is a positive correlation between citations from patents and from papers.
Highly cited papers in the scientific domain are not necessarily highly cited in the technological domain.
Patent citations generally lag behind paper citations.
ke2018_comparing_scientific_technologica.data_source: USPTO, PubMed, Web of Science, NLM Catalog
ke2018_comparing_scientific_technologica.data_description: The study uses data from 1976 to 2013, including 1,637,072 U.S. patents and 919,222 unique biomedical papers. Citation data from patents and papers are analyzed over time.

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year a paper was published.

independent_variables.name: citation_year
independent_variables.definition: The year a citation (either from a paper or a patent) was received.

independent_variables.name: citation_type
independent_variables.definition: Whether the citation is from a paper or a patent.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by a paper in a given year.

dependent_variables.name: citation_lag
dependent_variables.formula: The difference in years between the publication year of a paper and the year it was cited.

control_variables.name: journal_category
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior across biomedical subfields.

control_variables.name: paper_age
control_variables.rationale: To account for the effect of time since publication on citation likelihood.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and time series analysis to compare citation patterns from patents and papers.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations from patents and papers are tracked over time for each paper.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the overlap and differences between papers that are highly cited in the scientific and technological domains.

key_quantities: correlation between patent and paper citations

result_claims.metric: correlation between patent and paper citations

partial_results.metric: citation dynamics for delayed recognition papers
partial_results.note: The study identifies delayed recognition papers but does not provide a detailed statistical analysis of their characteristics.

conclusion_claims: There is a positive correlation between citations from patents and from papers, but the overlap in top-cited papers is small.
Patent citations generally lag behind paper citations by a median of 6 years, indicating a delay in technological impact.
limitations: The study focuses only on U.S. patents and biomedical papers, limiting generalizability.
The matching of non-patent references to MEDLINE papers may have some false negatives.

robustness_checks.method: manual validation of a subset of non-patent references
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the accuracy of the automated matching process.

kojaku2025_community_centric.title: Community-centric modeling of citation dynamics explains collective citation patterns in science, law, and patents
kojaku2025_community_centric.research_idea: The paper proposes a community-centric model of citation dynamics that explains collective citation patterns across science, law, and patents, including the phenomenon of delayed recognition (sleeping beauties). The model captures shared mechanisms across these systems and improves prediction of future citation success.
kojaku2025_community_centric.research_question: Do citation patterns in science, law, and patents exhibit universal statistical properties, and can a unified model explain these patterns and phenomena like delayed recognition?
hypotheses: Citation patterns in science, law, and patents exhibit similar statistical properties such as preferential attachment, recency bias, and delayed recognition.
A community-centric model can capture the core mechanisms driving citation dynamics and replicate observed patterns, including the delayed recognition of 'sleeping beauties'.
kojaku2025_community_centric.data_source: SciSciNet, American Physical Society (APS) dataset, United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO), and U.S. federal judicial opinions.
kojaku2025_community_centric.data_description: The datasets include scientific citations (SciSciNet and APS), patent citations (USPTO), and legal citations from U.S. federal judicial opinions. These datasets are large-scale and well-documented, covering exponential growth in publications and citations, preferential attachment, recency, and delayed recognition.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly specified, but data spans multiple decades to capture long-term citation dynamics.

independent_variables.name: publication_age
independent_variables.definition: The age of a publication, measured in years since its publication date.

independent_variables.name: accumulated_citations
independent_variables.definition: The total number of citations a publication has received up to a given time point.

independent_variables.name: recency
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how recent a publication is, influencing the likelihood of being cited.

dependent_variables.name: new_citations
dependent_variables.formula: The number of new citations a publication receives in a given year.

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across scientific, patent, and legal domains.

control_variables.name: publication_type
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in citation behavior between scientific papers, patents, and legal opinions.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistical analysis to identify patterns in citation dynamics, including degree distribution, preferential attachment, aging functions, and the distribution of sleeping beauties.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are constructed by linking each publication to the publications it cites, with edges representing citations and directed from citing to cited publications.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation patterns across different knowledge systems and assess the universality of observed statistical properties.

partial_results.note: The model's ability to predict future citation success is not fully quantified in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: Citation patterns in science, law, and patents exhibit remarkably similar statistical properties, including preferential attachment, recency bias, and delayed recognition.
The proposed Community Citation Model (CCM) captures the core mechanisms driving these patterns and provides a unifying framework for understanding citation dynamics across knowledge systems.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly address the limitations of the model in capturing all aspects of citation dynamics.
The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and does not include formal hypothesis testing or inferential methods.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the model's ability to predict future citation success across different domains and time periods.

mir2026_forsaking.title: Forsaking your own: unveiling the delayed recognition of Garfield’s work on the 'delayed recognition' phenomenon
mir2026_forsaking.research_idea: The paper investigates whether the concept of delayed recognition (DR) itself was subject to delayed recognition by analyzing the citation history of Garfield's 1980 paper on DR.
mir2026_forsaking.research_question: Has the phenomenon of delayed recognition itself suffered delayed recognition, particularly in the case of Garfield's 1980 paper?
hypotheses: Garfield's 1980 paper on DR did not receive adequate attention during its early years.
The paper experienced a delayed awakening in citations, similar to the DR phenomenon it describes.
mir2026_forsaking.data_source: Web of Science (WoS), Scopus, and manual analysis of citation data from Current Contents (CC) magazine.
mir2026_forsaking.data_description: Citation data for Garfield's 1980 paper on DR, including citation counts over time, with a focus on the period from 1980 to 2010.

independent_variables.name: year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the citation was recorded.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by Garfield's 1980 paper in a given year.

control_variables.name: publication_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior based on the type of publication (e.g., journal article, editorial).

control_variables.name: journal
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior based on the journal in which the paper was published.

statistical_method.specification: Analysis of citation trends over time using descriptive statistics and visual inspection of citation trajectories.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation behavior before and after the introduction of the term 'Sleeping Beauty' by van Raan (2004a).

result_claims.value: 10 citations up to 2004, 20 citations in the next four years

partial_results.note: Citations were largely self-citations in the early years, which may have skewed the results.

conclusion_claims: Garfield's 1980 paper on delayed recognition experienced a delayed awakening in citations, consistent with the DR phenomenon it describes.
The paper was largely overlooked for 28 years before receiving a modest increase in citations, suggesting that the concept of DR itself may have suffered delayed recognition.
limitations: The citation data for Garfield's work in Current Contents (CC) is not accurately reported in WoS and Scopus, which may affect the reliability of the citation counts.
The analysis is limited to a single paper, which may not be representative of the broader impact of Garfield's work on DR.

robustness_checks.method: Manual verification of citation data from CC
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the accuracy of citation counts for Garfield's 1980 paper.

_source.title: Forsaking your own: unveiling the delayed recognition of Garfield's work on the "delayed recognition" phenomenon

chandrasekaran2021_automating_transfer.title: Automating Transfer Credit Assessment in Student Mobility - A Natural Language Processing-based Approach
chandrasekaran2021_automating_transfer.research_idea: This paper proposes a model to automate the assessment of transfer credits for student mobility using Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, specifically leveraging semantic and taxonomic similarity of learning outcomes (LOs).
chandrasekaran2021_automating_transfer.research_question: How can NLP techniques be used to automate the assessment of transfer credits between courses for student mobility?
hypotheses: Using semantic similarity measures based on transformer models can effectively assess the similarity between learning outcomes.
Combining taxonomic similarity (based on Bloom's taxonomy) with semantic similarity can improve the accuracy of course-to-course similarity assessment.
chandrasekaran2021_automating_transfer.data_source: A new benchmark dataset containing seven course-to-course similarity measures was proposed due to the lack of quality benchmark datasets.
chandrasekaran2021_automating_transfer.data_description: The dataset contains learning outcomes (LOs) from courses, categorized based on Bloom's taxonomy. The dataset is used to assess the similarity between courses for transfer credit evaluation.

independent_variables.name: semantic_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between learning outcomes based on transformer-based language models like RoBERTa.

independent_variables.name: taxonomic_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between learning outcomes based on Bloom's taxonomy levels.

dependent_variables.name: course_similarity
dependent_variables.formula: Aggregation of semantic and taxonomic similarity scores between learning outcomes of two courses.

control_variables.name: domain_specificity
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in domain-specific terminology and structure of learning outcomes across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: The model uses a clustering-inspired methodology to aggregate semantic and taxonomic similarity scores. It also provides tunable parameters for flexibility in decision-making.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the effectiveness of the model in identifying similar and dissimilar courses based on their learning outcomes.

result_claims.value: Higher similarity scores for courses with aligned learning outcomes
Effective in capturing contextual meaning of learning outcomes
Helps in reducing bias by categorizing learning outcomes based on Bloom's taxonomy

partial_results.note: Results are descriptive and not statistically tested for significance due to the lack of benchmark datasets.

conclusion_claims: The proposed model effectively automates the assessment of transfer credits by leveraging semantic and taxonomic similarity of learning outcomes.
The model provides tunable parameters to accommodate different scenarios in the decision-making process for transfer credit evaluation.
limitations: Lack of quality benchmark datasets limits the ability to validate the model's performance comprehensively.
The model's effectiveness may vary across disciplines due to differences in the structure and terminology of learning outcomes.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the model can be adapted to different institutional requirements and scenarios.

sunahara2023_universal_productivity.title: Universal productivity patterns in research careers
sunahara2023_universal_productivity.research_idea: The paper investigates the productivity trajectories of scientists across disciplines and identifies six universal productivity patterns, challenging the canonical narrative of early productivity peaks.
sunahara2023_universal_productivity.research_question: Do universal productivity patterns exist in research careers, and if so, what are they?
