hypotheses: There exist universal productivity patterns in research careers.
The canonical productivity narrative (early peak and decline) is not the most prevalent pattern.
sunahara2023_universal_productivity.data_description: Academic curricula of 8,493 Brazilian researchers from 56 disciplines who hold the CNPq Research Productivity Fellowship, spanning over fifty research disciplines.

filters: career length of at least ten years

independent_variables.name: career_stage
independent_variables.definition: Early-career, mid-career, or senior researchers based on career length

independent_variables.name: discipline
independent_variables.definition: Research discipline of the scientist

independent_variables.name: productivity_inflation
independent_variables.definition: Discipline-specific productivity inflation adjusted to 2015 values

dependent_variables.name: productivity_trajectory
dependent_variables.formula: Deflated, standardized, and smoothed yearly publication counts using z-scores and Gaussian filtering

control_variables.name: career_length
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in career duration across researchers

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for discipline-specific productivity trends

statistical_method.specification: Dynamic time warping (DTW) for trajectory similarity, UMAP for dimensionality reduction, and clustering based on pairwise similarities

network_construction.construction_method: DTW-based similarity matrix and UMAP for network representation of productivity trajectories

grouping_strategy.rationale: To identify and compare the prevalence of different productivity patterns across researchers

partial_results.note: The study suggests cohort effects but does not provide a detailed analysis of their impact on productivity patterns

conclusion_claims: There are six universal productivity patterns in research careers: constant, u-shaped, decreasing, periodic, increasing, and canonical.
The canonical productivity pattern is the most prevalent, but productivity peaks occur more frequently around mid-career rather than early.
limitations: The study is limited to Brazilian researchers, which may affect generalizability to other countries.
The dataset does not include researchers outside the CNPq Research Productivity Fellowship, potentially introducing selection bias.

robustness_checks.method: deflation and standardization of productivity series
robustness_checks.rationale: To account for discipline-specific productivity inflation and make trajectories comparable
To reduce noise in productivity series and improve pattern identification

shirouyeh2023_predicting_star_scientists.title: Predicting Star Scientists in the Field of Artificial Intelligence: A Machine Learning Approach
shirouyeh2023_predicting_star_scientists.research_idea: This study proposes a machine learning model to predict star scientists in the field of artificial intelligence by analyzing early-career features, network measures, and diversity metrics. The model highlights the importance of collaboration, diversity, and research output in identifying rising stars.
shirouyeh2023_predicting_star_scientists.research_question: What features can be used to predict star scientists in the field of artificial intelligence, and how do these features differentiate rising stars from their non-rising counterparts?
hypotheses: Rising stars in AI will exhibit distinct patterns in early-career features compared to non-rising stars.
Features such as gender and ethnic diversity will significantly impact an author's career development and success.
The number of articles, group discipline diversity, and weighted degree centrality will be the most important predictors of star scientists in AI.

independent_variables.name: number_of_articles
independent_variables.definition: The total number of articles published by the researcher.

independent_variables.name: group_discipline_diversity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the diversity of disciplines within the research group of the scientist.

independent_variables.name: weighted_degree_centrality
independent_variables.definition: A network measure indicating the importance of a researcher in their scientific network, weighted by the strength of connections.

independent_variables.name: gender
independent_variables.definition: The gender of the researcher.

independent_variables.name: ethnic_diversity
independent_variables.definition: The ethnic background of the researcher.

dependent_variables.name: star_scientist_status
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the researcher is identified as a star scientist, 0 otherwise.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in research output and recognition.

control_variables.name: institution_rank
control_variables.rationale: To control for the influence of the researcher's affiliated institution on their success.

statistical_method.specification: A machine learning model is used to predict star scientists, incorporating features such as network measures, diversity metrics, and research output.
statistical_method.estimation: Machine learning algorithms (not specified in detail) are used for prediction.

network_construction.construction_method: The network is built based on coauthorship relationships among researchers in the field of AI, with edges weighted by the frequency and strength of collaborations.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the early-career features of researchers who become star scientists with those who do not, in order to identify distinguishing characteristics.

partial_results.note: The specific data sources used in the study are not detailed in the provided text.
The exact machine learning algorithm used for prediction is not specified in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: Rising stars in AI follow different patterns in early-career features compared to non-rising stars.
The most important features in predicting star scientists in AI are the number of articles, group discipline diversity, and weighted degree centrality.
limitations: The study does not specify the exact data sources used, which limits the reproducibility of the findings.
The specific machine learning algorithm used for prediction is not detailed, making it difficult to assess the model's performance and robustness.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the model's predictions are reliable and not overfit to the training data.
To assess the impact of varying input features on the model's predictions.

abramo2013_assessing_accuracy.title: Assessing the accuracy of the h and g indexes for measuring researchers’ productivity
abramo2013_assessing_accuracy.venue: Journal of the American Society for Information Science and Technology
abramo2013_assessing_accuracy.research_idea: The paper evaluates the accuracy of the h-index and g-index in measuring individual researchers' productivity by comparing them to a more accurate indicator, the fractional scientific strength (FSS).
abramo2013_assessing_accuracy.research_question: To what extent do the h-index and g-index accurately measure individual researchers' productivity compared to the FSS?
hypotheses: The h-index and g-index will show significant discrepancies in ranking researchers compared to the FSS.
The g-index will provide a more accurate measure of productivity than the h-index.
abramo2013_assessing_accuracy.data_description: The dataset includes the scientific production of Italian academic researchers in the hard sciences over the period 2001-2005. It covers over 20,000 researchers across 165 subject fields.

independent_variables.name: h-index
independent_variables.definition: The maximum number h of works by a scientist that have at least h citations each.

independent_variables.name: g-index
independent_variables.definition: The highest number g of articles that together received g² or more citations.

dependent_variables.name: FSS
dependent_variables.formula: A proxy measure of individual researcher productivity that accounts for the overall impact of a researcher's production, field normalization, and co-authorship adjustments.

control_variables.name: subject field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in publication intensity and citation practices across fields.

control_variables.name: number of co-authors
control_variables.rationale: To adjust for the contribution of individual researchers in multi-author publications.

statistical_method.specification: The study constructs and compares rankings of researchers based on the h-index, g-index, and FSS. It quantifies the shifts in ranks between the indicators.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the accuracy of the h-index and g-index in measuring productivity against the FSS.

key_quantities: percentage of researchers with significant rank changes

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide exact numerical values for rank shifts but describes them as significant.

conclusion_claims: The h-index and g-index show significant discrepancies in ranking researchers compared to the FSS, indicating that they are not fully accurate measures of productivity.
The g-index provides a more accurate measure of productivity than the h-index, but neither is as accurate as the FSS.
limitations: The study is limited to Italian academic researchers in the hard sciences, so the results may not generalize to other countries or fields.
The FSS is a newly proposed indicator, and its validity and reliability have not been widely tested in other contexts.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed differences in rankings are not due to random variation or specific to one indicator.

paraskevopoulos2021_academic_wanderer.title: The academic wanderer: structure of collaboration network and relation with research performance
paraskevopoulos2021_academic_wanderer.research_idea: This study explores the relationship between the structure of scientists' co-authorship ego networks, academic performance, and academic mobility, leveraging cognitive models from anthropology to understand how cognitive investment in collaborations affects research outcomes.
paraskevopoulos2021_academic_wanderer.research_question: How do the structure of co-authorship ego networks, academic performance, and academic mobility interrelate across different career stages?
hypotheses: There is a positive correlation between academic performance (productivity and impact) and the size of co-authorship ego network layers.
Academic mobility (both international and domestic) is associated with larger collaboration networks.
A cognitive capacity bound exists in the size of collaboration networks, similar to social networks.
International and domestic migrants differ in their efficiency in exploiting collaboration networks for productivity and impact.
paraskevopoulos2021_academic_wanderer.data_description: A dataset comprising the geo-referenced publications of 81,500 authors extracted from Scopus, one of the largest repositories of academic knowledge.

filters: Career stages ranging from young researchers to distinguished professors

independent_variables.name: academic_performance
independent_variables.definition: Measured by scientific productivity (number of publications) and impact (e.g., h-index).

independent_variables.name: academic_mobility
independent_variables.definition: Includes both pure academic mobility (number of affiliation changes) and geographical mobility (relocations to different countries).

independent_variables.name: ego_network_structure
independent_variables.definition: Structure of co-authorship ego networks, characterized by the sizes of concentric circles (layers) representing different levels of cognitive investment.

dependent_variables.name: ego_network_layer_size
dependent_variables.formula: Number of co-authors in each concentric layer of the ego network.

dependent_variables.name: research_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Measured by citation impact and h-index.

control_variables.name: career_stage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in academic performance and collaboration patterns at different stages of a researcher's career.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for variations in collaboration norms and research impact across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis and correlation to explore the relationships between academic performance, mobility, and ego network structure.

network_construction.construction_method: Ego networks are constructed by considering each author as the central node (ego) and connecting them to their co-authors (alters) based on co-authorship relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare how different types of academic mobility affect the structure and effectiveness of collaboration networks.

key_quantities: correlation between ego network layer sizes and academic performance
comparison of ego network growth between international and domestic migrants

result_claims.metric: correlation between ego network layer sizes and academic performance
capacity bound effect on ego network size
international migrants vs. domestic migrants in ego network growth
result_claims.value: International migrants grow larger networks, domestic migrants achieve higher impact

partial_results.metric: exact mechanisms of cognitive investment in collaboration
partial_results.note: Not fully quantified due to the descriptive nature of the analysis.

conclusion_claims: There is a clear correlation between the structure of co-authorship ego networks and academic performance indices, with higher productivity and impact associated with larger networks.
International academic migrants are better at growing their networks, while domestic migrants are better at achieving higher impact through their collaborations.
limitations: The study is descriptive and does not establish causal relationships between variables.
The dataset does not include detailed information on the cognitive effort or quality of collaborations.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing results across different career stages
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed patterns are not specific to a particular stage of a researcher's career.

robustness_checks.method: Analyzing the correlation between ego network size and impact separately for international and domestic migrants
robustness_checks.rationale: To confirm the differential effects of mobility types on research outcomes.

li2019_achieving_competitive_advantage.title: Achieving competitive advantage in academia through early career coauthorship with top scientists
li2019_achieving_competitive_advantage.research_idea: The paper investigates the long-term impact of coauthorship with top-cited scientists on the careers of junior researchers, finding that such coauthorship provides a persistent competitive advantage.
li2019_achieving_competitive_advantage.research_question: Does coauthorship with top scientists early in a junior researcher's career provide a long-term competitive advantage?
hypotheses: Junior researchers who coauthor with top scientists will have a higher probability of becoming top scientists themselves.
The impact of coauthorship with top scientists is stronger for junior researchers affiliated with less prestigious institutions.
li2019_achieving_competitive_advantage.data_source: Academic publication data from four scientific disciplines (Cell Biology, Chemistry, Physics, and Neuroscience)
li2019_achieving_competitive_advantage.data_description: The dataset includes researchers whose careers started between 1980 and 1998 and lasted at least 20 years, with at least 10 publications and at least one paper every five years. The total sample size is 22,601 researchers.

filters: At least one paper every five years

independent_variables.name: early_career_coauthorship_with_top_scientists
independent_variables.definition: Whether a junior researcher coauthored a paper with a top-cited scientist early in their career (none, single, multiple)

independent_variables.name: institutional_prestige
independent_variables.definition: Measured by the average adjusted Nature Index of the researcher's institution and the institutions of their coauthors

dependent_variables.name: probability_of_becoming_top_scientist
dependent_variables.formula: Whether a researcher becomes a top scientist (top 5% of cited authors in their discipline) in their 20th career year

control_variables.name: early_career_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To account for the number of papers published in the first 3 career years

control_variables.name: early_career_citations
control_variables.rationale: To account for the number of citations received in the first 3 career years

statistical_method.specification: Matched pair analysis comparing junior researchers who coauthored with top scientists to those who did not, controlling for institutional prestige, productivity, and citations.

network_construction.construction_method: Coauthorship networks are built based on publication data, with edges representing coauthorship relationships between researchers.

groups: No early coauthorship with top scientists
Single early coauthorship with top scientists
Multiple early coauthorships with top scientists
grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the long-term career outcomes of junior researchers based on their early coauthorship with top scientists.

key_quantities: Probability of becoming a top scientist
Number of junior researchers in each institutional prestige quintile

result_claims.metric: Probability of becoming a top scientist
result_claims.value: Higher for researchers with early coauthorship with top scientists

result_claims.metric: Impact of coauthorship on researchers from less prestigious institutions
result_claims.value: Stronger for researchers from less prestigious institutions

partial_results.note: The predictive power of early coauthorship is not fully quantified in the paper.

conclusion_claims: Junior researchers who coauthor with top scientists early in their careers enjoy a persistent competitive advantage, leading to a higher probability of becoming top scientists themselves.
The impact of coauthorship with top scientists is strongest for junior researchers affiliated with less prestigious institutions, suggesting that these institutions may have untapped potential.
limitations: The study is based on observational data, so causality cannot be definitively established.
The analysis is limited to four scientific disciplines and may not generalize to other fields.

robustness_checks.method: Controlling for institutional prestige, productivity, and citations
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed effects are not due to confounding factors.

zhao2025_novelty_measurements.title: A Review on the Novelty Measurements of Academic Papers
zhao2025_novelty_measurements.research_idea: This paper provides a systematic review of novelty measurements in academic papers, comparing novelty with related concepts and reviewing existing measures, validation approaches, and tools for quantifying scientific novelty.
zhao2025_novelty_measurements.research_question: What are the current methods, tools, and datasets for measuring the novelty of academic papers, and what are the open issues for future research?

statistical_method.specification: The paper is a literature review and does not present statistical models or analyses.

conclusion_claims: Novelty is a critical but challenging concept to measure in academic papers, with various definitions and approaches proposed in the literature.

The paper identifies limitations in current novelty measures and proposes open issues for future research, including the evaluation of interdisciplinary novelty and the development of more robust validation methods.
limitations: The paper is a review and does not present original empirical data or analysis.
It does not provide a comprehensive quantitative evaluation of the effectiveness of existing novelty measures.

slavov2022_eternalism.title: Eternalism and the problem of hyperplanes
slavov2022_eternalism.research_idea: The paper investigates the philosophical implications of eternalism in the context of the relativity of simultaneity and the conventionality of simultaneity. It explores whether eternalism can be supported without relying on hyperplanes of simultaneity.
slavov2022_eternalism.research_question: Can eternalism be defended without relying on hyperplanes of simultaneity, given the conventionality of simultaneity?
hypotheses: Traditional eternalist arguments based on hyperplanes are compromised by the conventionality of simultaneity.
Eternalism can still be supported through alternative strategies that do not rely on hyperplanes.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses philosophical analysis and logical reasoning to evaluate arguments for and against eternalism.

partial_results.note: Lack of a rigorous proof for eternalism remains an open issue.

conclusion_claims: Eternalism remains a credible alternative to presentism despite the challenges posed by the conventionality of simultaneity.
There are cogent reasons to prefer eternalism over presentism, even in the absence of hyperplanes.
limitations: The paper does not provide a rigorous proof for eternalism.
The analysis is primarily philosophical and does not rely on empirical data.

zhao2026_novelty_differences.title: Exploring Novelty Differences between Industry and Academia: A Knowledge Entity-centric Perspective
zhao2026_novelty_differences.research_idea: This study investigates the differences in novelty between academic and industrial research outputs by leveraging a unified semantic space to analyze fine-grained knowledge entities (Methods, Tools, Datasets, Metrics) in both patents and papers. It provides a generalizable framework for novelty comparison across different types of literature.
zhao2026_novelty_differences.research_question: Is there a difference in the novelty of scientific and technical literature between industry and academia?
hypotheses: Academic research outputs will demonstrate higher novelty compared to industry outputs.
Academia-industry collaboration will enhance the novelty of patents but not of research papers.
zhao2026_novelty_differences.data_source: Top conference papers and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) patent dataset
zhao2026_novelty_differences.data_description: The study integrates top conference papers in NLP with the USPTO patent dataset to analyze novelty across different types of literature. The data includes both academic and industrial outputs in the field of artificial intelligence.

independent_variables.name: institution_type
independent_variables.definition: Whether the research output is from academia or industry

independent_variables.name: collaboration_type
independent_variables.definition: Whether the research output is a result of academia-industry collaboration

dependent_variables.name: novelty_score
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated as the semantic distance between fine-grained knowledge entities in a unified semantic space

control_variables.name: document_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in novelty expression between patents and papers

control_variables.name: entity_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in novelty contributions from different types of knowledge entities (Methods, Tools, Datasets, Metrics)

statistical_method.specification: A regression model is constructed to analyze the differences in publication novelty between industry and academia

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the novelty of research outputs between academic and industrial institutions and to assess the impact of collaboration

result_claims.value: Academia-industry collaboration has a limited effect on enhancing the novelty of research papers but helps to enhance the novelty of patents

partial_results.note: The effect of collaboration on novelty is limited in research papers but significant in patents

conclusion_claims: Academia demonstrates higher novelty outputs compared to industry, particularly in patents.
Academia-industry collaboration has a limited effect on enhancing the novelty of research papers but helps to enhance the novelty of patents.
limitations: The study focuses on the field of artificial intelligence and natural language processing, limiting generalizability to other domains.
The novelty measurement is based on semantic distances in a unified semantic space, which may not capture all aspects of novelty.

robustness_checks.rationale: Not explicitly mentioned

wagner2023_openness_impact.title: Openness and Impact of Leading Scientific Countries
wagner2023_openness_impact.research_idea: This paper investigates the relationship between international scientific collaboration and the impact of scientific output, using fractionalized field-weighted citation impact (fracFWCI) and openness indices to assess the benefits of international collaboration and mobility.
wagner2023_openness_impact.research_question: Does international collaboration and researcher mobility correlate with higher scientific impact, and how can this be measured?
hypotheses: Countries with higher levels of international collaboration and researcher mobility will have higher scientific impact.
Public spending on R&D will moderate the relationship between openness and scientific impact.
wagner2023_openness_impact.data_description: The study uses Elsevier's national-level full and fractional paper counts and field-weighted citation counts for 2013, OECD data on international mobility of the scientific workforce in 2013, and OECD data on government budget allocation on R&D in 2011.

independent_variables.name: international_coauthorship_percentage
independent_variables.definition: The percentage of internationally coauthored papers for each country.

independent_variables.name: researcher_mobility
independent_variables.definition: OECD measures of the international mobility of the scientific workforce.

independent_variables.name: GBARD
independent_variables.definition: Government budget allocation on research and development in 2011.

dependent_variables.name: fracFWCI
dependent_variables.formula: Fractional field-weighted citation impact, calculated by assigning a proportional share of citations to each country based on coauthorship.

control_variables.name: country_population
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in national size and potential for scientific output.

control_variables.name: GDP
control_variables.rationale: To control for economic resources available for R&D.

statistical_method.specification: Principal component analysis was used to create an openness index. Correlation and regression analyses were conducted to assess the relationship between openness and scientific impact.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable; the study does not construct a network.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of openness in countries with well-developed science systems versus others.

partial_results.note: The relationship between public spending and scientific impact is not fully explored due to limited data.

conclusion_claims: Openness in terms of international collaboration and researcher mobility is strongly correlated with higher scientific impact.
The findings have important implications for policy making around investment in R&D and the management of international flows of students, researchers, and technical workers.
limitations: The study is limited to OECD countries and may not generalize to non-OECD countries.
The data on public spending on international collaborations is incomplete.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing field-level and 'all subjects' fracFWCI calculations
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not sensitive to the method of citation normalization.

abramo2017_stardom.title: How long do top scientists maintain their stardom? An analysis by region, gender and discipline: evidence from Italy
abramo2017_stardom.research_idea: The paper investigates the longevity of top scientists' stardom in Italy, analyzing how long they maintain their top status across different disciplines, regions, and genders.
abramo2017_stardom.research_question: How long do top scientists maintain their stardom, and how does this vary by discipline, gender, and region?
hypotheses: Top scientists are more likely to maintain their stardom over time due to the Matthew effect.
The longevity of stardom varies by discipline, with higher shares in life sciences and lower in engineering.
Female scientists are less likely to maintain top status compared to males.
Top status is less likely to survive in southern Italy than in the north.
abramo2017_stardom.data_source: Ministry of Education, Universities and Research (MIUR) database and the Observatory of Public Research (ORP) database.
abramo2017_stardom.data_description: The data includes all Italian professors in the hard sciences from 2001 to 2012. The dataset contains information on professors' names, gender, academic rank, field, and institutional affiliation. Bibliometric data is used to calculate research performance.

filters: Italian professors in the hard sciences

independent_variables.name: gender
independent_variables.definition: The gender of the professor (male or female)

independent_variables.name: discipline
independent_variables.definition: The research field or discipline of the professor (e.g., life sciences, engineering)

independent_variables.name: region
independent_variables.definition: The macro-region in Italy where the professor is affiliated (north or south)

dependent_variables.name: longevity_of_stardom
dependent_variables.formula: The number of consecutive four-year periods a professor maintains top status

control_variables.name: research_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To account for the baseline research performance of professors

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics to analyze the longevity of stardom among top scientists.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the career progression of top scientists and unproductive professors in terms of maintaining their status over time.

key_quantities: percentage of top scientists maintaining stardom
average longevity of stardom by discipline

result_claims.metric: percentage of top scientists maintaining stardom
longevity of stardom in life sciences

result_claims.value: females are less likely to maintain top status
less likely to survive in southern Italy

partial_results.note: The study investigates the longevity of unproductive professors but does not provide detailed results on this aspect.

conclusion_claims: More than a third of Italy's top scientists maintain their stardom over three consecutive periods.
The longevity of stardom varies by discipline, gender, and region, with implications for national policies on academic recruitment and advancement.
limitations: The study is limited to Italian professors in the hard sciences and may not generalize to other countries or disciplines.
The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and does not include inferential statistical methods to test hypotheses.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the findings are consistent with the observed data patterns.

nikonova2022_measuring_difficulty.title: Measuring Difficulty of Novelty Reaction
nikonova2022_measuring_difficulty.research_idea: The paper proposes a method to approximate the difficulty of novelty reaction for AI systems by measuring the distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces. This method helps evaluate how hard it is for AI systems to adapt to novel situations.
nikonova2022_measuring_difficulty.research_question: How can the difficulty of novelty reaction for AI systems be measured or approximated before the agent interacts with it?
hypotheses: The distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces can be used to approximate the difficulty of novelty reaction.
The predicted difficulty of novelty aligns with the actual performance of AI agents in novel scenarios.
nikonova2022_measuring_difficulty.data_description: The data consists of generated Science Birds levels with and without introduced novelties. A total of 12 novelties were tested using 100 base levels for difficulty estimation and over 1000 levels per novelty for agent evaluation.

independent_variables.name: novelty_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of novelty introduced to the base levels in the Science Birds environment.

independent_variables.name: solution_space_distance
independent_variables.definition: The distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces, calculated using a custom distance function.

dependent_variables.name: agent_pass_rate
dependent_variables.formula: The average pass percentage of state-of-the-art novelty reaction agents in novel game scenarios.

control_variables.name: base_level_similarity
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in agent performance are due to the novelty and not the base level characteristics.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics to compare predicted difficulty with observed agent performance.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Novelties are grouped based on the calculated distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces to evaluate agent performance across difficulty levels.

partial_results.note: These novelties were misclassified due to the randomness of the sampling agents or limited number of sampled levels.

conclusion_claims: The proposed method for measuring novelty reaction difficulty aligns with the observed performance of AI agents in most cases.
The method is practical and can be applied to other domains where novelty difficulty is measured by solution space differences.
limitations: The method relies on random sampling agents, which introduces randomness and potential misclassification.
The limited number of sampled levels may affect the accuracy of difficulty predictions.

robustness_checks.method: increasing the number of sampled levels
robustness_checks.rationale: To reduce the impact of randomness and improve the accuracy of difficulty predictions.

shekar2022_novelty_based.title: Novelty-based Generalization Evaluation for Traffic Light Detection
shekar2022_novelty_based.research_idea: The paper proposes a generalization scoring framework for Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in autonomous driving applications by incorporating the novelty of test samples. The framework uses representation learning and novelty detection to evaluate CNN performance on novel data.
shekar2022_novelty_based.research_question: How can the generalization ability of CNNs in autonomous driving applications be evaluated by considering the novelty of test samples?
hypotheses: Incorporating novelty detection in generalization evaluation will provide a more accurate assessment of CNN performance on novel data.
Using Variational Auto-encoders (VAEs) for dimensionality reduction and novelty scoring will improve the interpretability of the evaluation.
shekar2022_novelty_based.data_source: Bosch Small Traffic light dataset, DriveU dataset
shekar2022_novelty_based.data_description: The datasets contain traffic light images used for training and testing CNN models. The Bosch dataset has approximately 10,000 images, and the DriveU dataset subset has approximately 50,000 images.

independent_variables.name: novelty_score
independent_variables.definition: A score representing the novelty of a test sample based on its likelihood of belonging to the training data distribution.

dependent_variables.name: generalization_score
dependent_variables.formula: Combination of test data prediction performance and novelty score.

control_variables.name: test_dataset_similarity
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that the test dataset is independent of the training data and contains both similar and novel samples.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses Variational Auto-encoders (VAEs) for dimensionality reduction and novelty detection. The novelty of test samples is estimated based on their likelihood of belonging to the training data distribution.

groups: CNN architectures with different generalization scores
grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of different CNN architectures on the traffic light detection task based on their generalization scores.

result_claims.value: Higher scores indicate better performance on novel data
Higher scores indicate more novel test samples

partial_results.note: The results are specific to the traffic light detection application and may not generalize to other domains.

conclusion_claims: The proposed framework provides a more accurate evaluation of CNN generalization by incorporating the novelty of test samples.
Using VAEs for dimensionality reduction and novelty scoring improves the interpretability of the evaluation.
limitations: The results are specific to the traffic light detection application and may not generalize to other domains.
The novelty scoring approach may not capture all aspects of data novelty.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with other novelty detection algorithms
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the proposed framework is robust to different novelty detection methods.

kong2025_citation_structural_diversity.title: Citation Structural Diversity: A Novel and Concise Metric Combining Structure and Semantics for Literature Evaluation
kong2025_citation_structural_diversity.research_idea: This paper introduces a novel literature evaluation model called Citation Structural Diversity, which integrates citation structural features and semantic information to better capture the complexity of academic dissemination and the multidimensional impact of literature.
kong2025_citation_structural_diversity.research_question: Can a literature evaluation model that combines citation structural diversity and semantic information provide a more accurate and comprehensive assessment of academic impact compared to traditional metrics?
hypotheses: Literature with higher citation structural diversity will demonstrate higher citation frequency.
Literature with higher citation structural diversity will exhibit greater long-term academic influence.

independent_variables.name: citation_structural_diversity
independent_variables.definition: A measure combining citation network structure and semantic information to reflect the diversity of a paper's citations.

dependent_variables.name: citation_volume
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by a paper.

dependent_variables.name: long_term_academic_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Measured through a decade-long citation trend analysis.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: journal_impact_factor
control_variables.rationale: To control for the influence of the journal's prestige on citation counts.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses citation network analysis to evaluate the structural diversity of papers and its relationship with citation volume and long-term academic impact.

network_construction.construction_method: Academic papers are treated as nodes, and citation relationships are treated as directed edges to form a citation network.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the citation volume and long-term academic impact of papers with varying levels of citation structural diversity.

partial_results.note: The exact relationship between structural diversity and citation volume/impact is not quantified in the abstract.

conclusion_claims: Citation structural diversity is a promising metric for evaluating literature, offering advantages in capturing interdisciplinarity and long-term academic influence.
The proposed model complements traditional bibliometric indicators and provides a more comprehensive approach to literature evaluation.
limitations: The paper does not specify the exact datasets or databases used for the analysis.
The methodology for integrating semantic information is not detailed in the abstract.

robustness_checks.method: data grouping and decade-long citation trend analysis
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the potential application of the model in literature evaluation.

gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.title: Quantifying interdisciplinary synergy in higher STEM education
gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.research_idea: The paper proposes a data-driven framework to quantify and evaluate interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula using large-scale educational data and information theory-based metrics.
gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.research_question: How can interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula be quantified and evaluated to support the design of integrated educational programs?
hypotheses: Integrated curricula combining two disciplines will show higher synergy scores compared to single-discipline curricula.
Engineering disciplines will form the core of interdisciplinary curriculum design, while basic natural sciences will serve as peripheral stems.
gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.data_source: Higher Education in Korea portal operated by the Ministry of Education in Korea
gim2025_interdisciplinary_synergy.data_description: The dataset includes curricula from 2,841 STEM departments across 161 higher education institutions in Korea for the 2024 spring semester, with a total of 126,437 raw course entries.
available_fields: Mathematics, Physics, Astronomy, and Earth Science

filters: Departments classified under the Standard Classification Information system in Korea

independent_variables.name: discipline_similarity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of similarity between disciplines based on curriculum overlap.

independent_variables.name: credit_allocation
independent_variables.definition: The number of credits allocated to courses in a curriculum.

independent_variables.name: field_combination
independent_variables.definition: The pairing of two disciplines in an integrated curriculum.

dependent_variables.name: curriculum_synergy_score
dependent_variables.formula: Computed using information theory-based metrics that evaluate the integration and synergy between disciplines.

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in curriculum design across institutions.

control_variables.name: total_credits
control_variables.rationale: To normalize for variations in credit requirements across departments.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis and information theory-based metrics to evaluate curriculum synergy and integration.

network_construction.construction_method: A bipartite network is constructed linking departments to courses, and a projected network is used to analyze relationships between disciplines.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the level of interdisciplinarity between curricula that focus on a single discipline versus those that integrate two disciplines.

result_claims.value: Higher for combinations within or across closely related fields, especially in engineering fields.
Higher in engineering fields compared to basic natural sciences.

partial_results.note: The results are based on a specific dataset from Korea and may not generalize to other educational systems.

conclusion_claims: Engineering fields constitute the core structure of interdisciplinary curriculum design, while basic natural science fields serve as peripheral stems.
The proposed framework provides a principled method for quantifying and evaluating interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula.
limitations: The study is limited to data from Korea and may not be generalizable to other countries with different educational systems.
The analysis is based on department-level data, which may not capture finer distinctions at the program or course level.

robustness_checks.method: Sensitivity analysis of credit allocation and curriculum similarity metrics
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not sensitive to variations in credit requirements or similarity measures.

buruk2023_academic_writing.title: Academic Writing with GPT-3.5: Reflections on Practices, Efficacy and Transparency
buruk2023_academic_writing.research_idea: The paper explores the use of GPT-3.5 in academic writing, identifying five methods of integration and reflecting on their efficacy and ethical implications.
buruk2023_academic_writing.research_question: How can GPT-3.5 be effectively and transparently used in academic writing, and what are its impacts on writing practices and ethics?

statistical_method.specification: Qualitative reflection and analysis of personal experience with GPT-3.5 in writing the paper.

conclusion_claims: GPT-3.5 can be used in academic writing in five distinct ways: Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher.
The use of GPT-3.5 in academic writing raises ethical concerns, including plagiarism, misinformation, and transparency, which need to be addressed for responsible use.
limitations: The study is based on a single author's personal experience and is not generalizable.
The paper does not include quantitative analysis or comparative studies of GPT-3.5's efficacy across different writing tasks.

ahmad2025_university_of_lagos.title: Two Decades of Research at the University of Lagos (2004-2023): A Scientometric Analysis of Productivity, Collaboration, and Impact
ahmad2025_university_of_lagos.research_idea: This paper presents a scientometric analysis of research output from the University of Lagos over two decades (2004-2023), focusing on trends in publication volume, collaboration patterns, citation impact, and the most prolific authors, departments, and research domains.
ahmad2025_university_of_lagos.research_question: What are the trends in research productivity, collaboration, and impact at the University of Lagos from 2004 to 2023?
hypotheses: Research productivity at the University of Lagos has increased over the two-decade period.
Collaborative efforts, both local and international, correlate with higher citation impact.
Open-access publications contribute to higher visibility and citation rates.
ahmad2025_university_of_lagos.data_description: Bibliometric data retrieved from the Web of Science covering the period 2004 to 2023, focusing on research output from the University of Lagos.

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: Year of publication, used to analyze trends over time.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_type
independent_variables.definition: Type of collaboration (local, international) to assess its impact on citation rates.

independent_variables.name: open_access
independent_variables.definition: Whether the publication is open access (yes/no).

independent_variables.name: research_domain
independent_variables.definition: Field of research (e.g., Health Sciences, Engineering, Social Sciences).

dependent_variables.name: publication_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of publications per year.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by publications.

control_variables.name: department
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in research output across departments.

control_variables.name: author_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To control for individual author contributions to overall research output.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive analysis of publication trends, collaboration patterns, and citation impact over time.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable; no network analysis was conducted.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare research productivity and impact between the first and second decade of the study period.

partial_results.note: The exact magnitude of the impact of collaboration on citation rates is not quantified in the paper.

conclusion_claims: The University of Lagos has shown a consistent increase in research productivity over the two-decade period, with 2023 recording the highest publication output.
Collaborative efforts, particularly with the United States and the United Kingdom, are positively correlated with higher citation impact.
limitations: The study is based solely on data from the Web of Science, which may not capture all research outputs from the University of Lagos.
The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the specific factors driving the increase in research productivity.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the observed trends are consistent across the two-decade period.

agboke2020_social_media.title: The use of social media among library professionals and patrons: A review of literature
agboke2020_social_media.venue: International Journal of Academic Library and Information Science Research
agboke2020_social_media.research_idea: This paper reviews the literature on the use of social media among library professionals and patrons, highlighting its role in information dissemination, awareness creation, and library service promotion.
agboke2020_social_media.research_question: What are the popular social media platforms used by library professionals and patrons, and what are the reasons for their adoption?
agboke2020_social_media.data_source: Literature review of existing studies and reports on social media use in libraries.
agboke2020_social_media.data_description: The data consists of a synthesis of findings from various studies and reports published between 2010 and 2019, focusing on the use of social media in libraries across different regions.

filters: studies on library professionals and patrons

independent_variables.name: social_media_platform
independent_variables.definition: The specific social media platform used by libraries (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, YouTube).

independent_variables.name: library_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of library (e.g., academic, public, school).

dependent_variables.name: adoption_rate
dependent_variables.formula: Number of libraries adopting a specific social media platform.

dependent_variables.name: usage_frequency
dependent_variables.formula: Frequency of social media use for library services (e.g., number of posts, interactions).

control_variables.name: region
control_variables.rationale: To account for regional differences in social media adoption and usage patterns.

control_variables.name: library_size
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in library size and resources that may influence social media adoption.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis to summarize the findings from the literature review.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable as the study is a literature review and does not construct a network.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the use of social media between library professionals and patrons.

result_claims.value: Facebook is the most popular social media platform used by libraries, followed by YouTube, WhatsApp, and others.
Social media is frequently used for information dissemination, awareness creation, and library service promotion.

partial_results.note: The adoption rate varies by region and library type, but specific quantitative data is not provided.

conclusion_claims: Social media is widely adopted by libraries to enhance communication, information dissemination, and service promotion.
Library management should encourage the use of social media to keep libraries relevant and improve user engagement.
limitations: The study is based on a literature review and does not include primary data collection.
The findings are descriptive and do not establish causal relationships.

thelwall2025_openalex_citation.title: Is OpenAlex Suitable for Research Quality Evaluation and Which Citation Indicator is Best?
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.research_idea: This paper evaluates the suitability of OpenAlex for research quality evaluation and compares the effectiveness of different citation-based indicators (raw counts, NCS, NLCS) against expert quality assessments.
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.research_question: Which citation-based indicator and database (OpenAlex or Scopus) best correlates with research quality as judged by expert evaluations?
hypotheses: OpenAlex citation counts are better for research quality indicators than Scopus citation counts.
Field-normalized indicators (NCS or NLCS) are better than raw citation counts for evaluating research quality.
The broadest OpenAlex classification scheme provides the best indicators.
OpenAlex's inclusive document classification compromises the value of field normalization.
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.data_source: OpenAlex, Scopus, and UK Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021 data
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.data_description: 28.6 million articles from 2014-2020, with 97,816 UK REF2021 articles used as gold standards. Two gold standards: ChatGPT scores and average REF2021 expert review scores.

filters: articles in both OpenAlex and Scopus

independent_variables.name: citation_count
independent_variables.definition: Number of citations received by an article

independent_variables.name: NCS
independent_variables.definition: Normalised Citation Score, calculated as citation count divided by the mean citation count of all articles in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: NLCS
independent_variables.definition: Normalised Log-transformed Citation Score, calculated as ln(1 + citation count) divided by the mean ln(1 + citation count) of all articles in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: database
independent_variables.definition: Whether the citation data is from OpenAlex or Scopus

independent_variables.name: classification_scheme
independent_variables.definition: Subject classification scheme used (Scopus broad fields, OpenAlex domains, fields, subfields, or topics)

dependent_variables.name: ChatGPT_score
dependent_variables.formula: Quality score assigned by ChatGPT for each article

dependent_variables.name: REF2021_score
dependent_variables.formula: Average expert review score from the REF2021 evaluation for the department submitting the article

control_variables.name: year
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation rates over time

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across disciplines

control_variables.name: document_type
control_variables.rationale: To ensure only comparable document types are compared

statistical_method.specification: Correlation analysis between 32 citation-based indicators and two gold standard quality scores (ChatGPT and REF2021) across 8,704 correlations

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of citation indicators derived from different databases and their overlap

result_claims.value: at least as good as field-normalized indicators

partial_results.note: Field normalization may be ineffective because more cited fields tend to produce higher quality work, affecting interdisciplinary research or within-field topic differences.

conclusion_claims: OpenAlex is suitable for citation analysis in most fields.
Raw citation counts are at least as good as field-normalized indicators for evaluating research quality.
limitations: The study is limited to UK REF2021 articles and may not generalize to other regions or fields.
The use of ChatGPT as a gold standard may introduce biases or inaccuracies.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the impact of subject classification granularity on indicator performance

robustness_checks.method: correlation analysis with two gold standards
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the robustness of the findings against different quality assessments

park2023_grants_interdisciplinary_advanc.title: Interdisciplinary Papers Supported by Disciplinary Grants Garner Deep and Broad Scientific Impact
park2023_grants_interdisciplinary_advanc.research_idea: The paper investigates the relationship between the interdisciplinarity of research grants and the impact of the resulting scientific publications, revealing that interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants tend to have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
park2023_grants_interdisciplinary_advanc.research_question: Do interdisciplinary grants support high-impact interdisciplinary advances?
hypotheses: Interdisciplinary grants produce interdisciplinary papers with high impact.
Interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
park2023_grants_interdisciplinary_advanc.data_description: The study uses 350,000 grants from 164 agencies in 26 countries and 1.3 million resulting papers published between 1985 and 2009.

independent_variables.name: grant_interdisciplinarity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the interdisciplinarity of a grant, calculated using the Rao-Stirling diversity index based on the distribution of fields in the grant's abstract.

independent_variables.name: paper_interdisciplinarity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the interdisciplinarity of a paper, calculated using the Rao-Stirling diversity index based on the distribution of fields in the paper's references and citations.

dependent_variables.name: paper_impact
dependent_variables.formula: The impact of a paper is measured by its hit rate, defined as the probability of a paper being in the top 5% of citations in its field and year.

dependent_variables.name: number_of_papers
dependent_variables.formula: The number of papers produced by a grant.

control_variables.name: funding_amount
control_variables.rationale: To account for the possibility that larger funding amounts may influence the number of papers and their impact.

control_variables.name: author_prominence
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the prominence of the authors on the impact of the papers.

control_variables.name: team_size
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the size of the research team on the number of papers and their impact.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and comparisons across groups to analyze the relationship between grant interdisciplinarity and paper impact.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of papers supported by interdisciplinary grants versus those supported by disciplinary grants.

result_claims.value: Higher for interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants
Lower for papers supported by interdisciplinary grants

partial_results.note: The impact of interdisciplinary papers supported by interdisciplinary grants is lower than expected, but the reason for this is not fully explained.

conclusion_claims: Interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants tend to have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
Disciplinary grants play an important role in producing high-impact interdisciplinary advances.
limitations: The study does not fully explain why interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants have higher impact.
The results are based on a specific time period (1985-2009) and may not generalize to more recent years.

robustness_checks.method: Controlling for author prominence and team size
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed differences in impact are not due to differences in author prominence or team size.
To confirm the robustness of the results across different funding agencies, time periods, disciplines, and countries.

shaikh2023_social_media_learning.title: The Impact of Social Media in Learning and Teaching: A Bibliometric-based Citation Analysis
shaikh2023_social_media_learning.research_idea: This paper presents a systematic review of the literature on the impact of social media in learning and teaching through bibliometric-based citation analysis. The study maps the evolution of the current literature and identifies leading sources of knowledge in terms of influential journals, authors, and articles.
shaikh2023_social_media_learning.research_question: What is the impact of social media in teaching and learning, and what are the leading sources of knowledge in this field?
shaikh2023_social_media_learning.data_description: A total of 50 top most relevant articles were selected from the Scopus database for citation analysis.

fields: social media in learning and teaching

statistical_method.specification: Citation analysis was conducted to explore the theoretical foundation, identify leading sources of knowledge, and reveal research trends.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation analysis was used to build the network based on the selected articles.

result_claims.value: authored by Baaran S. and Bapitha L.

conclusion_claims: The study outlines the current state of knowledge regarding social media in learning and teaching.
The analysis reveals the leading journals, authors, and articles in the field of social media in education.
limitations: The study is limited to 50 selected articles from the Scopus database.
The focus is on citation analysis, which may not capture all aspects of research impact.

maddi2022_article_processing_charges.title: Article Processing Charges, Altmetrics and Citation Impact: Is there an economic rationale?
maddi2022_article_processing_charges.research_idea: This study investigates the relationship between Article Processing Charges (APCs) and the citation impact of Gold Open Access (OA) publications, as well as the determinants of APCs. It challenges the assumption that higher APCs lead to higher impact and explores the role of Altmetrics in this context.
maddi2022_article_processing_charges.research_question: Does paying high APCs necessarily increase the impact of scientific publications, and what factors determine APCs?
hypotheses: Paying high APCs increases the impact of scientific publications.
Large publishers with high impact charge the highest APCs.
Altmetrics have a significant impact on the visibility and impact of OA articles.
maddi2022_article_processing_charges.data_source: OpenAPC database, Web of Science (OST in-house database), Altmetric.com database
maddi2022_article_processing_charges.data_description: The study uses APC data from the OpenAPC database (109,141 publications from 2006 to 2019), citation data from the Web of Science (83,752 publications), and Altmetrics data from Altmetric.com (62,532 publications).

filters: Publications with matching DOIs in all three databases

independent_variables.name: APC_amount
independent_variables.definition: The amount of the Article Processing Charge (APC) paid for a publication.

independent_variables.name: Hybrid_journal
independent_variables.definition: A flag indicating whether the journal is a hybrid journal (1) or a full OA journal (0).

independent_variables.name: Altmetrics_score
independent_variables.definition: A score representing the Altmetrics attention received by a publication.

independent_variables.name: Publisher_impact
independent_variables.definition: The Mean Normalized Citation Score (MNCS) at the publisher level.

independent_variables.name: Journal_impact
independent_variables.definition: The Mean Normalized Impact of Journals (MNIJ) at the journal level.

dependent_variables.name: Normalized_Citations_Score
dependent_variables.formula: NCS = (Number of citations received by the article) / (Average number of citations in the same discipline and year)

dependent_variables.name: Altmetrics_score
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated based on the Altmetric.com database.

control_variables.name: Discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across disciplines.

control_variables.name: Country
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in APCs and impact based on the country of the journal.

control_variables.name: Publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in APCs and impact.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and correlation analysis to explore the relationships between APCs, citation impact, and Altmetrics.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare APCs and impact between hybrid and full OA journals.

key_quantities: Correlation between APCs and citation impact
Average APCs in hybrid vs. full OA journals
Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact

result_claims.metric: Correlation between APCs and citation impact

result_claims.value: 50% higher than in full OA journals

result_claims.metric: Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact

result_claims.value: More correlated with classic bibliometric indicators

partial_results.metric: Correlation between APCs and citation impact
partial_results.note: The correlation is moderate, suggesting that higher APCs do not necessarily lead to higher impact.

partial_results.metric: Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact
partial_results.note: The correlation is lower than expected, indicating that Altmetrics may not be a strong predictor of citation impact.

conclusion_claims: Paying high APCs does not necessarily increase the impact of scientific publications.
Hybrid journals charge significantly higher APCs than full OA journals.
Altmetrics do not have a strong impact on the visibility and citation impact of OA articles.
limitations: The study is based on a specific set of databases and may not be generalizable to all OA publications.
The analysis is descriptive and does not include causal inference methods.

robustness_checks.rationale: To verify the consistency of results across different subsets of the data.
To assess the impact of excluding certain variables or data points on the results.

thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.title: Do altmetric scores reflect article quality? Evidence from the UK Research Excellence Framework 2021
thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.research_idea: This paper investigates whether altmetric scores from Altmetric.com and Mendeley reflect the quality of academic journal articles, using expert peer review scores from the UK Research Excellence Framework 2021 as a benchmark.
thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.research_question: How useful are altmetric scores as indicators of research quality in all fields, and how do they compare to citation counts?
hypotheses: Altmetric scores are better indicators of research quality than previously thought.
Mendeley reader counts are the strongest altmetric indicator of quality in most fields.
Altmetric scores reflect non-scholarly impacts more than citation counts.
thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.data_source: UK Research Excellence Framework 2021 (REF2021) peer review scores, Altmetric.com, Mendeley, and Scopus.
thelwall2023_altmetric_reflection.data_description: The dataset includes 67,030+ journal articles from 2014-2018, across 34 Units of Assessment (UoAs) in all academic fields. Articles were rated by expert peer review for quality (0 to 4*). Altmetric and Mendeley data were matched via DOIs.

filters: Articles with DOIs and Scopus entries
Excluded duplicate articles and those with score 0
Excluded articles after 2018 due to insufficient citation time

independent_variables.name: Altmetric Attention Score
independent_variables.definition: A hybrid score combining various altmetric indicators (e.g., tweets, Mendeley readers, news mentions).

independent_variables.name: Mendeley reader count
independent_variables.definition: Number of readers of an article on Mendeley.

independent_variables.name: Tweet count
independent_variables.definition: Number of tweets mentioning the article.

independent_variables.name: Facebook mentions
independent_variables.definition: Number of Facebook mentions of the article.

independent_variables.name: Blog mentions
independent_variables.definition: Number of blog mentions of the article.

independent_variables.name: News mentions
independent_variables.definition: Number of news articles mentioning the article.

independent_variables.name: Raw Scopus citation count
independent_variables.definition: Total number of citations in Scopus.

independent_variables.name: Field-normalized Scopus citation count
independent_variables.definition: Scopus citation count adjusted for field-specific citation practices.

dependent_variables.name: REF2021 quality score
dependent_variables.formula: 0 (unclassified), 1* (nationally recognized), 2* (internationally recognized), 3* (internationally excellent), 4* (world-leading).

control_variables.name: Field of study
control_variables.rationale: To account for field-specific differences in citation and altmetric practices.

control_variables.name: Year of publication
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in altmetric and citation behavior.

statistical_method.specification: Correlation analysis between altmetric scores and REF2021 quality scores, with comparisons across fields and UoAs.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the strength of altmetric indicators across disciplines.

key_quantities: Correlation coefficients between altmetric scores and quality scores
Comparison of altmetric indicators by field

result_claims.metric: Correlation between Mendeley reader count and quality score
Correlation between tweet count and quality score
Correlation between Altmetric Attention Score and quality score
result_claims.value: Almost as strong as Mendeley reader count

result_claims.metric: Correlation between field-normalized citation count and quality score
result_claims.value: Stronger than altmetrics in most fields

result_claims.metric: Correlation between raw citation count and quality score

partial_results.note: May reduce its strength as a quality indicator in single fields.
Moderately strong in some UoAs but not consistently across all fields.

conclusion_claims: Altmetric scores are better indicators of research quality than previously thought, though not as strong as citation counts.
Mendeley reader counts are the strongest altmetric indicator of quality in most fields, followed by tweet counts and Altmetric Attention Score.
limitations: Field-normalized citation counts may not always be the best indicator in single fields.
Low numbers of articles in arts and humanities UoAs may affect precision.
Altmetric data may not be complete for all DOIs, especially for preprints or alternative versions.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure findings are not field-specific.

robustness_checks.method: Excluding duplicate articles and using median scores
robustness_checks.rationale: To reduce bias from multiple submissions of the same article.

bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.title: Industry Influence in High-Profile Social Media Research
bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.research_idea: This paper investigates the extent of industry influence in high-profile social media research, focusing on the prevalence of undisclosed ties to industry among authors, reviewers, and editors, and the impact of such ties on research visibility and focus.
bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.research_question: To what extent is social media research independent from industry influence?
hypotheses: A significant proportion of high-profile social media research has ties to industry.
These ties are often undisclosed in published research.
Industry-tied research garners more attention and influence than independent research.
Industry ties are associated with a shift in research focus away from platform-scale features.
bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.data_source: OpenAlex, Altmetrics, and manually validated data from industry-announced RFPs and fellowships.
bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.data_description: The dataset includes 295 articles published in top journals (Science, Nature, PNAS, and their transfer journals) on social media research, involving 1210 unique authors. The data spans multiple years and includes information on author affiliations, funding, collaborations, and employment with industry.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly specified, but data as of November 6th, 2025
filters: Articles mentioning specific social media platforms in title or abstract

independent_variables.name: Industry_tie
independent_variables.definition: Whether an author has a disclosable tie to industry (funding, collaboration, or employment) within the journal's specified period for competing interests.

independent_variables.name: Disclosure_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the industry tie is disclosed in the paper (affiliation, competing interests, or funding statements).

independent_variables.name: Journal
independent_variables.definition: The journal in which the paper is published (e.g., Science, Nature, PNAS).

dependent_variables.name: Impact
dependent_variables.formula: Measured by Altmetric attention score, citations, policy mentions, news mentions, and social media mentions.

dependent_variables.name: Topic_focus
dependent_variables.formula: Whether the research focuses on platform-scale features or other topics.

control_variables.name: Author_influence
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of individual authors on research visibility.

control_variables.name: Publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in research impact and industry ties.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and Bayesian binomial regression to analyze the probability of disclosure over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact and focus of research with and without industry ties.

result_claims.value: Twice the impact of independent work

result_claims.metric: Proportion of industry-tied research with no declared competing interests

partial_results.note: Not explicitly analyzed, but suggested to be due to lack of norms or incentives for disclosure.

conclusion_claims: Industry influence in social media research is extensive, impactful, and often opaque.
There is a need to strengthen disclosure norms and implement policies to ensure the visibility of independent research and the integrity of industry-supported research.
limitations: The study focuses on a relatively small corpus of high-profile research, which may not be representative of the broader field.
The analysis relies on public data and manual validation, which may not capture all industry ties.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the accuracy of the identified industry connections.
To assess trends in disclosure rates over time.

purkayastha2026_reviewing_reviewer.title: Reviewing the Reviewer: Elevating Peer Review Quality through LLM-Guided Feedback
purkayastha2026_reviewing_reviewer.research_idea: The paper introduces an LLM-driven framework to improve peer review quality by detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues in review segments and generating targeted feedback using a neurosymbolic module and genetic algorithm.
purkayastha2026_reviewing_reviewer.research_question: How can peer review quality be improved by detecting and addressing lazy thinking and specificity issues in review segments using LLMs?
hypotheses: An LLM-driven framework can outperform zero-shot LLM baselines in detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues in peer reviews.
The proposed feedback generation method can improve review quality by up to 92.4%.
The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset can support multi-label detection of lazy thinking and specificity issues.
purkayastha2026_reviewing_reviewer.data_description: The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset contains 1,309 expert-annotated review sentences from ARR 2022 and EMNLP 2024, with multi-label annotations for lazy thinking and specificity issues.

independent_variables.name: lazy_thinking_issues
independent_variables.definition: Segments of peer reviews annotated for lazy thinking issues based on ARR guidelines.

independent_variables.name: specificity_issues
independent_variables.definition: Segments of peer reviews annotated for specificity issues based on ARR guidelines.

dependent_variables.name: review_quality_improvement
dependent_variables.formula: Percentage reduction in review issues after applying the proposed feedback generation method.

control_variables.name: review_length
control_variables.rationale: Longer reviews may naturally contain more issues, so controlling for length ensures fair comparison.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and controlled review-rewriting studies to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the effectiveness of the proposed feedback generation method on review quality improvement.

partial_results.note: The paper evaluated multiple language models (GPT-4 Omni, Phi3, Gemma2, Llama3.1, and Mistral) but the performance of GPT-4 was not explicitly compared in the abstract.

conclusion_claims: The proposed neuro-symbolic issue detection approach outperforms the strongest LLM-only model in detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues.
The LLM-driven feedback generation module improves the constructiveness and relevance of feedback by about 20% over the best baselines.
The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset is the first multi-label review dataset annotated with lazy thinking and specificity issues for issue identification and feedback generation.
limitations: The experimental section is relatively weak, with only the Gemma model being tested in detail.
The paper does not provide a comprehensive comparison of all tested models (e.g., GPT-4) in the abstract.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed feedback generation method on real-world peer reviews.

kuznetsov2026_peer_review.title: Is Peer Review Really in Decline? Analyzing Review Quality across Venues and Time
kuznetsov2026_peer_review.research_idea: The paper introduces a new framework for evidence-based comparative study of review quality in computer science conferences, focusing on ICLR, NeurIPS, and ACL. It proposes a multi-dimensional schema for quantifying review quality and uses both LLM-based and lightweight measurements to analyze trends over time.
kuznetsov2026_peer_review.research_question: Is there a consistent decline in peer review quality across computer science conferences over time?
hypotheses: Peer review quality is declining due to factors like scarce qualified reviewers, increasing specialization, and high workloads.
Peer review quality has not declined, as conferences refine workflows, grow expert pools, and use AI assistance.
kuznetsov2026_peer_review.data_source: Peer review data from ICLR, NeurIPS, and ACL Rolling Review (ARR) conferences.
kuznetsov2026_peer_review.data_description: The data includes peer review reports from multiple years of these conferences, with varying review formats and structures. The data is semi-structured and includes free-text, numerical, and other fields.

independent_variables.name: time
independent_variables.definition: Year of the reviewing campaign

independent_variables.name: venue
independent_variables.definition: Conference name (ICLR, NeurIPS, ACL)

independent_variables.name: review_format
