independent_variables.definition: Structure of the review (e.g., free-text, structured form)

independent_variables.name: expertise
independent_variables.definition: Depth of reviewer expertise in the paper's topic

dependent_variables.name: review_quality
dependent_variables.formula: Composite score based on substantiveness, actionability, and grounding measurements

control_variables.name: paper_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of paper quality on review content

control_variables.name: reviewer_experience
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of reviewer experience on review quality

control_variables.name: conference_policy
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of conference-specific reviewing policies

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive analysis and correlation analysis to study the relationships between different measurements of review quality and their evolution over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare review quality across different computer science conferences

partial_results.metric: impact of LLMs on review quality
partial_results.note: The impact of LLMs on review quality is not fully quantified due to limited data on their use in reviewing campaigns.

conclusion_claims: Contrary to the popular narrative, there is no consistent decline in median review quality across venues and years.
The diversity of review formats and the evolution of reviewing workflows make it challenging to compare reviews across time and venues.
limitations: The data is scarce, biased, and noisy.
The diversity and evolution of reviewing workflows make consistent application of review quality measures difficult.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the relationships between different measurements of review quality
To ensure the findings are consistent across different time periods

parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.title: Towards A Sustainable Future for Peer Review in Software Engineering
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.venue: 2026 IEEE/ACM 48th International Conference on Software Engineering: Future of Software Engineering (ICSE-FoSE)
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.research_idea: The paper addresses the growing imbalance between the increasing number of paper submissions in software engineering and the limited availability of qualified reviewers, proposing a sustainable future for peer review through scalable training, incentives, and cautious integration of AI tools.
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.research_question: How can the software engineering research community ensure a sustainable and high-quality peer review process in the face of increasing submission volumes and reviewer overload?
hypotheses: Expanding the pool of qualified reviewers through training and mentorship programs will alleviate reviewer overload.
Incentivizing peer review will increase participation and improve the quality of the review process.
Cautious integration of AI tools can support and enhance the peer review process without compromising quality.
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.data_source: The paper references data from software engineering conferences (ICSE, FSE, ASE, ICSME, MSR, SANER) regarding program committee sizes and submission volumes over the last five years.
parra2026_sustainable_peer_review.data_description: The data includes the number of program committee members and the number of main track submissions for the top software engineering conferences from 2020 to 2025.

independent_variables.name: submission_volume
independent_variables.definition: The number of paper submissions to software engineering conferences over time

independent_variables.name: program_committee_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of members in the program committee for each conference

dependent_variables.name: reviewer_workload
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated as the average number of papers assigned to each program committee member

control_variables.name: conference_rank
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in prestige and submission volume between A* and A ranked conferences

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics to analyze trends in submission volumes, program committee sizes, and reviewer workloads over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of submission volume on reviewer workload and program committee size across different conferences.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide quantitative results on the impact of AI tools on the review process, as this is a proposed future direction rather than an evaluated mechanism.

conclusion_claims: The current imbalance between submission volume and reviewer availability is not sustainable and risks harming the quality of peer review in software engineering.
A sustainable future for peer review requires expanding the pool of qualified reviewers through training and mentorship programs, incentivizing participation, and cautiously integrating AI tools.
limitations: The paper is based on descriptive analysis and does not include quantitative evaluation of proposed solutions.
The impact of AI tools on the peer review process is not empirically tested.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed trends in submission volume and reviewer workload are consistent across different software engineering conferences.

bogner2026_state_peer_review.title: The State of Peer Review in Empirical Software Engineering: A Community Survey on Review Load, Quality, and GenAI Use
bogner2026_state_peer_review.research_idea: The paper investigates the current state of peer review in empirical software engineering (ESE) research, focusing on review load, quality, challenges, and the use of generative AI (GenAI) in the reviewing process. It aims to gather community insights to inform evidence-based improvements to the peer review system.
bogner2026_state_peer_review.research_question: What is the current state of peer review in the ESE community, including perceived review load, review quality, challenges, and the use of LLM-based tools, and what suggestions do community members have for improvement?
bogner2026_state_peer_review.data_source: Questionnaire survey responses collected from ESE researchers and reviewers.
bogner2026_state_peer_review.data_description: 120 responses from participants who had acted as peer reviewers in the past 12 months and were active in ESE research. Data included demographic information, perceived review load, review quality assessments, LLM usage, and suggestions for improvement.

filters: Participants must have reviewed at least one scientific paper in the past 12 months and be active in ESE research.

independent_variables.name: review_load
independent_variables.definition: Perceived review load rated on a 5-point ordinal scale from 'very low' to 'very high'.

independent_variables.name: review_type
independent_variables.definition: Type of review (journal, conference, workshop).

independent_variables.name: role
independent_variables.definition: Participant's role (tenured academic, postdoc, non-tenured academic, industry, PhD student).

independent_variables.name: llm_usage
independent_variables.definition: Whether participants use LLM-based tools in the reviewing process.

dependent_variables.name: review_quality
dependent_variables.definition: Perceived quality of reviews, including challenges and issues with the review process.

dependent_variables.name: number_of_reviews
dependent_variables.definition: Number of papers reviewed in the last year across workshops, conferences, and journals.

control_variables.name: experience
control_variables.rationale: Years of experience in ESE research to account for differences in review load and quality perception.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and thematic analysis of free-text responses to summarize the data.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare differences in review load, quality perception, and LLM usage across different career stages and roles.

key_quantities: average number of reviews per year

result_claims.value: High or very high for 80% of participants
Average of 25-32 reviews per person per year
Not explicitly quantified but discussed as a concern

partial_results.note: The extent and impact of LLM use in ESE review processes were not systematically quantified in the survey.

conclusion_claims: The ESE community is experiencing high review loads, with most participants rating their review load as high or very high.
Review quality is a concern, with challenges such as superficial evaluations and inconsistency.
The use of LLMs in the reviewing process is a growing concern, with potential impacts on review integrity and depth.
Community suggestions for improvement include adopting double-anonymous or open reviewing models, structured review forms, and reviewer training programs.
limitations: The sample is skewed towards senior researchers from Europe and North America, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.
The survey did not systematically quantify the extent and impact of LLM use in the ESE review process.
The study is based on self-reported data, which may be subject to social desirability bias.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure transparency and replicability, the survey data and analysis methods were published online.

ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.title: PeeriScope: A Multi-Faceted Framework for Evaluating Peer Review Quality
ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.venue: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW Companion '26)
ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.research_idea: PeeriScope is a modular platform that integrates structured features, rubric-guided large language model assessments, and supervised prediction to evaluate peer review quality along multiple dimensions. It is designed for openness and integration, providing a public interface and a documented API.
ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.research_question: How can peer review quality be systematically, interpretable, and extensibly assessed using a multi-faceted framework?
hypotheses: PeeriScope can effectively evaluate peer review quality along multiple dimensions using structured linguistic metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.
PeeriScope can provide practical deployment and research extensibility through its public interface and documented API.
ebrahimi2026_peeriscope.data_description: OpenAlex is used to retrieve reviewer publication profiles and derive topical-expertise and citation features. The data includes reviewer publication history, citation counts, and scholarly tenure.

independent_variables.name: Review Length
independent_variables.definition: The length of the review text, reported as a proxy for thoroughness.

independent_variables.name: Hedging
independent_variables.definition: Measured using a cue-based neural detector, captures the epistemic stance of the reviewer.

independent_variables.name: Lexical Diversity
independent_variables.definition: Measured as type-token ratio, reflects linguistic variation and effort.

independent_variables.name: Readability
independent_variables.definition: Captured using the Flesch Reading Ease score, reflects how easily the review can be understood.

independent_variables.name: Politeness
independent_variables.definition: Linked to perceptions of fairness and author receptivity.

independent_variables.name: Sentiment
independent_variables.definition: Polarity offers a coarse but informative indicator of evaluative direction.

independent_variables.name: Similarity
independent_variables.definition: General similarity of the review to the manuscript reflects the reviewer’s overall domain proximity.

independent_variables.name: Citations
independent_variables.definition: Mentions of citations provide support for reviewers’ claims and help ground them.

independent_variables.name: Questions
independent_variables.definition: Presence of questions in the review, indicating constructive feedback.

independent_variables.name: Topical Alignment
independent_variables.definition: Measured using SPECTER, reflects the alignment between the submission and the reviewer’s publication history.

independent_variables.name: Citation Count
independent_variables.definition: Characterizes the reviewer’s standing through citation count.

independent_variables.name: Scholarly Tenure
independent_variables.definition: Proxy for influence within a field, derived from the reviewer’s publication history.

dependent_variables.name: Review Quality Score
dependent_variables.formula: Integrated score derived from structured metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.

statistical_method.specification: PeeriScope uses a combination of structured linguistic metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling to evaluate peer review quality.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Not applicable

conclusion_claims: PeeriScope provides a multi-faceted framework for evaluating peer review quality using structured features, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.
PeeriScope is designed for openness and integration, providing a public interface and a documented API for practical deployment and research extensibility.
limitations: Peer-review datasets are inherently sparse, fragmented across venues, and largely inaccessible due to confidentiality constraints.
Evaluating LLM-assisted practices remains difficult due to the lack of annotated, high-quality review data.

benAvraham2026_dream.title: DREAM: Deep Research Evaluation with Agentic Metrics
benAvraham2026_dream.research_idea: The paper introduces DREAM, a framework for evaluating deep research agents (DRAs) that addresses the 'Mirage of Synthesis' by incorporating agentic metrics and active verification. DREAM combines static and adaptive metrics to assess research quality across four verticals: Presentation Quality, Task Compliance, Analytical Depth, and Source Quality.
benAvraham2026_dream.research_question: How can deep research agents be evaluated effectively, considering the multidimensional nature of research quality and the limitations of existing benchmarks?
hypotheses: Current DRE benchmarks suffer from the 'Mirage of Synthesis' due to their inability to detect factual, temporal, and logical degradation.
DREAM's agentic evaluation framework will be more sensitive to factual and temporal decay than existing benchmarks.
benAvraham2026_dream.data_source: The paper does not mention specific datasets or databases used for the study.
benAvraham2026_dream.data_description: The paper does not provide a detailed description of the data used in the study.

independent_variables.name: Evaluation Method
independent_variables.definition: The type of evaluation method used (e.g., DREAM, existing benchmarks).

independent_variables.name: Temporal Validity
independent_variables.definition: Whether the report contains time-sensitive information that is outdated.

independent_variables.name: Factual Correctness
independent_variables.definition: Whether the report contains factual errors.

dependent_variables.name: Evaluation Score
dependent_variables.formula: The score assigned to the report by the evaluation method.

dependent_variables.name: Sensitivity to Factual Errors
dependent_variables.formula: The degree to which the evaluation method detects factual errors in the report.

dependent_variables.name: Sensitivity to Temporal Decay
dependent_variables.formula: The degree to which the evaluation method detects outdated information in the report.

control_variables.name: Report Quality
control_variables.rationale: The overall quality of the report, including presentation and task compliance, to ensure that differences in evaluation scores are due to the evaluation method and not the report itself.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and controlled experiments to compare the performance of DREAM with existing benchmarks.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of DREAM with existing benchmarks in detecting factual and temporal errors.

result_claims.value: DREAM is significantly more sensitive to factual errors than existing benchmarks.
DREAM is significantly more sensitive to temporal decay than existing benchmarks.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide specific numerical results for the evaluation scores.

conclusion_claims: DREAM addresses the 'Mirage of Synthesis' by incorporating agentic metrics and active verification.
DREAM is significantly more sensitive to factual and temporal decay than existing benchmarks, offering a scalable, reference-free evaluation paradigm.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed data on the specific datasets or benchmarks used in the experiments.
The controlled experiments may not fully capture the complexity of real-world research scenarios.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not due to confounding variables and to validate the effectiveness of DREAM in detecting factual and temporal errors.

wu2025_large_language_models.title: Large language models for post-publication research evaluation: Evidence from expert recommendations and citation indicators
wu2025_large_language_models.research_idea: This study explores the potential of large language models (LLMs) to support post-publication research evaluation by benchmarking their outputs against expert judgments and citation-based indicators. The research contributes to understanding the effectiveness of LLMs in evaluating scientific articles and their alignment with human and citation-based metrics.
wu2025_large_language_models.research_question: To what extent can LLMs perform post-publication research evaluation tasks in comparison with expert judgments, and how do model selection and learning strategies influence performance?
hypotheses: LLMs can perform post-publication research evaluation tasks with performance comparable to expert judgments.
Supervised fine-tuning and few-shot prompting improve the alignment of LLM outputs with expert annotations and citation indicators.
wu2025_large_language_models.data_description: The study uses articles from the H1 Connect platform, which includes expert-driven recommendations and evaluations by biomedical faculty researchers. The dataset is used for two evaluation tasks: high-quality article identification and fine-grained evaluation including article rating, merit classification, and expert-style commenting.

independent_variables.name: model_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of LLM used, including BERT models, general-purpose LLMs, and reasoning-oriented LLMs.

independent_variables.name: learning_strategy
independent_variables.definition: The learning strategy applied, including in-context learning, few-shot prompting, and supervised fine-tuning.

dependent_variables.name: evaluation_accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Accuracy of LLM outputs in identifying high-quality articles and performing fine-grained evaluation tasks.

dependent_variables.name: alignment_with_expert_judgments
dependent_variables.formula: Correlation between LLM outputs and expert annotations.

dependent_variables.name: alignment_with_citation_indicators
dependent_variables.formula: Correlation between LLM outputs and citation-based indicators.

control_variables.name: article_quality
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that the evaluation tasks are based on a consistent set of high- and low-quality articles.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis to compare the performance of different LLMs and learning strategies in post-publication research evaluation tasks.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To examine the impact of model architecture on the performance of post-publication research evaluation tasks.

result_claims.value: improved with few-shot prompting and supervised fine-tuning

partial_results.note: Semantically similar examples do not consistently strengthen alignment with citation-based indicators.

conclusion_claims: LLMs demonstrate strong performance in coarse-grained evaluation tasks but struggle with fine-grained stratification of article quality.
Supervised fine-tuning produces the strongest and most balanced results for post-publication research evaluation.
limitations: The study is limited to the biomedical field and the H1 Connect platform.
The closed-source nature of GPT-5 prevents further analysis of its performance advantages.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not specific to a single model type.
To assess the robustness of LLM performance to variations in training and prompting methods.

kousha2025_stylistic_features.title: Which stylistic features fool ChatGPT research evaluations?
kousha2025_stylistic_features.research_idea: This paper investigates whether language-related factors unrelated to research quality influence ChatGPT's research quality scores, and whether these associations are stronger than those observed in expert scores from the Research Excellence Framework (REF).
kousha2025_stylistic_features.research_question: Which textual features of abstracts (e.g., readability, linguistic complexity, and length) associate with LLM scores, and are these associations stronger for LLM scores than for REF expert scores?
hypotheses: Linguistic complexity and length of abstracts are more strongly associated with ChatGPT scores than with REF expert scores.
Abstracts with higher linguistic complexity and lower readability will receive higher scores from ChatGPT compared to expert evaluations.
The relationship between abstract features and scores will vary across subject areas.
kousha2025_stylistic_features.data_description: A dataset of 99,277 journal articles submitted to the UK-wide Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021 assessments. Each article has associated ChatGPT scores, departmental REF scores, and abstracts from which readability indicators were calculated.

filters: articles submitted to the UK-wide Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021

independent_variables.name: readability
independent_variables.definition: Measured using readability indicators such as Flesch Reading Ease, Flesch–Kincaid Grade Level, and Gunning Fog Index.

independent_variables.name: linguistic_complexity
independent_variables.definition: Measured by the complexity of language used in the abstracts.

independent_variables.name: abstract_length
independent_variables.definition: Measured by the number of words in the abstract.

dependent_variables.name: ChatGPT_score
dependent_variables.formula: Scores generated by ChatGPT for the research quality of the article based on its title and abstract.

dependent_variables.name: REF_score
dependent_variables.formula: Average research quality score assigned by expert panels for the department submitting the article.

control_variables.name: subject_area
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in language use and evaluation criteria across disciplines.

control_variables.name: journal_impact_factor
control_variables.rationale: To control for the potential influence of journal prestige on both ChatGPT and expert scores.

statistical_method.specification: Correlation analysis between readability indicators, linguistic complexity, abstract length, and ChatGPT and REF scores.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the strength of associations between abstract features and scores from ChatGPT versus expert evaluations.

key_quantities: correlation between readability and ChatGPT scores
correlation between readability and REF scores

result_claims.metric: correlation between linguistic complexity and ChatGPT scores
correlation between abstract length and ChatGPT scores
correlation between readability and ChatGPT scores
correlation between linguistic complexity and REF scores
correlation between abstract length and REF scores
correlation between readability and REF scores

partial_results.note: Not tested in this study.

conclusion_claims: Linguistic complexity and length of abstracts are more strongly associated with ChatGPT scores than with REF expert scores in many subject areas.
ChatGPT may be more likely than human experts to reward linguistic complexity, with a potential bias towards longer and less readable abstracts in many fields.
limitations: Cause-and-effect relationships between abstract features and scores were not tested.
The use of departmental average scores as a proxy for individual article quality may introduce inaccuracies.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess whether the observed associations are consistent across disciplines.

robustness_checks.method: Replacing LLM scores with citation impact indicators
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess whether stylistic characteristics of abstracts are similarly or differently related to citation impact.

yadav2026_project_synapse.title: Project Synapse: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework with Hybrid Memory for Autonomous Resolution of Last-Mile Delivery Disruptions
yadav2026_project_synapse.research_idea: Project Synapse introduces a hierarchical multi-agent framework with a Hybrid Memory Architecture to autonomously resolve last-mile delivery disruptions, combining strategic task decomposition with specialized execution and context-aware reasoning.
yadav2026_project_synapse.research_question: How can a hierarchical, memory-augmented agentic architecture effectively manage and resolve complex, real-time disruptions in last-mile delivery operations?
hypotheses: A hierarchical multi-agent system with a supervisor agent and specialized worker agents will outperform single-agent systems in resolving complex disruptions.
The integration of a Hybrid Memory Architecture will enhance the system's ability to perform stateful, context-aware, and factually-grounded reasoning.
yadav2026_project_synapse.data_source: User reviews from a major super-app ecosystem
yadav2026_project_synapse.data_description: A benchmark dataset of 30 complex disruption scenarios curated from a qualitative analysis of over 6,000 real-world user reviews.

independent_variables.name: disruption_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of disruption (e.g., traffic congestion, incorrect address, unavailable merchant)

independent_variables.name: agent_configuration
independent_variables.definition: The configuration of the agent system (e.g., hierarchical multi-agent vs. single-agent)

dependent_variables.name: resolution_score
dependent_variables.formula: Overall score assigned by the LLM-as-a-Judge protocol, ranging from 0 to 1

control_variables.name: scenario_complexity
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the inherent complexity of disruption scenarios

control_variables.name: time_to_resolution
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time taken to resolve each scenario

statistical_method.specification: Performance evaluation using an LLM-as-a-Judge protocol with explicit bias mitigation

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of the Synapse system against existing solutions in resolving disruption scenarios

partial_results.note: Results are based on a limited benchmark of 30 scenarios and may not generalize to all real-world disruptions

conclusion_claims: The Synapse system demonstrates strong performance in resolving last-mile delivery disruptions, with an overall average score of 0.73.
The hierarchical, memory-augmented agentic architecture is viable for dynamic, real-world problem-solving in high-stakes operational environments.
limitations: The evaluation is based on a limited benchmark dataset of 30 scenarios, which may not fully represent the diversity of real-world disruptions.
The system's performance in real-time, high-pressure operational environments has not been tested.

robustness_checks.method: LLM-as-a-Judge protocol with explicit bias mitigation
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the evaluation is fair and not biased by the judge's preferences

hao2024_hlm_cite.title: HLM-Cite: Hybrid Language Model Workflow for Text-based Scientific Citation Prediction
hao2024_hlm_cite.venue: 38th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2024)
hao2024_hlm_cite.research_idea: The paper introduces HLM-Cite, a two-stage hybrid language model workflow for predicting core citations in scientific papers. It distinguishes core citations from superficial citations and non-citations, improving citation prediction by combining embedding and generative LLMs.
hao2024_hlm_cite.research_question: How can we accurately predict core citations in scientific papers by distinguishing them from superficial citations and non-citations, especially when dealing with large candidate sets?
hypotheses: Using a hybrid workflow combining embedding and generative LLMs will improve the accuracy of core citation prediction.
The proposed two-stage pipeline can scale to large candidate sets (up to 100K papers) while maintaining high precision.
hao2024_hlm_cite.data_description: The dataset includes 12M papers across 19 scientific fields. The data is used to analyze the statistical differences between core and superficial citations and to evaluate the performance of HLM-Cite.

independent_variables.name: candidate_set_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of papers in the candidate set from which citations are predicted.

independent_variables.name: textual_similarity
independent_variables.definition: The similarity between the query paper and candidate papers based on their textual content.

dependent_variables.name: core_citation_prediction
dependent_variables.formula: Whether a candidate paper is predicted as a core citation.

control_variables.name: superficial_citations
control_variables.rationale: To account for the presence of less relevant citations that may distract from core citations.

control_variables.name: non_citations
control_variables.rationale: To ensure the model distinguishes between citations and non-citations.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision) to evaluate the effectiveness of HLM-Cite.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations are derived from the Microsoft Academic Graph, where each paper cites a subset of previous papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To distinguish between different types of citations and improve the accuracy of citation prediction.

result_claims.significance: Thousands of times more than existing works

partial_results.note: The paper provides statistical evidence that core citations are more closely related to the query paper in terms of keyword overlap and frequency of mentions.

conclusion_claims: HLM-Cite improves citation prediction by distinguishing core citations from superficial citations and non-citations.
The hybrid workflow enables the model to scale to large candidate sets (up to 100K papers) while maintaining high precision.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention limitations in the provided text.
The evaluation is based on a single dataset (Microsoft Academic Graph), which may limit generalizability.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the model's performance is consistent across different scientific fields.

wu2026_geographical_biases.title: Persistent geographical biases in global scientific collaboration and citations
wu2026_geographical_biases.research_idea: This paper investigates how geographic distance and national structures influence global scientific collaboration and citation patterns, revealing persistent biases in knowledge flows.
wu2026_geographical_biases.research_question: How do geographic distance and country-level factors shape international scientific collaboration and citation?
hypotheses: Geographic distance has a stronger effect on collaboration than on citations.
National borders and country-specific biases influence scientific collaboration and citation patterns.
International mobility increases collaboration but has limited effects on citation flows.
wu2026_geographical_biases.data_description: A large-scale bibliometric dataset covering 39.35 million publications from 2000 to 2022, spanning 95 countries and 3,794 cities, including 2,444,439 city-level collaboration pairs and 14,754,699 city-level citation pairs.

independent_variables.name: geographic_distance
independent_variables.definition: The physical distance between cities or countries.

independent_variables.name: country
independent_variables.definition: The country of origin of the publication or researcher.

independent_variables.name: international_mobility
independent_variables.definition: Whether a researcher has moved internationally.

dependent_variables.name: collaboration_probability
dependent_variables.formula: The probability of collaboration between two cities or countries.

dependent_variables.name: citation_probability
dependent_variables.formula: The probability of citation between two cities or countries.

control_variables.name: publication_volume
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the number of publications on collaboration and citation probabilities.

control_variables.name: collaboration_preferences
control_variables.rationale: To account for country-specific collaboration tendencies.

control_variables.name: latent_collaboration_tendency
control_variables.rationale: To account for unobserved factors influencing collaboration, such as scientific quality and reputation.

statistical_method.specification: A Bayesian modeling framework is used to estimate the influence of geographic distance on cross-country collaboration and citation patterns.

network_construction.construction_method: Collaboration and citation networks are constructed based on city-level interactions, with directed and weighted edges representing the strength and direction of interactions.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the influence of geographic distance on collaboration and citation within and between countries.

partial_results.note: The study notes that China is systematically undercited despite being a favored collaboration partner.

conclusion_claims: Geographic distance has a stronger effect on collaboration than on citations, and this effect has intensified over time.
Persistent spatial and country biases in global science suggest structural or institutional barriers to equitable participation and recognition.
limitations: The study relies on bibliometric data, which may not capture all aspects of scientific interaction.
The analysis is limited to city-level data, which may not fully represent individual-level dynamics.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the influence of geographic distance on collaboration and citation patterns.
To study the effect of international mobility on researchers' collaboration and citation networks.

_source.title: Persistent geographical biases in global scientific collaboration and citations

bank2026_data_driven_analysis.title: A Data-Driven Analysis for Engineering Conferences: The Institute of Industrial and Systems Engineering (IISE) Annual Conference Proceedings (2002–2025)
bank2026_data_driven_analysis.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 IISE Annual Conference
bank2026_data_driven_analysis.research_idea: This paper presents a computational analysis of IISE proceedings from 2002 to 2025, combining ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets to map the intellectual evolution of Industrial and Systems Engineering (ISE) through thematic, citation, and co-authorship analyses.
bank2026_data_driven_analysis.research_question: What is the intellectual evolution of the Industrial and Systems Engineering (ISE) field as reflected in the IISE Annual Conference proceedings from 2002 to 2025?
hypotheses: The integration of ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets will provide a more comprehensive and accurate analysis of ISE research trends.
LLM-based classification will effectively identify dominant, emerging, and receding research topics in ISE.
Network analysis of citations and co-authorship will reveal influential papers, authors, and collaboration patterns.
bank2026_data_driven_analysis.data_description: The study uses 9,350 titles from ProQuest for thematic analysis and 8,958 titles from Google Scholar for citation analysis, covering the IISE Annual Conference proceedings from 2002 to 2025.

filters: Conference proceedings from IISE Annual Conference

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the paper was published, used to track temporal trends in research topics and collaboration patterns.

independent_variables.name: research_domain
independent_variables.definition: The primary research domain of the paper (e.g., Data Analytics and Information Systems, Manufacturing and Design), identified through LLM-based classification.

independent_variables.name: research_sub_area
independent_variables.definition: The sub-area within the research domain (e.g., Machine Learning, Computer Vision), identified through LLM-based classification.

dependent_variables.name: prevalence_of_domain
dependent_variables.formula: Percentage of papers in a given year that belong to a specific research domain or sub-area.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by a paper, used to identify influential papers and knowledge diffusion patterns.

control_variables.name: author_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in author productivity when analyzing co-authorship networks and collaboration patterns.

control_variables.name: publication_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in publication quality when analyzing citation patterns and influence.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and network analysis to map thematic evolution, identify influential papers and authors, and analyze collaboration patterns.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks were constructed using Google Scholar data, with edges representing citations between papers. Co-authorship networks were constructed using author data from both ProQuest and Google Scholar, with edges representing co-authorship relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To identify and compare research domains that are consistently prominent, gaining momentum, or losing relevance over time.

key_quantities: Prevalence of research domains and sub-areas over time

result_claims.value: Dominant, emerging, and receding research topics were identified
Influential papers and authors were identified

partial_results.note: The accuracy of LLM-based classification was not explicitly reported, but it was stated that open-source LLMs combined with semantic search can extract domain-specific knowledge with over 90% accuracy.

conclusion_claims: The study provides a comprehensive cartography of the ISE field's intellectual history, identifying dominant, emerging, and receding research topics.
The integration of ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets, along with LLM-based classification and network analysis, offers valuable insights into knowledge diffusion and community structure in ISE.
limitations: The study relies on automated classification methods, which may introduce errors in identifying research domains and sub-areas.
The citation data from Google Scholar may have quality and reproducibility issues, which could affect the accuracy of impact analysis.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of findings by validating results across ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets.

peng2026_structural_diversity.title: Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation
peng2026_structural_diversity.research_idea: The paper introduces Structural Diversity (SD) as a new metric to measure the extent to which a team bridges distinct knowledge communities in its prior collaboration network. It demonstrates that SD is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research, outperforming traditional metrics like team size and team freshness.
peng2026_structural_diversity.research_question: What organizational structure fosters disruptive scientific innovation, and how does Structural Diversity (SD) influence this process?
hypotheses: Structural Diversity (SD) is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research.
SD positively interacts with team size, mitigating the 'curse of scale' in large teams.
SD drives disruptive innovation by fostering disciplinary integration.
peng2026_structural_diversity.data_description: A century-scale dataset of 260 million scientific publications spanning 19 disciplines from 1900 to 2025.

independent_variables.name: Structural Diversity (SD)
independent_variables.definition: The weighted number of connected components (CCs) in a team's historical co-authorship network, capturing the extent to which a team bridges distinct knowledge communities.

independent_variables.name: Team Size
independent_variables.definition: The number of authors in a team, log-transformed to account for skewed distribution.

independent_variables.name: Team Freshness
independent_variables.definition: The proportion of previously unconnected collaborators in a team.

independent_variables.name: Edge Density
independent_variables.definition: The density of connections among team members in their prior collaboration network.

dependent_variables.name: CD Index
dependent_variables.formula: A field-normalized index measuring the disruptive impact of a scientific paper.

control_variables.name: Title Word Count
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in paper length and content.

control_variables.name: Title Readability Score
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the accessibility of paper titles.

control_variables.name: Title Promotional Words (%)
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the promotional language used in paper titles.

control_variables.name: Author Career Age
control_variables.rationale: To account for the career stage of authors and its non-linear effect on innovation.

control_variables.name: Author Institution h-index
control_variables.rationale: To account for the prestige of the author's institution.

control_variables.name: Author No. Publications (log)
control_variables.rationale: To account for the author's prior publication record.

statistical_method.specification: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression models with fixed effects for discipline and publication year.

network_construction.construction_method: Based on the historical co-authorship network of team members prior to a focal publication.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the disruptive innovation outcomes of teams with varying levels of structural diversity.

key_quantities: coefficient of SD on CD Index
interaction effect between SD and team size

result_claims.metric: Interaction between SD and Team Size

partial_results.note: The paper does not report a direct coefficient for edge density in the regression models.

conclusion_claims: Structural Diversity (SD) is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research, outperforming traditional metrics like team size and team freshness.
SD positively interacts with team size, transforming the 'curse of scale' into a resource for creative synthesis in large teams.
limitations: The study relies on a single dataset (OpenAlex), which may not capture all aspects of scientific collaboration.
The causal relationship between SD and disruptive innovation is inferred through quasi-natural experiments and causal inference methods, which may not fully eliminate all confounding factors.

robustness_checks.rationale: To account for potential confounding variables and ensure that the observed effects of SD are not due to other factors.

robustness_checks.method: Quasi-natural experiment based on a U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) policy change in 2012
robustness_checks.rationale: To provide additional evidence for the causal relationship between SD and disruptive innovation.

luo2023_prototype_based_interpretability.title: Prototype-Based Interpretability for Legal Citation Prediction
luo2023_prototype_based_interpretability.research_idea: The paper introduces a prototype-based architecture for legal citation prediction (LCP) to enhance model interpretability, aligning with the decision-making process of lawyers by incorporating precedents and legislative provisions.
luo2023_prototype_based_interpretability.research_question: How can a prototype-based model improve the interpretability of legal citation prediction while maintaining strong performance?
hypotheses: A prototype-based model will achieve strong performance in legal citation prediction.
Incorporating prototypes of precedents and provisions will improve the interpretability of the model's decisions.

independent_variables.name: precedents
independent_variables.definition: Prior legal cases or literature used as reference points for citation prediction

independent_variables.name: provisions
independent_variables.definition: Legislative provisions or statutes used as reference points for citation prediction

dependent_variables.name: citation_prediction
dependent_variables.formula: Predicted legal citations based on input text and prototypes

control_variables.name: case_text
control_variables.rationale: The factual description of the case is used as input for citation prediction

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a prototype-based architecture with a modified loss function to enhance interpretability. It compares performance with a baseline fine-tuning approach.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance and interpretability of the prototype-based model against a standard fine-tuning approach.

partial_results.note: Performance is compared before and after incorporating legal expert feedback, but specific metrics are not provided in the text.

conclusion_claims: The prototype-based architecture successfully enhances interpretability in legal citation prediction while maintaining strong performance.
Incorporating legal expert feedback improves the practical relevance and accuracy of citation predictions.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention the datasets used for training and evaluation.
Specific performance metrics are not provided in the text.

robustness_checks.rationale: To analyze the learned latent space and ensure the model's decisions are grounded in relevant legal references.

zheng2026_citerag.title: What Should I Cite? A RAG Benchmark for Academic Citation Prediction
zheng2026_citerag.venue: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW ’26)
zheng2026_citerag.research_idea: The paper introduces CiteRAG, the first comprehensive retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) benchmark for academic citation prediction. It proposes a multi-level hybrid RAG approach to citation prediction, fine-tuning embedding models with contrastive learning and specialized generation models. The benchmark includes two citation prediction tasks with different granularities and a large-scale hierarchical corpus of 554k papers.
zheng2026_citerag.research_question: How can a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework be designed and evaluated to effectively predict academic citations at both coarse-grained and fine-grained levels?
hypotheses: A multi-level hybrid RAG approach will outperform single-level methods in citation prediction.
Fine-grained position-specific citation prediction will require more context-aware models than coarse-grained list-specific prediction.
zheng2026_citerag.data_description: A hierarchical corpus of 554,719 academic papers spanning multiple disciplines. The dataset includes 7,267 instances for Task 1 (coarse-grained list-specific citation prediction) and 8,541 instances for Task 2 (fine-grained position-specific citation prediction).

independent_variables.name: Task Granularity
independent_variables.definition: Whether the citation prediction task is coarse-grained (list-specific) or fine-grained (position-specific).

independent_variables.name: Retrieval Method
independent_variables.definition: The type of retrieval method used, such as single-level or multi-level hybrid RAG.

dependent_variables.name: Citation Prediction Accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Measured as the proportion of correctly predicted citations out of total citations.

dependent_variables.name: Retrieval Precision
dependent_variables.formula: Measured as the proportion of relevant retrieved papers out of total retrieved papers.

control_variables.name: Model Type
control_variables.rationale: To ensure fair comparison between different models, including open-source, closed-source, and fine-tuned models.

control_variables.name: Corpus Size
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of the size of the candidate pool on retrieval effectiveness.

statistical_method.specification: The paper conducts extensive experiments across state-of-the-art language models and evaluates the performance of the proposed RAG framework using descriptive metrics such as accuracy and precision.

network_construction.construction_method: The citation network is constructed based on the citation relationships between papers in the corpus.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To evaluate the effectiveness of the RAG framework at different levels of granularity in citation prediction.

result_claims.value: Improved with the multi-level hybrid RAG approach
Improved with the multi-level hybrid RAG approach

partial_results.note: The paper mentions the evaluation of hallucination and diversity in RAG systems but does not provide detailed results.

conclusion_claims: The proposed multi-level hybrid RAG approach is effective for academic citation prediction at both coarse-grained and fine-grained levels.
The CiteRAG benchmark provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for citation prediction and serves as a methodological template for other scientific domains.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed results for hallucination and diversity evaluation.
The time window and specific disciplines covered in the dataset are not clearly specified.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with open-source and closed-source models
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the proposed RAG framework across different model types.

robustness_checks.method: Evaluation of retrieval and generation components separately
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the contribution of each component to the overall performance of the RAG framework.

lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.research_idea: The paper proposes a Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) that combines multi-agent feature extraction with robust graph representation learning to address the challenges of predicting citations for newborn papers, particularly in the presence of long-tailed distributions and implicit factors.
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.research_question: How can we design bias-aware models that deliver stable predictions on low-citation papers while revealing the underlying drivers of scientific impact?
hypotheses: Prior citation predictors overfit explicit signals, leading to degradation on long-tail papers and under distribution shifts.
A multi-source informed graph learning framework can enable robust prediction even without early citation information.
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.data_description: Two large-scale academic datasets containing metadata about papers, authors, institutions, venues, and related descriptors. The datasets are used to evaluate the proposed framework.

independent_variables.name: reproducibility
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how reproducible a paper's findings are, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_network
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the collaboration structure of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: text_quality
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the quality of the paper's text, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: topic_hotness
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how hot or trending the paper's topic is, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: venue_prestige
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the prestige of the venue where the paper was published, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: author_reputation
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the reputation of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: The number of citations a paper receives over time.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: number_of_authors
control_variables.rationale: To account for the potential influence of collaboration size on citation count.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a multi-agent × graph co-learning module to derive fine-grained, interpretable signals and fuses them with heterogeneous-network embeddings. It also incorporates a two-stage forward process, GroupDRO, and a regularization head for robust learning.

network_construction.construction_method: Heterogeneous academic graphs are constructed using metadata and external resources, with edges representing citations between papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To evaluate the performance of the model on different citation groups and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.

partial_results.metric: Model performance on lowly cited papers
partial_results.note: The paper does not provide detailed results on the performance of the model on lowly cited papers beyond the overall metrics.

conclusion_claims: The proposed Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) achieves significant improvements in citation prediction for newborn papers.
The framework addresses the research gaps of insufficient modeling of implicit factors and a lack of bias-aware learning, leading to more robust and accurate predictions.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information on the specific datasets used beyond their names.
The paper does not provide detailed information on the implementation of the multi-agent feature extraction module.

robustness_checks.rationale: To optimize worst-case group risk across environments and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.
To perform what-if analyses on controllable factors under monotonicity and smoothness constraints.

holm2021_longitudinal_citation_predictio.title: Longitudinal Citation Prediction using Temporal Graph Neural Networks
holm2021_longitudinal_citation_predictio.research_idea: The paper introduces the task of sequence citation prediction, where the goal is to predict the trajectory of citation counts over time for scholarly works. It proposes a model that combines graph convolutional networks and LSTMs to exploit both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks.
holm2021_longitudinal_citation_predictio.research_question: Can leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks improve the accuracy of predicting citation counts over time?
hypotheses: Modeling a paper's citation network is useful for predicting citation counts over time.
Leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks will significantly improve the performance of predicting citation counts over time.
holm2021_longitudinal_citation_predictio.data_description: The dataset contains a dynamic citation network spanning over 42 years, with an updated graph for each year. It includes information about papers, their citations, authors, venues, and abstracts.

independent_variables.name: topological_information
independent_variables.definition: Features derived from the structure of the citation network, such as node degrees and graph embeddings.

independent_variables.name: temporal_information
independent_variables.definition: Features derived from the temporal evolution of the citation network, such as citation count trajectories over time.

independent_variables.name: paper_metadata
independent_variables.definition: Information about the paper's authors, venue, and abstracts.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count_trajectory
dependent_variables.formula: The sequence of citation counts for a paper over time.

control_variables.name: paper_metadata
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of paper characteristics such as authors, venues, and abstracts on citation counts.

statistical_method.specification: An encoder-decoder model combining graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks to model both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks.

network_construction.construction_method: A dynamic citation network is constructed from the Semantic Scholar dataset, with an updated graph for each year. Each node represents a paper, and edges represent citations between papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To test the importance of topological and temporal information in predicting citation counts over time.

partial_results.note: The exact performance metrics are not provided in the text.

conclusion_claims: Leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks greatly increases the performance of predicting citation counts over time.
The proposed encoder-decoder model combining GCNs and LSTMs is effective for sequence citation prediction.
limitations: The paper does not provide specific performance metrics for the proposed model.
The paper does not discuss potential limitations of the model or the data.

robustness_checks.rationale: To test the importance of topological and temporal information in the model.

petersen2024_disruption_index.title: The disruption index suffers from citation inflation and is confounded by shifts in scholarly citation practice
petersen2024_disruption_index.research_idea: The disruption index (CD) is shown to be systematically decreasing over time due to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices, not due to a decline in innovation. This calls into question the validity of using CD to measure innovation trends.
petersen2024_disruption_index.research_question: Is the disruption index (CD) a reliable measure of innovation trends, or is it confounded by secular growth and citation practices?
hypotheses: The disruption index (CD) decreases over time due to citation inflation (increasing reference list lengths).
The disruption index (CD) is confounded by shifts in scholarly citation practices, such as self-citation.
petersen2024_disruption_index.data_source: SciSciNet database, PNAS publications (2011-2015), and prior studies (e.g., Wu et al., Park et al.)
petersen2024_disruption_index.data_description: The study uses 7.8 million articles from 1995-2015 from the SciSciNet database and a quasi-experimental comparison of PNAS and PNAS Plus articles from 2011-2015.

independent_variables.name: time
independent_variables.definition: Year of publication

independent_variables.name: team_size
independent_variables.definition: Number of coauthors on a paper

independent_variables.name: reference_list_length
independent_variables.definition: Number of references cited in a paper

dependent_variables.name: disruption_index_CD
dependent_variables.formula: CDp = (Ni - Nj) / (Ni + Nj + Nk), where Ni, Nj, Nk are subsets of citing nodes

control_variables.name: citation_window
control_variables.rationale: To account for right-censoring bias in citation data

control_variables.name: data_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for missing citations in early years of the dataset

statistical_method.specification: Multivariable regression analysis of 7.8 million articles, including controls for data quality and measurement biases.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are built using forward and backward citations within a 5-year citation window.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To conduct a quasi-experiment comparing articles with different reference list lengths but otherwise similar characteristics.

key_quantities: effect of team size on CD

result_claims.metric: relationship between CD and team size
result_claims.value: shifts from negative to positive for team size ≥8

result_claims.metric: effect size of temporal and team-size trends

partial_results.note: The observed decline is attributed to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices, not to a decline in innovation.

conclusion_claims: The disruption index (CD) is susceptible to measurement error due to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices.
The relationship between CD and team size is not consistently negative; it shifts to positive for larger teams.
limitations: The study relies on observational data and quasi-experimental designs, which may not fully account for all confounding variables.
The results are based on a specific citation window and may not generalize to other time periods or citation practices.

robustness_checks.method: quasi-experimental comparison of PNAS and PNAS Plus articles
robustness_checks.rationale: To isolate the effect of reference list length on CD while holding other variables constant.
To validate the mathematical deduction of the relationship between CD and reference list length.

petersen2023_citation_inflation.title: The disruption index is biased by citation inflation
petersen2023_citation_inflation.research_idea: The disruption index (CD) is systematically biased by citation inflation, a phenomenon driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which undermines its utility for cross-temporal analysis of scientific and technological disruption.
petersen2023_citation_inflation.research_question: Is the observed decline in the disruption index over time a true reflection of reduced innovation or an artifact of citation inflation?
hypotheses: The disruption index (CD) is biased by citation inflation, leading to a systematic decline over time.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase the density of citation networks and confound measures of disruption.
petersen2023_citation_inflation.data_source: Microsoft Academic Graph (MAG) citation network, Clarivate Analytics Web of Science
petersen2023_citation_inflation.data_description: The MAG dataset includes millions of research publications with metadata on citations and reference lists. The Web of Science dataset provides citation counts and growth rates of scientific production.

independent_variables.name: reference_list_length
independent_variables.definition: The number of references cited in a publication, denoted by rp = |{r}p|.

independent_variables.name: citation_volume
independent_variables.definition: The total number of citations produced per year, denoted by C(t) ≈ n(t)r(t).

independent_variables.name: self_citations
independent_variables.definition: The number of self-citations in a publication's reference list, which increases triadic closure in citation networks.

dependent_variables.name: disruption_index
dependent_variables.formula: CDp = (Ni - Nj) / (Ni + Nj + Nk), where Ni is the number of citing nodes that cite p but not {r}p, Nj is the number of citing nodes that cite both p and {r}p, and Nk is the number of citing nodes that cite {r}p but not p.

control_variables.name: team_size
control_variables.rationale: Team size is a proxy for the production effort associated with a research output and is correlated with reference list length.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: Publication year is controlled to account for time-dependent trends in citation practices and scientific production.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses deductive analysis, empirical analysis of the MAG citation network, and computational modeling of synthetic citation networks to demonstrate the effects of citation inflation on the disruption index.

network_construction.construction_method: The citation network is constructed using the subgraph Gp = {r}p ∪ p ∪ {c}p, where {r}p is the set of references cited by p, and {c}p is the set of nodes citing p or any member of {r}p.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare changes in reference list lengths and citation practices over time.

result_claims.value: grows exponentially with an annual rate of 0.051

partial_results.note: The decline in the disruption index is attributed to citation inflation rather than a true decline in innovation.

conclusion_claims: The disruption index is systematically biased by citation inflation, making it unsuitable for cross-temporal analysis of scientific and technological disruption.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase the density of citation networks and confound measures of disruption.
limitations: The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and computational modeling rather than controlled experiments.
The paper does not provide a fully validated alternative to the disruption index for cross-temporal analysis.

robustness_checks.method: computational modeling of synthetic citation networks
robustness_checks.rationale: To isolate the effects of citation inflation and demonstrate that the disruption index converges to 0 in the absence of other confounding factors.

kozlowski2023_uncited_articles.title: Uncited articles and their effect on the concentration of citations
kozlowski2023_uncited_articles.research_idea: This paper investigates the evolution of citation concentration over time, emphasizing the role of uncited articles in explaining variations in citation distribution. It shows that the inclusion of uncited articles leads to a downward trend in citation concentration.
kozlowski2023_uncited_articles.research_question: Are citations becoming more concentrated in a small number of articles, or are they becoming more decentralized due to recent geopolitical and technical changes in science?
hypotheses: Citation concentration is decreasing over time due to a decline in uncited articles.
The reduction in uncited articles is driven by the growing significance of Asia and Europe.
kozlowski2023_uncited_articles.data_description: Publication metadata from the WoS Core Collection, covering the period from 1980 to 2020. Two datasets were used: one restricted to journals indexed throughout the entire period and one including all articles and references in the complete WoS collection.

filters: journals indexed throughout the entire period
all articles in the complete WoS collection

independent_variables.name: uncited_articles
independent_variables.definition: The number of articles that have not received any citations

independent_variables.name: region
independent_variables.definition: Geographic region of the article's publication (e.g., Asia, Europe)

independent_variables.name: citation_window
independent_variables.definition: The time period over which citations are counted

independent_variables.name: self_citations
independent_variables.definition: The number of citations to an article from the same author or institution

dependent_variables.name: citation_concentration
dependent_variables.formula: Measured using inequality indices such as the Gini coefficient or the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), which quantify the degree of concentration in citation distribution

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: Older articles have more time to accumulate citations

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: Citation practices vary significantly across disciplines

control_variables.name: reference_list_length
control_variables.rationale: Longer reference lists may influence citation patterns

control_variables.name: database_coverage
control_variables.rationale: The expansion of bibliometric databases affects the visibility and citation potential of articles

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and inequality indices (e.g., Gini coefficient, HHI) to analyze the evolution of citation concentration over time. It also compares results from citation-based and reference-based approaches.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are constructed based on the citation relationships between articles in the WoS dataset.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze the impact of uncited articles on the overall citation concentration and to compare citation patterns across regions.

partial_results.note: The observed trend is sensitive to the inclusion of uncited articles and the choice of citation window.

conclusion_claims: Citation concentration is decreasing over time, primarily due to a decline in the number of uncited articles.
The reduction in uncited articles is largely driven by the increasing significance of Asia and Europe in the global scientific landscape.
limitations: The study is limited to the Web of Science database, which may not fully represent all scientific publications, especially in the social sciences and humanities.
The analysis does not account for the quality or impact of uncited articles, which may still contribute to scientific progress in ways not captured by citation counts.

robustness_checks.method: comparison of citation-based and reference-based approaches
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the sensitivity of the results to different methodological choices in measuring citation concentration
To evaluate the impact of time normalization on the observed trends in citation concentration

alSinani2025_aiGeneratedPapers.title: From Content Creation to Citation Inflation: A GenAI Case Study
alSinani2025_aiGeneratedPapers.research_idea: This paper investigates the presence and impact of AI-generated academic papers on preprint repositories, particularly ResearchGate, and their role in citation manipulation. It demonstrates how such papers can bypass platform checks and contribute to inflating citation metrics like the H-index and i10-index.
alSinani2025_aiGeneratedPapers.research_question: Are there fully AI-generated papers on ResearchGate, particularly in the area of GenAI-enhanced Cybersecurity, and if so, how are they being used to manipulate academic metrics?
hypotheses: AI-generated papers can be uploaded to ResearchGate and remain publicly accessible.
These papers can contribute to inflating citation metrics such as the H-index and i10-index.
alSinani2025_aiGeneratedPapers.data_description: The data includes a cluster of suspicious papers and associated ResearchGate profiles, as well as a controlled experiment involving the generation and upload of a fake academic paper.

filters: Papers with mutually reinforcing citation patterns

independent_variables.name: AI-generated_content
independent_variables.definition: Whether the paper is AI-generated or not

independent_variables.name: citation_pattern
independent_variables.definition: Type of citation pattern (e.g., mutually reinforcing, random, normal)

independent_variables.name: author_identity
independent_variables.definition: Verifiability of author identities (e.g., unverifiable, verified)

dependent_variables.name: citation_inflation
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by AI-generated papers

dependent_variables.name: H-index_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Change in H-index of authors citing AI-generated papers

dependent_variables.name: i10-index_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Change in i10-index of authors citing AI-generated papers

control_variables.name: publication_date
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior

control_variables.name: platform_usage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior across platforms

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for field-specific citation practices

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive analysis of citation patterns and paper characteristics

network_construction.construction_method: Manual identification of citation links between AI-generated papers and other papers

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation patterns and academic impact between AI-generated and legitimate papers

partial_results.note: Limited to manual checks and may not be comprehensive

conclusion_claims: AI-generated papers can bypass platform checks and remain publicly accessible on ResearchGate.
These papers contribute to inflating citation metrics such as the H-index and i10-index.
limitations: The study is limited to a single platform (ResearchGate) and a specific research area (GenAI-enhanced Cybersecurity).
The controlled experiment involved a single fake paper and may not generalize to all AI-generated papers.

robustness_checks.rationale: To confirm the unverifiability of author identities in AI-generated papers
To assess the impact of AI-generated papers on citation metrics

banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.title: Billions at Stake: How Self-Citation Adjusted Metrics Can Transform Equitable Research Funding
banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.research_idea: The paper introduces the Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI), a new metric that recalibrates citation counts by accounting for discipline-specific self-citation patterns, aiming to provide a more equitable assessment of research impact.
banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.research_question: How can citation metrics be adjusted to account for self-citation patterns in order to provide a more accurate and equitable assessment of research impact?
hypotheses: Excessive self-citation inflates traditional citation metrics by 10-20%.
Adjusting for self-citation patterns reduces the gender citation gap by approximately 8.5%.
banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.data_source: Not explicitly mentioned, but likely from open-access bibliometric databases.
banerjee2025_self_citation_adjusted.data_description: Analysis of 5,000 researcher profiles across diverse disciplines.

independent_variables.name: self_citation_rate
independent_variables.definition: The proportion of citations to a researcher's work that are self-citations.

dependent_variables.name: adjusted_citation_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated using the Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI) algorithm.

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: Self-citation rates vary significantly across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: Comprehensive analysis of citation patterns and self-citation rates across 5,000 researcher profiles.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze gender disparities in self-citation rates and their impact on citation metrics.

partial_results.note: The compounding effect of self-citation over time is not fully quantified in the paper.

conclusion_claims: The Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI) provides a more accurate and equitable assessment of research impact by accounting for self-citation patterns.
Adjusting for self-citation can reduce the gender citation gap and improve the fairness of research funding allocation.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention the specific databases or datasets used for the analysis.
The long-term impact of self-citation on citation metrics is not fully quantified.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the SCAI metric is effective across different fields with varying self-citation rates.

wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.title: Persistent geographical biases in global scientific collaboration and citations
wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.research_idea: This paper investigates how geographic distance and national structures influence global scientific collaboration and citation patterns, revealing persistent biases in knowledge flows.
wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.research_question: How do geographic distance and country-level factors shape international scientific collaboration and citation?
hypotheses: Geographic distance has a stronger effect on collaboration than on citations.
National borders and country-specific biases influence scientific collaboration and citation patterns.
International mobility increases collaboration but has limited effects on citation flows.
wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.data_description: A large-scale bibliometric dataset covering 39.35 million publications from 2000 to 2022, spanning 95 countries and 3,794 cities, including 2,444,439 city-level collaboration pairs and 14,754,699 city-level citation pairs.

independent_variables.name: geographic_distance
independent_variables.definition: The physical distance between cities or countries.

independent_variables.name: country
independent_variables.definition: The country of origin of the publication or researcher.

independent_variables.name: international_mobility
independent_variables.definition: Whether a researcher has moved internationally.

dependent_variables.name: collaboration_probability
dependent_variables.formula: The probability of collaboration between two cities or countries.

dependent_variables.name: citation_probability
dependent_variables.formula: The probability of citation between two cities or countries.

control_variables.name: publication_volume
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the number of publications on collaboration and citation probabilities.

control_variables.name: collaboration_preferences
control_variables.rationale: To account for country-specific preferences in collaboration.

control_variables.name: latent_collaboration_tendency
control_variables.rationale: To account for unobserved factors influencing collaboration, such as scientific quality and reputation.

statistical_method.specification: A Bayesian modeling framework is used to estimate the influence of geographic distance on cross-country collaboration and citation patterns.

network_construction.construction_method: Collaboration and citation networks are constructed based on city-level interactions between 2000 and 2022.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the influence of geographic distance on collaboration and citation within and between countries.

partial_results.note: The study notes that China is systematically undercited despite being a favored collaboration partner.

conclusion_claims: Geographic distance has a stronger effect on collaboration than on citations, and this effect has intensified over time.
Persistent spatial and country biases in global science suggest structural or institutional barriers to equitable participation and recognition.
limitations: The study focuses on city-level interactions and may not capture finer-grained or individual-level effects.
The analysis is based on a single dataset (OpenAlex), which may have coverage or representation biases.

robustness_checks.rationale: To study the effect of international mobility on researchers' collaboration and citation patterns.

arXiv_2503.02216v1.title: Hunting for Room Temperature Superconductors
arXiv_2503.02216v1.research_idea: The paper reviews the historical and current efforts in the search for room temperature superconductors (RTS), highlighting the challenges, false claims, and potential pathways for discovery.
