result_claims.metric: relationship between structural role and future ℎ-index

partial_results.note: Textual features generated from the author's published papers do not provide much information.

conclusion_claims: The structural role of an author in the co-authorship network is empirically related to their future ℎ-index.
Graph neural networks outperform traditional machine learning methods in predicting the ℎ-index.
limitations: The study is limited to the field of computer science.
The predictive power of textual features is limited.

robustness_checks.method: comparison with standard machine learning techniques
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed GNN approach.

_source.title: Can Author Collaboration Reveal Impact? The Case of h-index

Scientometrics_2106.10700v1.title: On predicting research grants productivity
Scientometrics_2106.10700v1.research_idea: The study investigates whether bibliometric features can predict the success of research grants, focusing on the academic history of researchers and the role of publication and institutional factors.
Scientometrics_2106.10700v1.research_question: Can bibliometric features extracted from researchers, institutions, and publications predict the productivity of research grants?
hypotheses: Research subject and publication history are significant predictors of grant productivity.
Institution-based features, when combined with other features, contribute to predicting grant success.
Machine learning models can outperform text-based attributes in predicting grant productivity.
Scientometrics_2106.10700v1.data_source: São Paulo Research Foundation (FAPESP) dataset, retrieved from Biblioteca Virtual website
Scientometrics_2106.10700v1.data_description: The dataset includes metadata on research grants, including the number of publications derived from the grant, titles, abstracts, and information about PIs and institutions. The dataset contains approximately 31,000 regular grants with a duration of 23-24 months, starting before 2016.

filters: grants with at least 3 years to produce at least one publication

independent_variables.name: research_subject
independent_variables.definition: The topic or field of the research grant.

independent_variables.name: publication_history
independent_variables.definition: The number of publications and citations yielded by the researcher’s previous grants.

independent_variables.name: institution_relevance
independent_variables.definition: The relevance or prestige of the PI’s institution.

independent_variables.name: diversity_of_subfields
independent_variables.definition: The diversity of research topics or subfields studied by the researcher.

dependent_variables.name: grant_productivity
dependent_variables.formula: Whether the grant resulted in at least one scientific publication.

control_variables.name: grant_duration
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time available to produce publications.

control_variables.name: research_area
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in publication patterns across fields.

statistical_method.specification: Binary classification using machine learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP).

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable, as the study does not involve network analysis.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics of grants that resulted in at least one publication versus those that did not.

result_claims.value: research subject and publication history were the most effective predictors
SVM and MLP yielded the highest accuracy rates

partial_results.note: Typical prediction accuracy was not very high, though higher than approaches based on textual features alone.

conclusion_claims: Research subject and publication history are significant predictors of grant productivity.
Institution-based features contribute to predicting grant success when combined with other features.
Machine learning models outperformed text-based attributes in predicting grant productivity, but the overall accuracy was not very high.
limitations: The evaluated features were not highly discriminative.
The study focused on a specific set of bibliometric features, which may not capture all relevant factors influencing grant success.
The dataset is limited to grants from FAPESP in Brazil, which may limit generalizability.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess whether combining different features improves prediction accuracy.
To ensure the robustness of the machine learning models across different subsets of the data.

_source.title: On predicting research grants productivity

Scientometrics_2206.11470v2.title: Higher-order rich-club phenomenon in collaborative research grant networks
Scientometrics_2206.11470v2.research_idea: The paper investigates higher-order rich-club phenomena in networks of collaborative research grants among institutions and their associations with research impact. It uses a bipartite network representation to analyze the structure and productivity of these collaborations.
Scientometrics_2206.11470v2.research_question: Do institutions participating in many collaborative grants gain advantages in their per-dollar research impact when they densely collaborate with each other (i.e., they form a rich club) in research grants?
hypotheses: Institutions that form rich clubs in collaborative grant networks will have higher per-dollar research impact compared to control groups.
Collaborative grants within rich clubs will be more productive in a per-dollar sense than those outside of rich clubs.
Scientometrics_2206.11470v2.data_description: The study uses publicly available data from the National Science Foundation (NSF) in the US, covering collaborative grants and single-institution grants awarded between January 1, 2000, and December 31, 2020. The Web of Science Core Collection is used to quantify research outputs, including papers published between 2000 and 2020 with acknowledgments of NSF funding.

filters: Collaborative grants with at least two awards (institutions)
Papers with at least five citations or acknowledgments
Institutions categorized as universities, colleges, or schools

independent_variables.name: rich_club_participation
independent_variables.definition: Whether an institution is part of a rich club in the collaborative grant network.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of institutions involved in a collaborative grant.

independent_variables.name: grant_amount
independent_variables.definition: The monetary amount of a grant awarded to an institution.

dependent_variables.name: per_dollar_productivity
dependent_variables.formula: Number of published papers per dollar of grant funding.

dependent_variables.name: per_dollar_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations per dollar of grant funding.

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in research output and impact based on the type of institution.

control_variables.name: grant_duration
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time available for research output and impact.

control_variables.name: research_discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices and research output across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses bipartite network analysis to identify rich clubs and their properties. It compares the productivity and impact of grants within and outside of rich clubs.

network_construction.construction_method: A bipartite network is constructed with institutions and collaborative grants as nodes. Edges represent the participation of an institution in a grant. The network is analyzed to identify rich clubs based on the number of collaborations and the density of connections among institutions.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the research productivity and impact of institutions that are part of dense collaboration networks (rich clubs) with those that are not.

key_quantities: Per-dollar productivity of grants within and outside of rich clubs
Per-dollar impact of grants within and outside of rich clubs

partial_results.note: The relationship between collaboration size and productivity/impact is not fully quantified in the summary.

conclusion_claims: Institutions that form rich clubs in collaborative grant networks tend to have higher per-dollar research productivity and impact.
Collaborative grants within rich clubs are more productive and impactful in a per-dollar sense compared to those outside of rich clubs.
limitations: The study is limited to US-based institutions and NSF-funded grants, which may not generalize to other regions or funding agencies.
The analysis does not account for unobserved factors that may influence research productivity and impact, such as institutional resources or researcher expertise.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed effects are specific to collaborative grants and not due to general differences in funding or research practices.

robustness_checks.method: Exclusion of grants with fewer than five published papers
robustness_checks.rationale: To reduce the influence of outliers and ensure reliable impact measurements.

_source.title: Higher-order rich-club phenomenon in collaborative research grants

arXiv_1804.03732v3.title: Contest models highlight inherent inefficiencies of scientific funding competitions
arXiv_1804.03732v3.research_idea: The paper uses contest theory to analyze the efficiency of scientific funding competitions and compares them with partially randomized alternatives like lotteries. It finds that the effort wasted on proposal writing can be comparable to the scientific value of funded research, especially when few proposals are funded.
arXiv_1804.03732v3.research_question: To what extent does the community’s investment in proposal preparation negate the scientific impact of the funding program? Are there alternative mechanisms for awarding funds that advance science more efficiently?
hypotheses: The effort researchers waste in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports.

When professional pressures motivate investigators to seek funding for reasons beyond the value of the proposed science, the entire program can hamper scientific progress when the number of awards is small.

independent_variables.name: proposal_strength
independent_variables.definition: The strength of a proposal, denoted x ≥ 0, which corresponds to how much effort is invested in writing the proposal.

independent_variables.name: scientific_value
independent_variables.definition: The intrinsic scientific value of a project idea, denoted v ≥ 0, which combines the ability of the investigator and the promise of the idea itself.

independent_variables.name: extra_scientific_reward
independent_variables.definition: An additional reward for investigators, denoted v0 ≥ 0, such as prestige or promotion, which is private and not shared with the funder.

independent_variables.name: number_of_awards
independent_variables.definition: The number of proposals that can be funded, which affects the competition and the effort required to win.

dependent_variables.name: net_scientific_progress
dependent_variables.formula: The total scientific value of funded projects minus the scientific waste from proposal writing.

dependent_variables.name: proposal_effort
dependent_variables.formula: The effort (disutility cost) invested in writing proposals, denoted c(v, x) = g(v)h(x).

control_variables.name: intrinsic_scientific_value_of_proposal_writing
control_variables.rationale: A proportion k ∈ [0, 1) of the disutility cost c(v, x) is recouped by honing ideas, which is considered as the intrinsic scientific value of writing a proposal.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a contest model based on economic theory to analyze the efficiency of funding competitions and compare them with lotteries.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the efficiency of traditional proposal competitions with partially randomized alternatives like lotteries.

result_claims.value: The effort wasted in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports.

When professional pressures motivate investigators to seek funding for reasons beyond the value of the proposed science, the entire program can hamper scientific progress when the number of awards is small.

partial_results.note: The results depend on the number of proposals funded and the presence of extra-scientific incentives.

conclusion_claims: The effort researchers waste in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports, especially when only a few proposals can be funded.
Lost efficiency may be restored either by partial lotteries for funding or by funding researchers based on past scientific success instead of proposals for future work.
limitations: The model assumes a large number of scientists and predictable competition strength, which may not always hold in practice.
The model does not account for variability in reviewer opinions or other real-world complexities in the funding process.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing the model with alternative funding mechanisms like lotteries
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the robustness of the findings to different assumptions about the funding process.

_source.title: Contest models highlight inherent inefficiencies of scientific funding competitions

Research_Policy_1602.07396v1.title: Concentration of research funding leads to decreasing marginal returns
Research_Policy_1602.07396v1.research_idea: This study investigates whether the concentration of research funding in the hands of a few researchers leads to diminishing marginal returns in terms of scientific output and impact.
Research_Policy_1602.07396v1.research_question: How do research productivity and scientific impact of individual researchers vary with the amount of funding they receive, and is this variation similar across different scientific fields?
hypotheses: Higher amounts of funding will lead to higher scientific output and impact.
The relationship between funding and scientific output/impact will exhibit diminishing marginal returns.
Research_Policy_1602.07396v1.data_source: Funding data from research councils in Québec, combined with publication and citation data.
Research_Policy_1602.07396v1.data_description: The study analyzes data on 12,720 researchers in Québec over a 15-year period (1998-2012), with scientific output and impact measured from 2000 to 2013.

filters: Researchers in Québec who received funding from national and provincial research councils

independent_variables.name: funding_amount
independent_variables.definition: The total amount of research funding received by each researcher over the 15-year period.

dependent_variables.name: scientific_output
dependent_variables.formula: Number of scientific papers produced by each researcher from 2000 to 2013.

dependent_variables.name: scientific_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Citation impact of each researcher's publications from 2000 to 2013.

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in output and impact across scientific fields.

control_variables.name: career_stage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in productivity and impact based on the researcher's career stage.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and regression analysis to examine the relationship between funding and scientific output and impact.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the scientific output and impact of researchers who received the most funding versus those who received less funding.

result_claims.value: do not stand out in terms of output and impact

partial_results.note: The study does not provide detailed results on how the relationship between funding and output/impact varies across specific fields.

conclusion_claims: The concentration of research funding in the hands of a few researchers generally produces diminishing marginal returns in terms of scientific output and impact.
The most funded researchers do not necessarily produce the highest scientific output or impact.
limitations: The study is limited to researchers in Québec and may not be generalizable to other regions or countries.
The study does not account for all potential confounding factors that may influence scientific output and impact.

robustness_checks.rationale: To verify whether the results hold across different scientific fields and career stages.

_source.title: Concentration of research funding leads to decreasing marginal returns

arXiv_1409.7889v2.title: Is your EPL attractive? Classification of publications through download statistics
arXiv_1409.7889v2.research_idea: The paper analyzes download statistics of EPL publications to classify papers based on their attractiveness and to understand the dynamics of download activity over time.
arXiv_1409.7889v2.research_question: How can the attractiveness of EPL publications be classified and modeled using download statistics?
hypotheses: Papers exhibit different download patterns over time, which can be modeled and classified.
Open-access papers have different download dynamics compared to non-open-access papers.
arXiv_1409.7889v2.data_description: Full-text download statistics for EPL papers published between January 2007 and June 2013, with one month resolution. The dataset includes 4,986 non-OA papers and 377 OA papers.

independent_variables.name: paper_age
independent_variables.definition: The number of months since the paper was published online.

independent_variables.name: open_access_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the paper is open access (OA) or non-open access (non-OA).

dependent_variables.name: download_density
dependent_variables.formula: Mean number of downloads per paper as a function of paper age.

control_variables.name: publication_month
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in download activity.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a two-factor exponential decay model to fit the download density as a function of paper age.
statistical_method.estimation: Model fitting using exponential decay functions.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare download patterns between open-access and non-open-access papers.

result_claims.value: 2% of non-OA papers show intense bursts of download activity
18% of non-OA papers have an especially high degree of attractiveness
20% of papers exhibit delayed recognition with late spurs in download activity
OA papers have more downloads on average than non-OA papers

partial_results.note: The model predicts long-term download proportions, but actual data may vary.

conclusion_claims: EPL papers exhibit distinct download patterns that can be classified into categories such as intense bursts of activity, high attractiveness, and delayed recognition.
Open-access papers accumulate downloads faster than non-open-access papers, but the proportions of papers in each category are similar.
limitations: Download statistics may not fully capture the impact or quality of a paper.
The analysis is limited to EPL and may not generalize to other journals.

robustness_checks.method: model fitting with different decay constants
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the exponential decay model in capturing download patterns.

_source.title: Is your EPL attractive? Classification of publications through download statistics

arXiv_2006.16737v1.title: Delayed Recognition; the Co-citation Perspective
arXiv_2006.16737v1.research_idea: The paper investigates the phenomenon of delayed recognition in scientific literature, focusing on co-citations as a lens to study delayed recognition of combined ideas. It extends prior work by analyzing a much larger dataset of co-cited article pairs.
arXiv_2006.16737v1.research_question: Can delayed recognition be observed in co-citations, and what are the characteristics of such delayed co-citations?
hypotheses: Co-citations can exhibit delayed recognition patterns similar to single publications.
Delayed co-citations are more likely to originate from 'Sleeping Beauty' publications.
arXiv_2006.16737v1.data_description: The study uses a dataset of over 940 million unique co-cited article pairs from Scopus, covering publications from 1985 to 1995. The dataset includes citation data up to 2018.

filters: Publications with at least 5 cited references
Cited references published no earlier than 1970

independent_variables.name: sleeping_duration
independent_variables.definition: The number of years between the first possible year of co-citation and the year of awakening.

independent_variables.name: peak_co_citation_frequency
independent_variables.definition: The highest annual co-citation frequency observed for a pair.

independent_variables.name: total_co_citation_frequency
independent_variables.definition: The total number of co-citation events for a pair across all years.

dependent_variables.name: delayed_recognition
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the pair meets the criteria for delayed co-citation, 0 otherwise.

dependent_variables.name: awakening_intensity
dependent_variables.formula: The average co-citation frequency during the awakening period.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for disciplinary differences in citation patterns.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and frequency analysis to identify and characterize delayed co-citations.

network_construction.construction_method: Co-citation pairs are generated by identifying all combinations of cited references in each article and counting the frequency of co-citation events over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics of co-citation pairs that exhibit delayed recognition with those that do not.

partial_results.note: The study does not provide a definitive answer on whether delayed co-citations are derived from Sleeping Beauty publications.

conclusion_claims: Delayed recognition can be observed in co-citations, with 1,196 cases identified in the dataset.
The study provides a refined methodology for identifying delayed co-citations and extends prior work by analyzing a much larger dataset.
limitations: The study is limited to co-citations in the Scopus database and may not generalize to other citation databases.
The criteria for identifying delayed co-citations are relatively conservative and may exclude some valid cases.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the methodology and results by comparing them with those of previous studies on delayed recognition.

_source.title: Delayed Recognition; the Co-citation Perspective

Scientometrics_1804.04105v2.title: Comparing scientific and technological impact of biomedical research
Scientometrics_1804.04105v2.research_idea: The paper investigates the technological impact of biomedical research by analyzing how papers are cited by patents, and compares it with their scientific impact as measured by citations from other papers.
Scientometrics_1804.04105v2.research_question: How do the scientific and technological impacts of biomedical research compare in terms of citation patterns, overlap, and temporal dynamics?
hypotheses: There is a positive correlation between citations from patents and from papers.
Highly cited papers in the scientific domain are not necessarily highly cited in the technological domain.
Patent citations generally lag behind paper citations.
Scientometrics_1804.04105v2.data_source: USPTO, PubMed, Web of Science, NLM Catalog
Scientometrics_1804.04105v2.data_description: The study uses data from 1976 to 2013, including 1,637,072 U.S. patents and 919,222 unique biomedical papers. Citation data from patents and papers are analyzed over time.

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year a paper was published.

independent_variables.name: citation_year
independent_variables.definition: The year a citation (either from a paper or a patent) was received.

independent_variables.name: citation_type
independent_variables.definition: Whether the citation is from a paper or a patent.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by a paper in a given year.

dependent_variables.name: citation_lag
dependent_variables.formula: The difference in years between the publication year of a paper and the year it was cited.

control_variables.name: journal_category
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior across biomedical subfields.

control_variables.name: paper_age
control_variables.rationale: To account for the effect of time since publication on citation likelihood.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and time series analysis to compare citation patterns from patents and papers.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations from patents and papers are tracked over time for each paper.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the overlap and differences between papers that are highly cited in the scientific and technological domains.

key_quantities: correlation between patent and paper citations

result_claims.metric: correlation between patent and paper citations

partial_results.metric: citation dynamics for delayed recognition papers
partial_results.note: The study identifies delayed recognition papers but does not provide a detailed statistical analysis of their characteristics.

conclusion_claims: There is a positive correlation between citations from patents and from papers, but the overlap in top-cited papers is small.
Patent citations generally lag behind paper citations by a median of 6 years, indicating a delay in technological impact.
limitations: The study focuses only on U.S. patents and biomedical papers, limiting generalizability.
The matching of non-patent references to MEDLINE papers may have some false negatives.

robustness_checks.method: manual validation of a subset of non-patent references
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the accuracy of the automated matching process.

_source.title: Comparing scientific and technological impact of biomedical research

Science_2501.15552v2.title: Community-centric modeling of citation dynamics explains collective citation patterns in science, law, and patents
Science_2501.15552v2.research_idea: The paper proposes a community-centric model of citation dynamics that explains collective citation patterns across science, law, and patents, including the phenomenon of delayed recognition (sleeping beauties). The model captures shared mechanisms across these systems and improves prediction of future citation success.
Science_2501.15552v2.research_question: Do citation patterns in science, law, and patents exhibit universal statistical properties, and can a unified model explain these patterns and phenomena like delayed recognition?
hypotheses: Citation patterns in science, law, and patents exhibit similar statistical properties such as preferential attachment, recency bias, and delayed recognition.
A community-centric model can capture the core mechanisms driving citation dynamics and replicate observed patterns, including the delayed recognition of 'sleeping beauties'.
Science_2501.15552v2.data_source: SciSciNet, American Physical Society (APS) dataset, United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO), and U.S. federal judicial opinions.
Science_2501.15552v2.data_description: The datasets include scientific citations (SciSciNet and APS), patent citations (USPTO), and legal citations from U.S. federal judicial opinions. These datasets are large-scale and well-documented, covering exponential growth in publications and citations, preferential attachment, recency, and delayed recognition.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly specified, but data spans multiple decades to capture long-term citation dynamics.

independent_variables.name: publication_age
independent_variables.definition: The age of a publication, measured in years since its publication date.

independent_variables.name: accumulated_citations
independent_variables.definition: The total number of citations a publication has received up to a given time point.

independent_variables.name: recency
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how recent a publication is, influencing the likelihood of being cited.

dependent_variables.name: new_citations
dependent_variables.formula: The number of new citations a publication receives in a given year.

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across scientific, patent, and legal domains.

control_variables.name: publication_type
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in citation behavior between scientific papers, patents, and legal opinions.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistical analysis to identify patterns in citation dynamics, including degree distribution, preferential attachment, aging functions, and the distribution of sleeping beauties.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are constructed by linking each publication to the publications it cites, with edges representing citations and directed from citing to cited publications.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation patterns across different knowledge systems and assess the universality of observed statistical properties.

partial_results.note: The model's ability to predict future citation success is not fully quantified in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: Citation patterns in science, law, and patents exhibit remarkably similar statistical properties, including preferential attachment, recency bias, and delayed recognition.
The proposed Community Citation Model (CCM) captures the core mechanisms driving these patterns and provides a unifying framework for understanding citation dynamics across knowledge systems.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly address the limitations of the model in capturing all aspects of citation dynamics.
The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and does not include formal hypothesis testing or inferential methods.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the model's ability to predict future citation success across different domains and time periods.

_source.title: Community-centric modeling of citation dynamics explains collective citation patterns in science, law, and patents

arXiv_2104.01955v1.title: Automating Transfer Credit Assessment in Student Mobility - A Natural Language Processing-based Approach
arXiv_2104.01955v1.research_idea: This paper proposes a model to automate the assessment of transfer credits for student mobility using Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, specifically leveraging semantic and taxonomic similarity of learning outcomes (LOs).
arXiv_2104.01955v1.research_question: How can NLP techniques be used to automate the assessment of transfer credits between courses for student mobility?
hypotheses: Using semantic similarity measures based on transformer models can effectively assess the similarity between learning outcomes.
Combining taxonomic similarity (based on Bloom's taxonomy) with semantic similarity can improve the accuracy of course-to-course similarity assessment.
arXiv_2104.01955v1.data_source: A new benchmark dataset containing seven course-to-course similarity measures was proposed due to the lack of quality benchmark datasets.
arXiv_2104.01955v1.data_description: The dataset contains learning outcomes (LOs) from courses, categorized based on Bloom's taxonomy, and includes semantic and taxonomic similarity measures between courses.

independent_variables.name: semantic_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between learning outcomes based on transformer-based language models like RoBERTa

independent_variables.name: taxonomic_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between learning outcomes based on Bloom's taxonomy levels

dependent_variables.name: course_similarity
dependent_variables.formula: Aggregation of semantic and taxonomic similarity scores between courses

control_variables.name: domain_expertise
control_variables.rationale: To account for potential bias in manual assessments by domain experts

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in course structure and learning outcomes across institutions

statistical_method.specification: The model uses a clustering-inspired methodology to aggregate semantic and taxonomic similarity scores.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the effectiveness of the model in identifying similar and dissimilar courses based on learning outcomes.

result_claims.value: Higher similarity scores for courses with aligned learning outcomes
Tunable parameters allow for scenario-specific adjustments

partial_results.note: The dataset is newly proposed and may require further validation for broader applicability.

conclusion_claims: The proposed model effectively automates the assessment of transfer credits by leveraging semantic and taxonomic similarity of learning outcomes.
The model provides tunable parameters to accommodate different decision-making scenarios, offering flexibility to administrators.
limitations: The lack of quality benchmark datasets may limit the generalizability of the model.
The model's performance may be influenced by the inherent complexity and domain specificity of learning outcomes.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the model's flexibility and performance under different scenarios
To validate the model's accuracy against human judgments

_source.title: Automating Transfer Credit Assessment in Student Mobility -- A Natural Language Processing-based Approach

arXiv_2311.03834v1.title: Universal productivity patterns in research careers
arXiv_2311.03834v1.research_idea: The paper investigates the productivity trajectories of scientists across disciplines and identifies six universal productivity patterns, challenging the canonical narrative of early productivity peaks.
arXiv_2311.03834v1.research_question: Do universal productivity patterns exist in research careers, and if so, what are they?
hypotheses: There exist universal productivity patterns in research careers.
The canonical productivity narrative (early peak and decline) is not the most prevalent pattern.
arXiv_2311.03834v1.data_description: Academic curricula of 8,493 Brazilian researchers from 56 disciplines who hold the CNPq Research Productivity Fellowship, spanning over fifty research disciplines.

filters: career length of at least ten years

independent_variables.name: career_stage
independent_variables.definition: Early-career, mid-career, or senior researchers based on career length

independent_variables.name: discipline
independent_variables.definition: Research discipline of the scientist

independent_variables.name: productivity_inflation
independent_variables.definition: Discipline-specific productivity inflation adjusted to 2015 values

dependent_variables.name: productivity_trajectory
dependent_variables.formula: Deflated, standardized, and smoothed yearly publication counts using z-scores and Gaussian filtering

control_variables.name: career_length
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in career duration across researchers

control_variables.name: discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for discipline-specific productivity trends

statistical_method.specification: Dynamic time warping (DTW) for trajectory similarity, UMAP for dimensionality reduction, and clustering based on pairwise similarities

network_construction.construction_method: DTW-based similarity matrix and UMAP for network representation of productivity trajectories

grouping_strategy.rationale: To identify and compare the prevalence of different productivity patterns across researchers

partial_results.note: The study suggests cohort effects but does not provide a detailed analysis of their impact on productivity patterns

conclusion_claims: There are six universal productivity patterns in research careers: constant, u-shaped, decreasing, periodic, increasing, and canonical.
The canonical productivity pattern is the most prevalent, but productivity peaks occur more frequently around mid-career rather than early.
limitations: The study is limited to Brazilian researchers, which may affect generalizability to other countries.
The dataset does not include researchers outside the CNPq Research Productivity Fellowship, potentially introducing selection bias.

robustness_checks.method: deflation and standardization of productivity series
robustness_checks.rationale: To account for discipline-specific productivity inflation and make trajectories comparable
To reduce noise in productivity series and improve pattern identification

_source.title: Universal productivity patterns in research careers

arXiv_2407.14559v1.title: Predicting Star Scientists in the Field of Artificial Intelligence: A Machine Learning Approach
arXiv_2407.14559v1.research_idea: This study proposes a machine learning model to predict star scientists in the field of artificial intelligence by analyzing early-career features, emphasizing the importance of network measures, diversity metrics, and research output.
arXiv_2407.14559v1.research_question: What features can be used to predict star scientists in the field of artificial intelligence, and how do these features differentiate rising stars from their non-rising counterparts?
hypotheses: Rising stars in AI follow different patterns in early-career features compared to non-rising stars.
Features such as gender and ethnic diversity significantly impact an author's career development and success.

arXiv_2105.06840v1.title: The academic wanderer: structure of collaboration network and relation with research performance
arXiv_2105.06840v1.research_idea: This study explores the relationship between the structure of scientists' co-authorship ego networks, academic performance, and academic mobility, leveraging cognitive models from anthropology to understand how cognitive investment in collaborations affects research outcomes.
arXiv_2105.06840v1.research_question: How do the structure of co-authorship ego networks, academic performance, and academic mobility interrelate across different career stages?
hypotheses: There is a positive correlation between academic performance (productivity and impact) and the size of co-authorship ego network layers.
Academic mobility (both international and domestic) is associated with larger collaboration networks.
A cognitive capacity bound exists in the size of collaboration networks, similar to social networks.
International and domestic migrants differ in their efficiency in exploiting collaboration networks for productivity and impact.
arXiv_2105.06840v1.data_description: A dataset comprising the geo-referenced publications of 81,500 authors extracted from Scopus, one of the largest repositories of academic knowledge.

filters: Career stages ranging from young researchers to distinguished professors

independent_variables.name: academic_performance

independent_variables.name: academic_mobility
independent_variables.definition: Includes both pure academic mobility (number of affiliation changes) and geographical mobility (relocations to different countries).

independent_variables.name: ego_network_structure
independent_variables.definition: Structure of co-authorship ego networks, characterized by the sizes of concentric circles (layers) representing different levels of cognitive investment.

dependent_variables.name: ego_network_layer_size
dependent_variables.formula: Number of co-authors in each concentric layer of the ego network.

dependent_variables.name: research_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Measured by citation impact and h-index.

control_variables.name: career_stage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in academic performance and collaboration patterns at different stages of a researcher's career.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for variations in collaboration norms and research impact across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis and correlation to explore the relationships between academic performance, mobility, and ego network structure.

network_construction.construction_method: Ego networks are constructed by considering each author as the central node (ego) and connecting them to their co-authors (alters) based on co-authorship relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare how different types of academic mobility affect the structure and effectiveness of collaboration networks.

key_quantities: correlation between ego network layer sizes and academic performance
comparison of ego network growth between international and domestic migrants

result_claims.metric: correlation between ego network layer sizes and academic performance
capacity bound effect on ego network size
international migrants vs. domestic migrants in ego network growth
result_claims.value: International migrants grow larger networks, domestic migrants achieve higher impact

partial_results.metric: exact mechanisms of cognitive investment in collaboration
partial_results.note: Not fully quantified due to the descriptive nature of the analysis.

conclusion_claims: There is a clear correlation between the structure of co-authorship ego networks and academic performance indices, with higher productivity and impact associated with larger networks.
International academic migrants are better at growing their networks, while domestic migrants are better at achieving higher impact through their collaborations.
limitations: The study is descriptive and does not establish causal relationships between variables.
The dataset does not include detailed information on the cognitive effort or quality of collaborations.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing results across different career stages
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed patterns are not specific to a particular stage of a researcher's career.

robustness_checks.rationale: To confirm the differential effects of mobility types on research outcomes.

Science_1812.09241v1.title: Assessing the accuracy of the h and g indexes for measuring researchers’ productivity
Science_1812.09241v1.venue: Journal of the American Society for Information Science and Technology
Science_1812.09241v1.research_idea: The paper evaluates the accuracy of the h-index and g-index in measuring individual researchers' productivity by comparing them to a more accurate indicator, the fractional scientific strength (FSS).
Science_1812.09241v1.research_question: To what extent do the h-index and g-index accurately measure individual researchers' productivity compared to the FSS?
hypotheses: The h-index and g-index will show significant discrepancies in ranking researchers compared to the FSS.
The g-index will provide a more accurate measure of productivity than the h-index.
Science_1812.09241v1.data_description: The dataset includes the scientific production of Italian academic researchers in the hard sciences over the period 2001-2005. It covers over 20,000 researchers across 165 subject fields.

independent_variables.name: h-index
independent_variables.definition: The maximum number h of works by a scientist that have at least h citations each.

independent_variables.name: g-index
independent_variables.definition: The highest number g of articles that together received g² or more citations.

dependent_variables.name: FSS
dependent_variables.formula: A proxy measure of individual researcher productivity that accounts for the overall impact of a researcher's production, field normalization, and co-authorship adjustments.

control_variables.name: subject field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in publication intensity and citation practices across fields.

control_variables.name: number of co-authors
control_variables.rationale: To adjust for the contribution of individual researchers in multi-author publications.

statistical_method.specification: The study constructs and compares rankings of researchers based on the h-index, g-index, and FSS. It quantifies the shifts in ranks between the indicators.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the accuracy of the h-index and g-index in measuring productivity against the FSS.

key_quantities: percentage of researchers with significant rank changes

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide exact numerical values for rank shifts but describes them as significant.

conclusion_claims: The h-index and g-index show significant discrepancies in ranking researchers compared to the FSS, indicating that they are not fully accurate measures of productivity.
The g-index provides a more accurate measure of productivity than the h-index, but neither is as accurate as the FSS.
limitations: The study is limited to Italian academic researchers in the hard sciences, so the results may not generalize to other countries or fields.
The FSS is a newly proposed indicator, and its validity and reliability have not been widely tested in other contexts.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed differences in rankings are not due to random variation or specific to one indicator.

_source.title: Assessing the accuracy of the h and g indexes for measuring researchers' productivity

Nature_1906.04619v1.title: Achieving competitive advantage in academia through early career coauthorship with top scientists
Nature_1906.04619v1.research_idea: The paper investigates the long-term impact of coauthorship with top-cited scientists on the careers of junior researchers, finding that such coauthorship provides a persistent competitive advantage.

filters: At least one paper every five years

independent_variables.name: early_career_coauthorship_with_top_scientists

Single early coauthorship with top scientists

Number of junior researchers in each institutional prestige quintile

Scientometrics_2501.17456v1.title: A Review on the Novelty Measurements of Academic Papers
Scientometrics_2501.17456v1.research_idea: This paper provides a systematic review of novelty measurements in academic papers, comparing novelty with related concepts and reviewing existing measures, validation approaches, and tools for quantifying scientific novelty.
Scientometrics_2501.17456v1.research_question: What are the current methods, tools, and datasets for measuring the novelty of academic papers, and what are the open issues in this field?

statistical_method.specification: The paper is a literature review and does not present statistical models or analyses.

conclusion_claims: Novelty is a critical but challenging concept to measure in academic papers, with various definitions and approaches proposed in the literature.

limitations: The paper is a review and does not present original empirical data or analysis.

arXiv_2202.06661v2.title: Eternalism and the problem of hyperplanes
arXiv_2202.06661v2.research_idea: The paper investigates the philosophical implications of eternalism in the context of the relativity of simultaneity and the conventionality of simultaneity. It explores whether eternalism can be supported without relying on hyperplanes of simultaneity.
arXiv_2202.06661v2.research_question: Can eternalism be defended without relying on hyperplanes of simultaneity, given the conventionality of simultaneity?
hypotheses: Traditional eternalist arguments based on hyperplanes are compromised by the conventionality of simultaneity.
Eternalism can still be supported through alternative strategies that do not rely on hyperplanes.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses philosophical analysis and logical reasoning to evaluate arguments for and against eternalism.

partial_results.note: Lack of a rigorous proof for eternalism remains an open issue.

conclusion_claims: Eternalism remains a credible alternative to presentism despite the challenges posed by the conventionality of simultaneity.
There are cogent reasons to prefer eternalism over presentism, even in the absence of hyperplanes.
limitations: The paper does not provide a rigorous proof for eternalism.
The analysis is primarily philosophical and does not rely on empirical data.

_source.title: Eternalism and the Problem of Hyperplanes

arXiv_1810.07812v1.title: Openness and Impact of Leading Scientific Countries
arXiv_1810.07812v1.research_idea: This paper investigates the relationship between international scientific collaboration and the impact of scientific output, using fractionalized field-weighted citation impact (fracFWCI) and openness indices to assess the benefits of international collaboration and mobility.
arXiv_1810.07812v1.research_question: Does international collaboration and researcher mobility correlate with higher scientific impact, and how can this be measured?
hypotheses: Countries with higher levels of international collaboration and researcher mobility will have higher scientific impact.
Public spending on R&D will moderate the relationship between openness and scientific impact.
arXiv_1810.07812v1.data_description: The study uses Elsevier's national-level full and fractional paper counts and field-weighted citation counts for 2013, OECD data on international mobility of the scientific workforce in 2013, and OECD data on government budget allocation on R&D in 2011.

independent_variables.name: international_collaboration
independent_variables.definition: Percentage of internationally coauthored papers for each country

independent_variables.name: researcher_mobility

independent_variables.name: GBARD
independent_variables.definition: Government budget allocation on research and development in 2011

dependent_variables.name: fracFWCI
dependent_variables.formula: Fractional field-weighted citation impact, calculated as a weighted average where each article has a weight equal to the proportion of authors that represent the entity (country) on the paper

control_variables.name: country_population
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in national size and potential for scientific output

control_variables.name: GDP
control_variables.rationale: To account for economic resources available for R&D

statistical_method.specification: Principal component analysis was used to create an openness index combining international collaboration and researcher mobility. Correlation analysis was used to assess the relationship between openness and fracFWCI.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of openness in countries with well-developed science systems versus others

partial_results.note: The field-level calculation led to over-counting of articles due to multiple field assignments, but the overall correlation results were similar.

conclusion_claims: Openness, as measured by international collaboration and researcher mobility, is strongly correlated with scientific impact.

Scientometrics_2603.19319v1.research_idea: This study investigates the differences in novelty between academic and industrial research outputs by leveraging a unified semantic space to analyze fine-grained knowledge entities (Methods, Tools, Datasets, Metrics) in both patents and papers. Scientometrics_2603.19319v1.research_question: Is there a difference in the novelty of scientific and technical literature between industry and academia?

independent_variables.name: collaboration_type

arXiv_2207.13857v1.title: Measuring Difficulty of Novelty Reaction
arXiv_2207.13857v1.research_idea: The paper proposes a method to approximate the difficulty of novelty reaction for AI systems by measuring the distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces. This method helps evaluate how hard it is for AI systems to adapt to novel situations.
arXiv_2207.13857v1.research_question: How can the difficulty of novelty reaction for AI systems be measured or approximated before the agent interacts with it?
hypotheses: The distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces can be used to approximate the difficulty of novelty reaction.
The predicted difficulty of novelty aligns with the actual performance of AI agents in novel scenarios.
arXiv_2207.13857v1.data_description: The data consists of generated Science Birds levels with and without introduced novelties. A total of 12 novelties were tested using 100 base levels for difficulty estimation and over 1000 levels per novelty for agent evaluation.

independent_variables.name: novelty_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of novelty introduced to the base levels in the Science Birds environment.

independent_variables.name: solution_space_distance
independent_variables.definition: The distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces, calculated using a custom distance function.

dependent_variables.name: agent_pass_rate
dependent_variables.formula: The average pass percentage of state-of-the-art novelty reaction agents in novel game scenarios.

control_variables.name: base_level_similarity
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in agent performance are due to the novelty and not the base level characteristics.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics to compare predicted difficulty with observed agent performance.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Novelties are grouped based on the calculated distance between pre-novel and post-novel solution spaces to evaluate agent performance across difficulty levels.

partial_results.note: These novelties were misclassified due to the randomness of the sampling agents or limited number of sampled levels.

conclusion_claims: The proposed method for measuring novelty reaction difficulty aligns with the observed performance of AI agents in most cases.
The method is practical and can be applied to other domains where novelty difficulty is measured by solution space differences.
limitations: The method relies on random sampling agents, which introduces randomness and potential misclassification.
The limited number of sampled levels may affect the accuracy of difficulty predictions.

robustness_checks.method: increasing the number of sampled levels
robustness_checks.rationale: To reduce the impact of randomness and improve the accuracy of difficulty predictions.

_source.title: Measuring Difficulty of Novelty Reaction

arXiv_2201.00531v1.research_idea: The paper proposes a generalization scoring framework for Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in autonomous driving applications by incorporating the novelty of test samples. limitations: The results are specific to the traffic light detection application and may not generalize to other domains.
