arXiv_2502.17841v3.title: Quantifying interdisciplinary synergy in higher STEM education
arXiv_2502.17841v3.research_idea: The paper proposes a data-driven framework to quantify and evaluate interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula using large-scale educational data and information theory-based metrics.
arXiv_2502.17841v3.research_question: How can interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula be quantified and evaluated to support the design of integrated educational programs?
hypotheses: Integrated curricula combining two disciplines will show higher synergy scores compared to single-discipline curricula.
Engineering disciplines will form the core of interdisciplinary curriculum design, while basic natural sciences will serve as peripheral stems.
arXiv_2502.17841v3.data_source: Higher Education in Korea portal operated by the Ministry of Education in Korea
arXiv_2502.17841v3.data_description: The dataset includes curricula from 2,841 STEM departments across 161 higher education institutions in Korea for the 2024 spring semester, with a total of 126,437 raw course entries.
available_fields: Mathematics, Physics, Astronomy, and Earth Science

filters: Departments classified under the Standard Classification Information system in Korea

independent_variables.name: discipline_similarity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of similarity between disciplines based on curriculum overlap.

independent_variables.name: credit_allocation
independent_variables.definition: The number of credits allocated to courses in a curriculum.

independent_variables.name: field_combination
independent_variables.definition: The pairing of two disciplines in an integrated curriculum.

dependent_variables.name: curriculum_synergy_score
dependent_variables.formula: Computed using information theory-based metrics that evaluate the integration and synergy between disciplines.

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in curriculum design across institutions.

control_variables.name: total_credits
control_variables.rationale: To normalize for variations in credit requirements across departments.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis and information theory-based metrics to evaluate curriculum synergy and integration.

network_construction.construction_method: A bipartite network is constructed linking departments to courses, and a projected network is used to analyze relationships between disciplines.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the level of interdisciplinarity between curricula that focus on a single discipline versus those that integrate two disciplines.

result_claims.value: Higher for combinations within or across closely related fields, especially in engineering fields.
Higher in engineering fields compared to basic natural sciences.

partial_results.note: The results are based on a specific dataset from Korea and may not generalize to other educational systems.

conclusion_claims: Engineering fields constitute the core structure of interdisciplinary curriculum design, while basic natural science fields serve as peripheral stems.
The proposed framework provides a principled method for quantifying and evaluating interdisciplinary synergy in STEM curricula.
limitations: The study is limited to data from Korea and may not be generalizable to other countries with different educational systems.
The analysis is based on department-level data, which may not capture finer distinctions at the program or course level.

robustness_checks.method: Sensitivity analysis of credit allocation and curriculum similarity metrics
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not sensitive to variations in credit requirements or similarity measures.

_source.title: Quantifying interdisciplinary synergy in higher STEM education

arXiv_2304.11079v1.title: Academic Writing with GPT-3.5: Reflections on Practices, Efficacy and Transparency
arXiv_2304.11079v1.research_idea: The paper explores the use of GPT-3.5 in academic writing, identifying five methods of integration and reflecting on their efficacy and ethical implications.
arXiv_2304.11079v1.research_question: How can GPT-3.5 be effectively and transparently used in academic writing, and what are its impacts on writing practices and ethics?

statistical_method.specification: Qualitative reflection and analysis of personal experience with GPT-3.5 in writing the paper.

conclusion_claims: GPT-3.5 can be used in academic writing in five distinct ways: Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher.
The use of GPT-3.5 in academic writing raises ethical concerns, including plagiarism, misinformation, and transparency, which need to be addressed for responsible use.
limitations: The study is based on a single author's personal experience and is not generalizable.
The paper does not include quantitative analysis or comparative studies of GPT-3.5's efficacy across different writing tasks.

_source.title: Academic Writing with GPT-3.5: Reflections on Practices, Efficacy and Transparency

Science_2502.18427v1.title: Is OpenAlex Suitable for Research Quality Evaluation and Which Citation Indicator is Best?
Science_2502.18427v1.research_idea: This paper evaluates the suitability of OpenAlex for research quality evaluation and compares the effectiveness of different citation-based indicators (raw counts, NCS, NLCS) against expert quality assessments.
Science_2502.18427v1.research_question: Which citation-based indicator and database (OpenAlex or Scopus) best correlates with research quality as judged by expert evaluations?
hypotheses: OpenAlex citation counts are better for research quality indicators than Scopus citation counts.
Field-normalized indicators (NCS or NLCS) are better than raw citation counts for evaluating research quality.
The broadest OpenAlex classification scheme provides the best indicators.
OpenAlex's inclusive document classification compromises the value of field normalization.
Science_2502.18427v1.data_source: OpenAlex, Scopus, and UK Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021 data
Science_2502.18427v1.data_description: 28.6 million articles from 2014-2020, with 97,816 UK REF2021 articles used as gold standards. Two gold standards: ChatGPT scores and average REF2021 expert review scores.

filters: articles in both OpenAlex and Scopus

independent_variables.name: citation_count
independent_variables.definition: Number of citations received by an article

independent_variables.name: NCS
independent_variables.definition: Normalised Citation Score, calculated as citation count divided by the mean citation count of all articles in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: NLCS
independent_variables.definition: Normalised Log-transformed Citation Score, calculated as ln(1 + citation count) divided by the mean ln(1 + citation count) of all articles in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: database
independent_variables.definition: Whether the citation data is from OpenAlex or Scopus

independent_variables.name: classification_scheme
independent_variables.definition: Subject classification scheme used (Scopus broad fields, OpenAlex domains, fields, subfields, or topics)

dependent_variables.name: ChatGPT_score
dependent_variables.formula: Quality score assigned by ChatGPT for each article

dependent_variables.name: REF2021_score
dependent_variables.formula: Average expert review score from the REF2021 evaluation for the department submitting the article

control_variables.name: year
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation rates over time

control_variables.name: field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across disciplines

control_variables.name: document_type
control_variables.rationale: To ensure only comparable document types are compared

statistical_method.specification: Correlation analysis between 32 citation-based indicators and two gold standard quality scores (ChatGPT and REF2021) across 8,704 correlations

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of citation indicators derived from different databases and their overlap

result_claims.value: at least as good as field-normalized indicators

partial_results.note: Field normalization may be ineffective because more cited fields tend to produce higher quality work, affecting interdisciplinary research or within-field topic differences.

conclusion_claims: OpenAlex is suitable for citation analysis in most fields.
Raw citation counts are at least as good as field-normalized indicators for evaluating research quality.
limitations: The study is limited to UK REF2021 articles and may not generalize to other regions or fields.
The use of ChatGPT as a gold standard may introduce biases or inaccuracies.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the impact of subject classification granularity on indicator performance

robustness_checks.method: correlation analysis with two gold standards
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the robustness of the findings against different quality assessments

_source.title: Is OpenAlex Suitable for Research Quality Evaluation and Which Citation Indicator is Best?

Science_2511.05051v1.title: The use of social media among library professionals and patrons: A review of literature
Science_2511.05051v1.venue: International Journal of Academic Library and Information Science Research
Science_2511.05051v1.research_idea: This paper reviews the literature on the use of social media among library professionals and patrons, highlighting its role in information dissemination, awareness creation, and library service promotion.
Science_2511.05051v1.research_question: What are the popular social media platforms used by library professionals and patrons, and what are the reasons for their adoption?
Science_2511.05051v1.data_source: Literature review of existing studies and reports on social media use in libraries.
Science_2511.05051v1.data_description: The data consists of a synthesis of findings from various studies and reports published between 2010 and 2019, focusing on the use of social media in libraries across different regions.

filters: studies on library professionals and patrons

independent_variables.name: social_media_platform
independent_variables.definition: The specific social media platform used by libraries (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, YouTube).

independent_variables.name: library_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of library (e.g., academic, public, school).

dependent_variables.name: adoption_rate
dependent_variables.formula: Number of libraries adopting a specific social media platform.

dependent_variables.name: usage_frequency
dependent_variables.formula: Frequency of social media use for library services and communication.

control_variables.name: region
control_variables.rationale: To account for regional differences in social media adoption and usage patterns.

control_variables.name: library_size
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in library size and resources.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis to summarize the findings from the literature review.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable as the study is a literature review and does not involve network construction.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the use of social media between library professionals and patrons.

result_claims.value: Facebook is the most popular social media platform used by libraries.
Social media is frequently used for information dissemination and awareness creation.

partial_results.note: The adoption rate varies by region and library type.

conclusion_claims: Social media is widely adopted by libraries to enhance communication, information dissemination, and service promotion.
Library management should encourage the use of social media to keep libraries relevant and improve user engagement.
limitations: The study is based on a literature review and does not include primary data collection.
The findings may not be generalizable to all regions and library types.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the consistency and reliability of the findings.

_source.title: The use of social media among library professionals and patrons: A review of literature

arXiv_2511.04075v1.title: Two Decades of Research at the University of Lagos (2004-2023): A Scientometric Analysis of Productivity, Collaboration, and Impact
arXiv_2511.04075v1.research_idea: This paper presents a scientometric analysis of research output from the University of Lagos over two decades (2004-2023), focusing on trends in publication volume, collaboration patterns, citation impact, and the most prolific authors, departments, and research domains.
arXiv_2511.04075v1.research_question: What are the trends in research productivity, collaboration, and impact at the University of Lagos from 2004 to 2023?
hypotheses: Research productivity at the University of Lagos has increased over the two-decade period.
Collaborative efforts, both local and international, correlate with higher citation impact.
Open-access publications contribute to higher visibility and citation rates.
arXiv_2511.04075v1.data_description: Bibliometric data retrieved from the Web of Science covering the period 2004 to 2023, focusing on research output from the University of Lagos.

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: Year of publication, used to analyze trends over time.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_type
independent_variables.definition: Type of collaboration (local, international) to assess its impact on citation rates.

independent_variables.name: open_access
independent_variables.definition: Whether the publication is open access (yes/no).

independent_variables.name: research_domain
independent_variables.definition: Field of research (e.g., Health Sciences, Engineering, Social Sciences).

dependent_variables.name: publication_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of publications per year.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Total number of citations received by publications.

control_variables.name: department
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in research output across departments.

control_variables.name: author_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To control for individual author contributions to overall research output.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive analysis of publication trends, collaboration patterns, and citation impact over time.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable; no network analysis was conducted.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare research productivity and impact between the first and second decade of the study period.

partial_results.note: The exact magnitude of the impact of collaboration on citation rates is not quantified in the paper.

conclusion_claims: The University of Lagos has shown a consistent increase in research productivity over the two-decade period, with 2023 recording the highest publication output.
Collaborative efforts, particularly with the United States and the United Kingdom, are positively correlated with higher citation impact.
limitations: The study is based solely on data from the Web of Science, which may not capture all research outputs from the University of Lagos.
The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the specific factors driving the increase in research productivity.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the observed trends are consistent across the two-decade period.

_source.title: Two Decades of Research at the University of Lagos (2004-2023): A Scientometric Analysis of Productivity, Collaboration, and Impact

PNAS_2303.14732v3.title: Interdisciplinary Papers Supported by Disciplinary Grants Garner Deep and Broad Scientific Impact
PNAS_2303.14732v3.research_idea: The paper investigates the relationship between the interdisciplinarity of research grants and the impact of the resulting scientific publications, revealing that interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants tend to have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
PNAS_2303.14732v3.research_question: Do interdisciplinary grants support high-impact interdisciplinary advances?
hypotheses: Interdisciplinary grants produce interdisciplinary papers with high impact.
Interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
PNAS_2303.14732v3.data_description: The study uses 350,000 grants from 164 agencies in 26 countries and 1.3 million resulting papers published between 1985 and 2009.

independent_variables.name: grant_interdisciplinarity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the interdisciplinarity of a grant, calculated using the Rao-Stirling diversity index based on the distribution of fields in the grant's abstract.

independent_variables.name: paper_interdisciplinarity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the interdisciplinarity of a paper, calculated using the Rao-Stirling diversity index based on the distribution of fields in the paper's references and citations.

dependent_variables.name: paper_impact
dependent_variables.formula: The impact of a paper is measured by its hit rate, defined as the probability of a paper being in the top 5% of citations in its field and year.

dependent_variables.name: number_of_papers
dependent_variables.formula: The number of papers produced by a grant.

control_variables.name: funding_amount
control_variables.rationale: To account for the possibility that larger funding amounts may influence the number of papers and their impact.

control_variables.name: author_prominence
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the prominence of the authors on the impact of the papers.

control_variables.name: team_size
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of the size of the research team on the number of papers and their impact.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and comparisons across groups to analyze the relationship between grant interdisciplinarity and paper impact.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of papers supported by interdisciplinary grants versus those supported by disciplinary grants.

result_claims.value: Higher for interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants
Lower for papers supported by interdisciplinary grants

partial_results.note: The impact of interdisciplinary papers supported by interdisciplinary grants is lower than expected, but the reason for this is not fully explained.

conclusion_claims: Interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants tend to have higher impact than those supported by interdisciplinary grants.
Disciplinary grants play an important role in producing high-impact interdisciplinary advances.
limitations: The study does not fully explain why interdisciplinary papers supported by disciplinary grants have higher impact.
The results are based on a specific time period (1985-2009) and may not generalize to more recent years.

robustness_checks.method: Controlling for author prominence and team size
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed differences in impact are not due to differences in author prominence or team size.
To confirm the robustness of the results across different funding agencies, time periods, disciplines, and countries.

_source.title: Interdisciplinary Papers Supported by Disciplinary Grants Garner Deep and Broad Scientific Impact

arXiv_2209.11284v1.title: The Impact of Social Media in Learning and Teaching: A Bibliometric-based Citation Analysis
arXiv_2209.11284v1.research_idea: This paper presents a systematic review of the literature on the impact of social media in learning and teaching through bibliometric-based citation analysis. The study maps the evolution of the current literature and identifies leading sources of knowledge in terms of influential journals, authors, and articles.
arXiv_2209.11284v1.research_question: What is the impact of social media in teaching and learning, and what are the leading sources of knowledge in this field?
arXiv_2209.11284v1.data_description: A total of 50 top most relevant articles were selected from the Scopus database for citation analysis.

fields: social media in learning and teaching

statistical_method.specification: Citation analysis was conducted to explore the theoretical foundation, identify leading sources of knowledge, and reveal research trends.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation analysis was used to build the network based on the selected articles.

result_claims.value: authored by Baaran S. and Bapitha L.

conclusion_claims: The study outlines the current state of knowledge regarding social media in learning and teaching.
The analysis reveals the leading journals, authors, and articles in the field of social media in education.
limitations: The study is limited to 50 selected articles from the Scopus database.
The focus is on citation analysis, which may not capture all aspects of research impact.

_source.title: The Impact of Social Media in Learning and Teaching: A Bibliometric-based Citation Analysis

Scientometrics_2203.06218v2.title: Article Processing Charges, Altmetrics and Citation Impact: Is there an economic rationale?
Scientometrics_2203.06218v2.research_idea: This study investigates the relationship between Article Processing Charges (APCs) and the citation impact of Gold Open Access (OA) publications, as well as the determinants of APCs. It challenges the assumption that higher APCs lead to higher impact and explores the role of Altmetrics in this context.
Scientometrics_2203.06218v2.research_question: Does paying high APCs necessarily increase the impact of scientific publications, and what factors determine APCs?
hypotheses: Paying high APCs increases the impact of scientific publications.
Large publishers with high impact charge the highest APCs.
Altmetrics have a significant impact on the visibility and impact of OA articles.
Scientometrics_2203.06218v2.data_source: OpenAPC database, Web of Science (OST in-house database), Altmetric.com database
Scientometrics_2203.06218v2.data_description: The study uses APC data from the OpenAPC database (109,141 publications from 2006 to 2019), citation data from the Web of Science (83,752 publications), and Altmetrics data from Altmetric.com (62,532 publications).

filters: Publications with matching DOIs in all three databases

independent_variables.name: APC_amount
independent_variables.definition: The amount of the Article Processing Charge (APC) paid for a publication.

independent_variables.name: Hybrid_journal
independent_variables.definition: A flag indicating whether the journal is a hybrid journal (1) or a full OA journal (0).

independent_variables.name: Altmetrics_score
independent_variables.definition: A score representing the Altmetrics attention received by a publication.

independent_variables.name: Publisher_impact
independent_variables.definition: The Mean Normalized Citation Score (MNCS) at the publisher level.

independent_variables.name: Journal_impact
independent_variables.definition: The Mean Normalized Impact of Journals (MNIJ) at the journal level.

dependent_variables.name: Normalized_Citations_Score
dependent_variables.formula: NCS = (Number of citations received by the article) / (Average number of citations in the same discipline and year)

dependent_variables.name: Altmetrics_score
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated from Altmetric.com data representing the attention received by a publication.

control_variables.name: Discipline
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation practices across disciplines.

control_variables.name: Country
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in APCs and impact based on the country of the journal.

control_variables.name: Publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in APCs and impact.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and correlation analysis to explore the relationships between APCs, citation impact, and Altmetrics.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare APCs and impact between hybrid and full OA journals.

key_quantities: Correlation between APCs and citation impact
Average APCs in hybrid vs. full OA journals
Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact

result_claims.metric: Correlation between APCs and citation impact

result_claims.value: 50% higher than in full OA journals

result_claims.metric: Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact
Correlation between 'number of readers' and citation impact

partial_results.metric: Correlation between APCs and citation impact
partial_results.note: The correlation is moderate, suggesting that higher APCs do not necessarily lead to higher impact.

partial_results.metric: Correlation between Altmetrics and citation impact
partial_results.note: The correlation is low, suggesting that Altmetrics do not strongly predict citation impact.

conclusion_claims: Paying high APCs does not necessarily increase the impact of scientific publications.
Hybrid journals charge significantly higher APCs than full OA journals.
Altmetrics do not have a strong impact on the visibility and citation impact of OA articles.
limitations: The study is based on a specific set of databases and may not be generalizable to all OA publications.
The analysis is descriptive and does not establish causal relationships.

robustness_checks.rationale: To verify the consistency of results across different subsets of the data.
To assess the impact of excluding certain variables or data points on the results.

_source.title: Article Processing Charges, Altmetrics and Citation Impact: Is there an economic rationale?

Science_2212.07811v1.title: Do altmetric scores reflect article quality? Evidence from the UK Research Excellence Framework 2021
Science_2212.07811v1.research_idea: This paper investigates whether altmetric scores from Altmetric.com and Mendeley reflect the quality of academic journal articles, using expert peer review scores from the UK Research Excellence Framework 2021 as a benchmark.
Science_2212.07811v1.research_question: How useful are altmetric scores as indicators of research quality in all fields, and how do they compare to citation counts?
hypotheses: Altmetric scores are better indicators of research quality than previously thought.
Mendeley reader counts are the strongest altmetric indicator of quality in most fields.
Altmetric scores reflect non-scholarly impacts more than citation counts.
Science_2212.07811v1.data_source: UK Research Excellence Framework 2021 (REF2021) peer review scores, Altmetric.com, Mendeley, and Scopus.
Science_2212.07811v1.data_description: The dataset includes 67,030+ journal articles from 2014-2018, across 34 Units of Assessment (UoAs) in all academic fields. Articles were rated by expert peer review for quality (0 to 4*). Altmetric and Mendeley data were matched via DOIs.

filters: Articles with DOIs and Scopus entries
Excluded duplicate articles and those with score 0
Excluded articles after 2018 due to insufficient citation time

independent_variables.name: Altmetric Attention Score
independent_variables.definition: A hybrid score combining various altmetric indicators (e.g., tweets, Mendeley readers, news mentions).

independent_variables.name: Mendeley reader count
independent_variables.definition: Number of readers of an article on Mendeley.

independent_variables.name: Tweet count
independent_variables.definition: Number of tweets mentioning the article.

independent_variables.name: Facebook mentions
independent_variables.definition: Number of Facebook mentions of the article.

independent_variables.name: Blog mentions
independent_variables.definition: Number of blog mentions of the article.

independent_variables.name: News mentions
independent_variables.definition: Number of news articles mentioning the article.

independent_variables.name: Raw Scopus citation count
independent_variables.definition: Total number of citations in Scopus.

independent_variables.name: Field-normalized Scopus citation count
independent_variables.definition: Scopus citation count adjusted for field-specific citation practices.

dependent_variables.name: REF2021 quality score
dependent_variables.formula: 0 (unclassified), 1* (nationally recognized), 2* (internationally recognized), 3* (internationally excellent), 4* (world-leading).

control_variables.name: Field of study
control_variables.rationale: To account for field-specific differences in citation and altmetric practices.

control_variables.name: Year of publication
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in altmetric and citation behavior.

statistical_method.specification: Correlation analysis between altmetric scores and REF2021 quality scores, with comparisons across fields and UoAs.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the strength of altmetric indicators across disciplines.

key_quantities: Correlation coefficients between altmetric scores and quality scores
Comparison of altmetric indicators by field

result_claims.metric: Correlation between Mendeley reader count and quality score
Correlation between tweet count and quality score
Correlation between Altmetric Attention Score and quality score
result_claims.value: Almost as strong as Mendeley reader count

result_claims.metric: Correlation between field-normalized citation count and quality score
result_claims.value: Stronger than altmetrics in most fields

result_claims.metric: Correlation between raw citation count and quality score

partial_results.note: May reduce its strength as a quality indicator in single fields.
Moderately strong in some UoAs but not consistently across all fields.

conclusion_claims: Altmetric scores are better indicators of research quality than previously thought, though not as strong as citation counts.
Mendeley reader counts are the strongest altmetric indicator of quality in most fields, followed by tweet counts and Altmetric Attention Score.
limitations: Field-normalized citation counts may not always be the best indicator in single fields.
Low numbers of articles in arts and humanities UoAs may affect precision.
Altmetric data may not be complete for all DOIs, especially for preprints or alternative versions.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure findings are not field-specific.

robustness_checks.method: Excluding duplicate articles and using median scores
robustness_checks.rationale: To reduce bias from multiple submissions of the same article.

_source.title: Do altmetric scores reflect article quality? Evidence from the UK Research Excellence Framework 2021

arXiv_2601.11507v1.title: Industry Influence in High-Profile Social Media Research
arXiv_2601.11507v1.research_idea: This paper investigates the extent of industry influence in high-profile social media research, focusing on the prevalence of undisclosed ties to industry among authors, reviewers, and editors, and the impact of such ties on research visibility and focus.
arXiv_2601.11507v1.research_question: To what extent is social media research independent from industry influence?
hypotheses: A significant proportion of high-profile social media research has ties to industry.
These ties are often undisclosed in published research.
Industry-tied research garners more attention and influence than independent research.
Industry ties are associated with a shift in research focus away from platform-scale features.
arXiv_2601.11507v1.data_source: OpenAlex, Altmetrics, and manually validated data from industry-announced RFPs and fellowships.
arXiv_2601.11507v1.data_description: The dataset includes 295 articles published in top journals (Science, Nature, PNAS, and their transfer journals) on social media research, involving 1210 unique authors. The data spans multiple years and includes information on author affiliations, funding, collaborations, and employment with industry.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly specified, but data as of November 6th, 2025
filters: Articles mentioning specific social media platforms in title or abstract

independent_variables.name: Industry_tie
independent_variables.definition: Whether an author has a disclosable tie to industry (funding, collaboration, or employment) within the journal's specified period for competing interests.

independent_variables.name: Disclosure_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the industry tie is disclosed in the paper (affiliation, competing interests, or funding statements).

independent_variables.name: Type_of_tie
independent_variables.definition: The nature of the industry tie (employment, collaboration, or funding).

dependent_variables.name: Impact
dependent_variables.formula: Measured by Altmetric attention score, citations, policy mentions, news mentions, and social media mentions.

dependent_variables.name: Topic_focus
dependent_variables.formula: Whether the research focuses on platform-scale features or other topics.

control_variables.name: Journal
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in impact and visibility across journals.

control_variables.name: Publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in research impact and industry influence.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and Bayesian binomial regression to analyze the probability of disclosure over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact and focus of research with and without industry ties.

result_claims.value: Twice the impact of independent work

result_claims.metric: Undisclosed ties among authors with no declared competing interests

partial_results.note: Unclear whether due to data access, PR campaigns, or researcher characteristics.

conclusion_claims: Industry influence in social media research is extensive, impactful, and often opaque.
There is a need to strengthen disclosure norms and implement policies to ensure the visibility of independent research and the integrity of industry-supported research.
limitations: The study focuses on a relatively small corpus of high-profile research, which may not be representative of the broader field.
The analysis relies on public data and manual validation, which may not capture all industry ties.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the accuracy of detected ties.
To assess trends in disclosure probability over time.

_source.title: Industry Influence in High-Profile Social Media Research

arXiv_2602.10118v1.title: Reviewing the Reviewer: Elevating Peer Review Quality through LLM-Guided Feedback
arXiv_2602.10118v1.research_idea: The paper introduces an LLM-driven framework to improve peer review quality by detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues in review segments and generating targeted feedback using a neurosymbolic module and genetic algorithm.
arXiv_2602.10118v1.research_question: How can peer review quality be improved by detecting and addressing lazy thinking and specificity issues in review segments using LLMs?
hypotheses: An LLM-driven framework can outperform zero-shot LLM baselines in detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues in peer reviews.
The proposed feedback generation method can improve review quality by up to 92.4%.
The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset can support multi-label detection of lazy thinking and specificity issues.
arXiv_2602.10118v1.data_description: The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset contains 1,309 expert-annotated review sentences from ARR 2022 and EMNLP 2024, with multi-label annotations for lazy thinking and specificity issues.

independent_variables.name: lazy_thinking_issues
independent_variables.definition: Segments of peer reviews annotated for lazy thinking issues based on ARR guidelines.

independent_variables.name: specificity_issues
independent_variables.definition: Segments of peer reviews annotated for specificity issues based on ARR guidelines.

dependent_variables.name: review_quality_improvement
dependent_variables.formula: Percentage reduction in review issues after applying the proposed feedback generation method.

control_variables.name: review_length
control_variables.rationale: Longer reviews may naturally contain more issues, so controlling for length ensures fair comparison.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and controlled review-rewriting studies to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the effectiveness of the proposed feedback generation method on review quality improvement.

partial_results.note: The paper evaluated multiple language models (GPT-4 Omni, Phi3, Gemma2, Llama3.1, and Mistral) but the performance of GPT-4 was not explicitly compared in the abstract.

conclusion_claims: The proposed neuro-symbolic issue detection approach outperforms the strongest LLM-only model in detecting lazy thinking and specificity issues.
The LLM-driven feedback generation module improves the constructiveness and relevance of feedback by about 20% over the best baselines.
The LAZYREVIEWPLUS dataset is the first multi-label review dataset annotated with lazy thinking and specificity issues for issue identification and feedback generation.
limitations: The experimental section is relatively weak, with only the Gemma model being tested in detail.
The paper does not provide a comprehensive comparison of all tested models (e.g., GPT-4) in the abstract.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed feedback generation method on real-world peer reviews.

_source.title: Reviewing the Reviewer: Elevating Peer Review Quality through LLM-Guided Feedback

arXiv_2604.24071v1.title: PeeriScope: A Multi-Faceted Framework for Evaluating Peer Review Quality
arXiv_2604.24071v1.venue: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW Companion '26)
arXiv_2604.24071v1.research_idea: PeeriScope is a modular platform that integrates structured features, rubric-guided large language model assessments, and supervised prediction to evaluate peer review quality along multiple dimensions. It is designed for openness and integration, providing a public interface and a documented API.
arXiv_2604.24071v1.research_question: How can peer review quality be systematically, interpretable, and extensibly assessed using a multi-faceted framework?
hypotheses: PeeriScope can effectively evaluate peer review quality along multiple dimensions using structured linguistic metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.
PeeriScope can provide practical deployment and research extensibility through its public interface and documented API.
arXiv_2604.24071v1.data_description: OpenAlex is used to retrieve reviewer publication profiles and derive topical-expertise and citation features. The data includes reviewer publication history, citation counts, and scholarly tenure.

independent_variables.name: Review Length
independent_variables.definition: The length of the review text, reported as a proxy for thoroughness.

independent_variables.name: Hedging
independent_variables.definition: Measured using a cue-based neural detector, captures the epistemic stance of the reviewer.

independent_variables.name: Lexical Diversity
independent_variables.definition: Measured as type-token ratio, reflects linguistic variation and effort.

independent_variables.name: Readability
independent_variables.definition: Captured using the Flesch Reading Ease score, reflects how easily the review can be understood.

independent_variables.name: Politeness
independent_variables.definition: Linked to perceptions of fairness and author receptivity.

independent_variables.name: Sentiment
independent_variables.definition: Polarity offers a coarse but informative indicator of evaluative direction.

independent_variables.name: Similarity
independent_variables.definition: General similarity of the review to the manuscript reflects the reviewer’s overall domain proximity.

independent_variables.name: Citations
independent_variables.definition: Mentions of citations provide support for reviewers’ claims and help ground them.

independent_variables.name: Questions
independent_variables.definition: Presence of questions in the review, indicating constructive feedback.

independent_variables.name: Topical Alignment
independent_variables.definition: Measured using SPECTER, reflects the alignment between the submission and the reviewer’s publication history.

independent_variables.name: Citation Count
independent_variables.definition: Characterizes the reviewer’s standing through citation count.

independent_variables.name: Scholarly Tenure
independent_variables.definition: Proxy for influence within a field, derived from the reviewer’s publication history.

dependent_variables.name: Review Quality Score
dependent_variables.formula: Integrated score derived from structured metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.

statistical_method.specification: PeeriScope uses a combination of structured linguistic metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling to evaluate peer review quality.

grouping_strategy.rationale: Not applicable

conclusion_claims: PeeriScope provides a multi-faceted framework for evaluating peer review quality using structured features, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.
PeeriScope is designed for openness and integration, providing a public interface and a documented API for practical deployment and research extensibility.
limitations: Peer-review datasets are inherently sparse, fragmented across venues, and largely inaccessible due to confidentiality constraints.
Evaluating LLM-assisted practices remains difficult due to the lack of annotated, high-quality review data.

_source.title: PeeriScope: A Multi-Faceted Framework for Evaluating Peer Review Quality

arXiv_2606.04716v1.title: The State of Peer Review in Empirical Software Engineering: A Community Survey on Review Load, Quality, and GenAI Use
arXiv_2606.04716v1.research_idea: The paper investigates the current state of peer review in empirical software engineering (ESE) research, focusing on review load, quality, challenges, and the use of generative AI (GenAI) in the reviewing process. It aims to gather community insights to inform evidence-based improvements to the peer review system.
arXiv_2606.04716v1.research_question: What is the current state of peer review in the ESE community, including perceived review load, review quality, challenges, and the use of LLM-based tools, and what suggestions do community members have for improvement?
arXiv_2606.04716v1.data_source: Questionnaire survey responses collected from ESE researchers and reviewers.
arXiv_2606.04716v1.data_description: 120 responses from participants who had acted as peer reviewers in the past 12 months and were active in ESE research. Data included demographic information, perceived review load, review quality assessments, LLM usage, and suggestions for improvement.

filters: Participants must have reviewed at least one scientific paper in the past 12 months and be active in ESE research.

independent_variables.name: review_load
independent_variables.definition: Perceived review load rated on a 5-point ordinal scale from 'very low' to 'very high'.

independent_variables.name: review_type
independent_variables.definition: Type of review (journal, conference, workshop).

independent_variables.name: role
independent_variables.definition: Participant's role (tenured academic, postdoc, non-tenured academic, industry, PhD student).

independent_variables.name: llm_usage
independent_variables.definition: Whether participants use LLM-based tools in the reviewing process.

dependent_variables.name: review_quality
dependent_variables.definition: Perceived quality of reviews, including challenges and issues with the review process.

dependent_variables.name: number_of_reviews
dependent_variables.definition: Number of papers reviewed in the last year across workshops, conferences, and journals.

control_variables.name: experience
control_variables.rationale: Years of experience in ESE research to account for differences in review load and quality perception.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and thematic analysis of free-text responses to summarize the data.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare differences in review load, quality perception, and LLM usage across different career stages and roles.

key_quantities: average number of reviews per year

result_claims.value: High or very high for 80% of participants
Average of 25-32 reviews per person per year
Not explicitly quantified but discussed as a concern

partial_results.note: The extent and impact of LLM use in ESE review processes were not systematically quantified in the survey.

conclusion_claims: The ESE community is experiencing high review loads, with most participants rating their review load as high or very high.
Review quality is a concern, with challenges such as superficial evaluations and inconsistency.
The use of LLMs in the reviewing process is a growing concern, with potential impacts on review integrity and depth.
Community suggestions for improvement include adopting double-anonymous or open reviewing models, structured review forms, and reviewer training programs.
limitations: The sample is skewed towards senior researchers from Europe and North America, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.
The survey did not systematically quantify the extent and impact of LLM use in the ESE review process.
The study is based on self-reported data, which may be subject to social desirability bias.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure transparency and replicability of qualitative findings.

_source.title: The State of Peer Review in Empirical Software Engineering: A Community Survey on Review Load, Quality, and GenAI Use

arXiv_2601.21761v1.title: Towards A Sustainable Future for Peer Review in Software Engineering
arXiv_2601.21761v1.venue: 2026 IEEE/ACM 48th International Conference on Software Engineering: Future of Software Engineering (ICSE-FoSE)
arXiv_2601.21761v1.research_idea: The paper addresses the growing imbalance between the increasing number of paper submissions in software engineering and the limited availability of qualified reviewers, proposing a sustainable future for peer review through scalable training, incentives, and cautious integration of AI tools.
arXiv_2601.21761v1.research_question: How can the software engineering research community ensure a sustainable and high-quality peer review process in the face of increasing submission volumes and reviewer overload?
hypotheses: Expanding the pool of qualified reviewers through training and mentorship programs will alleviate reviewer overload.
Incentivizing peer review will increase participation and improve the quality of the review process.
Cautious integration of AI tools can support and enhance the peer review process without compromising quality.
arXiv_2601.21761v1.data_source: The paper references data from software engineering conferences (ICSE, FSE, ASE, ICSME, MSR, SANER) regarding program committee sizes and submission volumes over the last five years.
arXiv_2601.21761v1.data_description: The data includes the number of program committee members and the number of main track submissions for the top software engineering conferences from 2020 to 2025.

independent_variables.name: submission_volume
independent_variables.definition: The number of paper submissions to software engineering conferences over time

independent_variables.name: program_committee_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of members in the program committee for each conference

dependent_variables.name: reviewer_workload
dependent_variables.formula: Calculated as the average number of papers assigned to each program committee member

control_variables.name: conference_rank
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in prestige and submission volume between A* and A ranked conferences

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics to analyze trends in submission volumes, program committee sizes, and reviewer workloads over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the impact of submission volume on reviewer workload and program committee size across different conferences.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide quantitative results on the impact of AI tools on the review process, as this is a proposed future direction rather than an evaluated mechanism.

conclusion_claims: The current imbalance between submission volume and reviewer availability is not sustainable and risks harming the quality of peer review in software engineering.
A sustainable future for peer review requires expanding the pool of qualified reviewers through training and mentorship programs, incentivizing participation, and cautiously integrating AI tools.
limitations: The paper is based on descriptive analysis and does not include quantitative evaluation of proposed solutions.
The impact of AI tools on the peer review process is not empirically tested.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed trends in submission volume and reviewer workload are consistent across different software engineering conferences.

_source.title: Towards A Sustainable Future for Peer Review in Software Engineering

arXiv_2601.15172v1.title: Is Peer Review Really in Decline? Analyzing Review Quality across Venues and Time
arXiv_2601.15172v1.research_idea: The paper introduces a new framework for evidence-based comparative study of review quality in computer science conferences, focusing on ICLR, NeurIPS, and ACL. It proposes a multi-dimensional schema for quantifying review quality and uses both LLM-based and lightweight measurements to analyze trends over time.
arXiv_2601.15172v1.research_question: Is there a consistent decline in peer review quality across computer science conferences over time?
hypotheses: Peer review quality is declining due to factors like scarce qualified reviewers, increasing specialization, and high workloads.
Peer review quality has not declined, as conferences refine workflows, grow expert pools, and use AI assistance.
arXiv_2601.15172v1.data_source: Peer review data from ICLR, NeurIPS, and ACL Rolling Review (ARR) conferences.
arXiv_2601.15172v1.data_description: The data includes peer review reports from multiple years of these conferences, with varying review formats and structures. The data is semi-structured and includes free-text, numerical, and other fields.

independent_variables.name: time
independent_variables.definition: Year of the reviewing campaign

independent_variables.name: venue
independent_variables.definition: Conference name (ICLR, NeurIPS, ACL)

independent_variables.name: review_format
independent_variables.definition: Structure of the review (e.g., free-text, structured form)

independent_variables.name: expertise
independent_variables.definition: Depth of reviewer expertise in the paper's topic

dependent_variables.name: review_quality
dependent_variables.formula: Composite score based on substantiveness, actionability, and grounding measurements

control_variables.name: paper_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of paper quality on review quality

control_variables.name: reviewer_experience
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of reviewer experience on review quality

control_variables.name: workload
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of reviewer workload on review quality

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive analysis and correlation analysis to study the relationships between different measurements of review quality and its evolution over time.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare review quality across different computer science conferences

partial_results.metric: impact of LLMs on review quality
partial_results.note: The impact of LLMs on review quality is not fully quantified due to limited data on their use in reviewing campaigns.

conclusion_claims: Contrary to the popular narrative, there is no consistent decline in median review quality across venues and years.
The diversity of review formats and the use of LLMs for review standardization can help mitigate differences in review quality measurements.
limitations: The data is scarce, biased, and noisy, which can affect the accuracy of review quality measurements.
The evolving nature of reviewing workflows makes consistent application of review quality measures difficult.

robustness_checks.rationale: To verify the relationships between different measurements of review quality
To ensure the consistency of findings across different time periods

_source.title: Is Peer Review Really in Decline? Analyzing Review Quality across Venues and Time

Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.title: DREAM: Deep Research Evaluation with Agentic Metrics
Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.research_idea: The paper introduces DREAM, a framework for evaluating deep research agents (DRAs) that addresses the 'Mirage of Synthesis' by incorporating agentic metrics and active verification. DREAM combines static and adaptive metrics to assess research quality across four verticals: Presentation Quality, Task Compliance, Analytical Depth, and Source Quality.
Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.research_question: How can deep research agents be evaluated effectively, considering the multidimensional nature of research quality and the limitations of existing benchmarks?
hypotheses: Current DRE benchmarks suffer from the 'Mirage of Synthesis' due to their inability to detect factual, temporal, and logical degradation.
DREAM's agentic evaluation framework will be more sensitive to factual and temporal decay than existing benchmarks.
Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.data_source: The paper does not mention specific datasets or databases used for the study.
Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.data_description: The paper does not provide a detailed description of the data used in the study.

independent_variables.name: Evaluation Method
independent_variables.definition: The type of evaluation method used (e.g., DREAM, existing benchmarks).

independent_variables.name: Temporal Validity
independent_variables.definition: Whether the report contains time-sensitive information that is outdated.

independent_variables.name: Factual Correctness
independent_variables.definition: Whether the report contains factual errors.

dependent_variables.name: Evaluation Score
dependent_variables.formula: The score assigned to the report by the evaluation method.

dependent_variables.name: Sensitivity to Factual Errors
dependent_variables.formula: The degree to which the evaluation method detects factual errors in the report.

dependent_variables.name: Sensitivity to Temporal Decay
dependent_variables.formula: The degree to which the evaluation method detects outdated information in the report.

control_variables.name: Report Quality
control_variables.rationale: The overall quality of the report, including presentation and task compliance, to ensure that differences in evaluation scores are due to the evaluation method and not the report itself.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and controlled experiments to compare the performance of DREAM with existing benchmarks.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of DREAM with existing benchmarks in detecting factual and temporal errors.

result_claims.value: DREAM is significantly more sensitive to factual errors than existing benchmarks.
DREAM is significantly more sensitive to temporal decay than existing benchmarks.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide specific numerical results for the evaluation scores.

conclusion_claims: DREAM addresses the 'Mirage of Synthesis' by incorporating agentic metrics and active verification.
DREAM is significantly more sensitive to factual and temporal decay than existing benchmarks, offering a scalable, reference-free evaluation paradigm.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed data on the specific datasets or benchmarks used in the experiments.
The controlled experiments may not fully capture the complexity of real-world research scenarios.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not due to confounding variables and to validate the effectiveness of DREAM in detecting factual and temporal errors.

_source.title: DREAM: Deep Research Evaluation with Agentic Metrics

Research_Policy_2604.16387v1.title: Large language models for post-publication research evaluation: Evidence from expert recommendations and citation indicators
Research_Policy_2604.16387v1.research_idea: This study explores the potential of large language models (LLMs) to support post-publication research evaluation by benchmarking their outputs against expert judgments and citation-based indicators. The research contributes to understanding the effectiveness of LLMs in evaluating scientific articles and their alignment with human and citation-based metrics.
Research_Policy_2604.16387v1.research_question: To what extent can LLMs perform post-publication research evaluation tasks in comparison with expert judgments, and how do model selection and learning strategies influence performance?
hypotheses: LLMs can perform post-publication research evaluation tasks with performance comparable to expert judgments.
Supervised fine-tuning and few-shot learning strategies improve the alignment of LLM outputs with expert annotations and citation indicators.
Research_Policy_2604.16387v1.data_description: The study uses articles from the H1 Connect platform, which includes expert-driven recommendations and evaluations by biomedical faculty researchers. The dataset is used for two evaluation tasks: high-quality article identification and fine-grained evaluation including article rating, merit classification, and expert-style commenting.

independent_variables.name: model_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of LLM used, including BERT models, general-purpose LLMs, and reasoning-oriented LLMs.

independent_variables.name: learning_strategy
independent_variables.definition: The learning strategy applied, including in-context learning, few-shot prompting, and supervised fine-tuning.

dependent_variables.name: evaluation_accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Accuracy of LLM outputs in identifying high-quality articles and performing fine-grained evaluation tasks.

dependent_variables.name: alignment_with_expert_judgments
dependent_variables.formula: Correlation between LLM outputs and expert annotations.

dependent_variables.name: alignment_with_citation_indicators
dependent_variables.formula: Correlation between LLM outputs and citation-based indicators.

control_variables.name: article_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for the inherent quality of the articles being evaluated.

control_variables.name: article_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for field-specific differences in evaluation criteria.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive analysis to compare the performance of different LLMs and learning strategies in post-publication research evaluation tasks.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of different model families in post-publication research evaluation tasks.

result_claims.value: improved with few-shot prompting and supervised fine-tuning

partial_results.note: Semantically similar examples do not consistently strengthen alignment with citation-based indicators.

conclusion_claims: LLMs demonstrate strong performance in coarse-grained evaluation tasks but struggle with fine-grained stratification of article quality.
Supervised fine-tuning produces the strongest and most balanced results for post-publication research evaluation.
limitations: The study is limited to the biomedical field and the H1 Connect platform.
The closed-source nature of GPT-5 prevents further analysis of its performance advantages.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not specific to a single model type.
To assess the robustness of LLM performance to variations in training and prompting methods.

_source.title: Large language models for post-publication research evaluation: Evidence from expert recommendations and citation indicators

arXiv_2601.08156v1.title: Project Synapse: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework with Hybrid Memory for Autonomous Resolution of Last-Mile Delivery Disruptions
arXiv_2601.08156v1.research_idea: Project Synapse introduces a hierarchical multi-agent framework with a hybrid memory architecture to autonomously resolve last-mile delivery disruptions. The framework combines strategic task decomposition with specialized worker agents and integrates working, episodic, and semantic memory for context-aware reasoning.
arXiv_2601.08156v1.research_question: How can a hierarchical, memory-augmented agentic architecture effectively manage and resolve complex, real-time disruptions in last-mile delivery operations?
hypotheses: A hierarchical multi-agent system with a hybrid memory architecture will outperform traditional rule-based automation in resolving last-mile delivery disruptions.
The integration of working, episodic, and semantic memory will enhance the system's ability to perform stateful, context-aware, and factually grounded reasoning.
arXiv_2601.08156v1.data_source: User reviews from a major super-app ecosystem
arXiv_2601.08156v1.data_description: A benchmark dataset of 30 complex disruption scenarios curated from a qualitative analysis of over 6,000 real-world user reviews.

independent_variables.name: disruption_type
independent_variables.definition: The type of disruption (e.g., traffic congestion, incorrect address, unavailable merchant)

independent_variables.name: agent_configuration
independent_variables.definition: The configuration of the multi-agent system (e.g., hierarchical vs. flat structure)

dependent_variables.name: resolution_score
dependent_variables.formula: Overall score assigned by the LLM-as-a-Judge protocol, ranging from 0 to 1

control_variables.name: scenario_complexity
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the inherent complexity of disruption scenarios

control_variables.name: time_to_resolution
control_variables.rationale: To control for the time taken to resolve each scenario

statistical_method.specification: Performance evaluation using an LLM-as-a-Judge protocol with explicit bias mitigation

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance of the Synapse system against traditional rule-based automation systems

partial_results.note: Results are based on a limited benchmark of 30 scenarios and may not generalize to all real-world disruptions

conclusion_claims: The Synapse system demonstrates strong performance in resolving last-mile delivery disruptions, with an overall average score of 0.73.
The hierarchical, memory-augmented agentic architecture is viable for dynamic, real-world problem-solving in high-stakes operational environments.
limitations: The evaluation is based on a limited benchmark of 30 scenarios, which may not fully represent the diversity of real-world disruptions.
The system's performance may vary in different operational contexts and with different configurations of the multi-agent architecture.

robustness_checks.method: LLM-as-a-Judge protocol with explicit bias mitigation
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the evaluation is fair and not biased by the judge's preferences or assumptions

_source.title: Project Synapse: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework with Hybrid Memory for Autonomous Resolution of Last-Mile Delivery Disruptions

Research_Policy_2603.14919v1.title: Which stylistic features fool ChatGPT research evaluations?
Research_Policy_2603.14919v1.research_idea: This paper investigates whether language-related factors unrelated to research quality influence ChatGPT's research quality scores, and whether these associations are stronger than those observed in expert scores from the Research Excellence Framework (REF).
Research_Policy_2603.14919v1.research_question: Which textual features of abstracts (e.g., readability, linguistic complexity, and length) associate with LLM scores, and are these associations stronger for LLM scores than for REF expert scores?
hypotheses: Linguistic complexity and length of abstracts are more strongly associated with ChatGPT scores than with REF expert scores.
Abstracts with higher linguistic complexity and lower readability will receive higher scores from ChatGPT compared to expert evaluations.
The relationship between abstract features and scores will vary across subject areas.
Research_Policy_2603.14919v1.data_description: A dataset of 99,277 journal articles submitted to the UK-wide Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021 assessments. Each article has associated ChatGPT scores, departmental REF scores, and abstracts from which readability indicators were calculated.

filters: articles submitted to the UK-wide Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021

independent_variables.name: readability
independent_variables.definition: Measured using readability indicators such as Flesch Reading Ease, Flesch–Kincaid Grade Level, and Gunning Fog Index.

independent_variables.name: linguistic_complexity
independent_variables.definition: Measured by the complexity of language used in the abstracts.

independent_variables.name: abstract_length
independent_variables.definition: Measured by the number of words in the abstract.

dependent_variables.name: ChatGPT_score
dependent_variables.formula: Scores generated by ChatGPT for the research quality of the article based on its title and abstract.

dependent_variables.name: REF_score
dependent_variables.formula: Average research quality score assigned by expert panels for the department submitting the article.

control_variables.name: subject_area
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in language use and evaluation criteria across disciplines.

control_variables.name: journal_impact_factor
control_variables.rationale: To control for the potential influence of journal prestige on both ChatGPT and expert scores.

statistical_method.specification: Correlation analysis between readability indicators, linguistic complexity, abstract length, and ChatGPT and REF scores.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the strength of associations between abstract features and scores from ChatGPT versus expert evaluations.

key_quantities: correlation between readability and ChatGPT scores
correlation between readability and REF scores

result_claims.metric: correlation between linguistic complexity and ChatGPT scores
correlation between abstract length and ChatGPT scores
correlation between readability and ChatGPT scores
correlation between linguistic complexity and REF scores
correlation between abstract length and REF scores
correlation between readability and REF scores

partial_results.note: Not tested in this study.

conclusion_claims: Linguistic complexity and length of abstracts are more strongly associated with ChatGPT scores than with REF expert scores in many subject areas.
ChatGPT may be more likely than human experts to reward linguistic complexity, with a potential bias towards longer and less readable abstracts in many fields.
limitations: Cause-and-effect relationships between abstract features and scores were not tested.
The use of departmental average scores as a proxy for individual article quality may introduce inaccuracies.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess whether the observed associations are consistent across disciplines.

robustness_checks.method: Replacing LLM scores with citation impact indicators
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess whether stylistic characteristics of abstracts are similarly or differently related to citation impact.

_source.title: Which stylistic features fool ChatGPT research evaluations?

arXiv_2410.09112v1.title: HLM-Cite: Hybrid Language Model Workflow for Text-based Scientific Citation Prediction
arXiv_2410.09112v1.venue: 38th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2024)
arXiv_2410.09112v1.research_idea: The paper introduces HLM-Cite, a two-stage hybrid language model workflow for predicting core citations in scientific papers. It distinguishes core citations from superficial citations and non-citations, improving citation prediction by combining embedding and generative LLMs.
arXiv_2410.09112v1.research_question: How can we accurately predict core citations in scientific papers by distinguishing them from superficial citations and non-citations, especially when dealing with large candidate sets?
hypotheses: Using a hybrid workflow combining embedding and generative LLMs will improve the accuracy of core citation prediction.
The proposed two-stage pipeline can scale to large candidate sets (up to 100K papers) while maintaining high precision.
arXiv_2410.09112v1.data_description: The dataset includes 12M papers across 19 scientific fields. The data is used to analyze the statistical differences between core and superficial citations and to evaluate the performance of HLM-Cite.

independent_variables.name: candidate_set_size
independent_variables.definition: The number of papers in the candidate set from which citations are predicted.

independent_variables.name: textual_similarity
independent_variables.definition: The similarity between the query paper and candidate papers based on their textual content.

dependent_variables.name: core_citation_prediction
dependent_variables.formula: Whether a candidate paper is predicted as a core citation.

control_variables.name: superficial_citations
control_variables.rationale: To account for the presence of less relevant citations that may distract from core citations.

control_variables.name: non_citations
control_variables.rationale: To ensure the model distinguishes between citations and non-citations.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision) to evaluate the effectiveness of HLM-Cite.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations are derived from the Microsoft Academic Graph, where each paper cites a subset of previous papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To distinguish between different types of citations and improve the accuracy of citation prediction.

result_claims.significance: Thousands of times more than existing works

partial_results.note: The paper provides statistical evidence that core citations are more closely related to the query paper in terms of keyword overlap and frequency of mentions.

conclusion_claims: HLM-Cite improves citation prediction by distinguishing core citations from superficial citations and non-citations.
The hybrid workflow enables the model to scale to large candidate sets (up to 100K papers) while maintaining high precision.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention limitations in the provided text.
The evaluation is based on a single dataset (Microsoft Academic Graph), which may limit generalizability.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the model's performance is consistent across different scientific fields.

_source.title: HLM-Cite: Hybrid Language Model Workflow for Text-based Scientific Citation Prediction

arXiv_2603.00399v2.title: A Data-Driven Analysis for Engineering Conferences: The Institute of Industrial and Systems Engineering (IISE) Annual Conference Proceedings (2002–2025)
arXiv_2603.00399v2.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 IISE Annual Conference
arXiv_2603.00399v2.research_idea: This paper presents a computational analysis of IISE proceedings from 2002 to 2025, combining ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets to map the intellectual evolution of Industrial and Systems Engineering (ISE) through thematic, citation, and co-authorship analyses.
arXiv_2603.00399v2.research_question: What is the intellectual evolution of the Industrial and Systems Engineering (ISE) field as reflected in the IISE Annual Conference proceedings from 2002 to 2025?
hypotheses: The integration of ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets will provide a more comprehensive and accurate analysis of ISE research trends.
LLM-based classification will effectively identify dominant, emerging, and receding research topics in ISE.
Network analysis of citations and co-authorship will reveal influential papers, authors, and collaboration patterns.
arXiv_2603.00399v2.data_description: The study uses 9,350 titles from ProQuest for thematic analysis and 8,958 titles from Google Scholar for citation analysis, covering the IISE Annual Conference proceedings from 2002 to 2025.

filters: Conference proceedings from IISE Annual Conference

independent_variables.name: publication_year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the paper was published, used to track temporal trends in research topics and collaboration patterns.

independent_variables.name: research_domain
independent_variables.definition: The primary research domain of the paper (e.g., Data Analytics and Information Systems, Manufacturing and Design), identified through LLM-based classification.

independent_variables.name: research_sub_area
independent_variables.definition: The sub-area within the research domain (e.g., Machine Learning, Computer Vision), identified through LLM-based classification.

dependent_variables.name: prevalence_of_domain
dependent_variables.formula: Percentage of papers in a given year that belong to a specific research domain or sub-area.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by a paper, used to identify influential papers and knowledge diffusion patterns.

control_variables.name: author_productivity
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in author productivity when analyzing co-authorship networks and collaboration patterns.

control_variables.name: publication_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in publication quality when analyzing citation patterns and influence.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and network analysis to map thematic evolution, identify influential papers and authors, and analyze collaboration patterns.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks were constructed using Google Scholar data, with edges representing citations between papers. Co-authorship networks were constructed using author data from both ProQuest and Google Scholar, with edges representing co-authorship relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To identify and compare research domains that are consistently prominent, gaining momentum, or losing relevance over time.

key_quantities: Prevalence of research domains and sub-areas over time

result_claims.value: Dominant, emerging, and receding research topics were identified
Influential papers and authors were identified

partial_results.note: The accuracy of LLM-based classification was not explicitly reported, but it was stated that open-source LLMs combined with semantic search can extract domain-specific knowledge with over 90% accuracy.

conclusion_claims: The study provides a comprehensive cartography of the ISE field's intellectual history, identifying dominant, emerging, and receding research topics.
The integration of ProQuest and Google Scholar datasets, along with LLM-based classification and network analysis, offers valuable insights into knowledge diffusion and community structure in ISE.
limitations: The study relies on automated classification methods, which may introduce errors in identifying research domains and sub-areas.
The citation data from Google Scholar may have quality and reproducibility issues, which could affect the accuracy of impact analysis.
