arXiv_2605.12514v1.title: Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation
arXiv_2605.12514v1.research_idea: The paper introduces Structural Diversity (SD) as a new metric to measure the extent to which a team bridges distinct knowledge communities in its prior collaboration network. It demonstrates that SD is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research, outperforming traditional metrics like team size and team freshness.
arXiv_2605.12514v1.research_question: What organizational structure fosters disruptive scientific innovation, and how does Structural Diversity (SD) influence this process?
hypotheses: Structural Diversity (SD) is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research.
SD positively interacts with team size, mitigating the 'curse of scale' in large teams.
SD drives disruptive innovation by fostering disciplinary integration.
arXiv_2605.12514v1.data_description: A century-scale dataset of 260 million scientific publications spanning 19 disciplines from 1900 to 2025.

independent_variables.name: Structural Diversity (SD)
independent_variables.definition: The weighted number of connected components (CCs) in a team's historical co-authorship network, representing the extent to which a team bridges distinct knowledge communities.

independent_variables.name: Team Size
independent_variables.definition: The number of authors in a team, log-transformed to account for skewed distribution.

independent_variables.name: Team Freshness
independent_variables.definition: The proportion of previously unconnected collaborators in a team.

independent_variables.name: Edge Density
independent_variables.definition: The density of connections among team members in their prior collaboration network.

dependent_variables.name: CD Index
dependent_variables.formula: A field-normalized index measuring the disruptive impact of a scientific paper.

control_variables.name: Title Word Count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the length of the paper title as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Title Readability Score
control_variables.rationale: To account for the readability of the paper title as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Title Promotional Words (%)
control_variables.rationale: To account for the presence of promotional language in the paper title as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Author Career Age
control_variables.rationale: To account for the career stage of authors as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Author Career Age Squared
control_variables.rationale: To capture the non-linear effect of author career age on innovation behaviors.

control_variables.name: Author Institution h-index
control_variables.rationale: To account for the prestige of the author's institution as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Author Number of Publications (log)
control_variables.rationale: To account for the productivity of authors as a potential confounder.

statistical_method.specification: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression models with fixed effects for discipline and publication year.

network_construction.construction_method: Based on the historical co-authorship network of team members prior to a focal publication.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the disruptive innovation outcomes of teams with varying levels of structural diversity.

key_quantities: coefficient of SD on CD Index
interaction effect between SD and team size

result_claims.metric: Interaction between SD and Team Size

partial_results.metric: Effect of SD on CD Index
partial_results.note: The effect of SD on CD Index is robust even after controlling for key confounding factors.

conclusion_claims: Structural Diversity (SD) is a powerful and robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research.
SD positively interacts with team size, transforming team scale from a liability into a resource for creative synthesis.
limitations: The study relies on historical co-authorship data, which may not fully capture the cognitive diversity of team members.
The CD Index is a field-normalized measure of disruptive impact, which may not capture all aspects of scientific innovation.

robustness_checks.rationale: To account for potential confounding variables and ensure the causal interpretation of the results.

robustness_checks.method: Quasi-natural experiment based on a U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) policy change in 2012
robustness_checks.rationale: To provide additional evidence for the causal effect of SD on disruptive innovation.

_source.title: Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation

arXiv_2601.14949v2.title: What Should I Cite? A RAG Benchmark for Academic Citation Prediction
arXiv_2601.14949v2.venue: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW ’26)
arXiv_2601.14949v2.research_idea: The paper introduces CiteRAG, the first comprehensive retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) benchmark for academic citation prediction. It proposes a multi-level hybrid RAG approach to citation prediction, fine-tuning embedding models with contrastive learning and specialized generation models. The benchmark includes two citation prediction tasks with different granularities and a large-scale hierarchical corpus of 554k papers.
arXiv_2601.14949v2.research_question: How can a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework be designed and evaluated to effectively predict academic citations at both coarse-grained and fine-grained levels?
hypotheses: A multi-level hybrid RAG approach will outperform single-level methods in citation prediction.
Fine-grained position-specific citation prediction will require more context-aware models than coarse-grained list-specific prediction.
arXiv_2601.14949v2.data_description: A hierarchical corpus of 554,719 academic papers spanning multiple disciplines. The dataset includes 7,267 instances for Task 1 (coarse-grained list-specific citation prediction) and 8,541 instances for Task 2 (fine-grained position-specific citation prediction).

independent_variables.name: Task Granularity
independent_variables.definition: Whether the citation prediction task is coarse-grained (list-specific) or fine-grained (position-specific).

independent_variables.name: Retrieval Method
independent_variables.definition: The type of retrieval method used, such as single-level or multi-level hybrid RAG.

dependent_variables.name: Citation Prediction Accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Measured as the proportion of correctly predicted citations out of total citations.

dependent_variables.name: Retrieval Precision
dependent_variables.formula: Measured as the proportion of relevant retrieved papers out of total retrieved papers.

control_variables.name: Model Type
control_variables.rationale: To ensure fair comparison between different models, including open-source, closed-source, and fine-tuned models.

control_variables.name: Corpus Size
control_variables.rationale: To account for the impact of the size of the candidate pool on retrieval effectiveness.

statistical_method.specification: The paper conducts extensive experiments across state-of-the-art language models and evaluates the performance of the proposed RAG framework using descriptive metrics such as accuracy and precision.

network_construction.construction_method: The citation network is constructed based on the citation relationships between papers in the corpus.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To evaluate the effectiveness of the RAG framework at different levels of granularity in citation prediction.

result_claims.value: Improved with the multi-level hybrid RAG approach
Improved with the multi-level hybrid RAG approach

partial_results.note: The paper mentions the evaluation of hallucination and diversity in RAG systems but does not provide detailed results.

conclusion_claims: The proposed multi-level hybrid RAG approach is effective for academic citation prediction at both coarse-grained and fine-grained levels.
The CiteRAG benchmark provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for citation prediction and serves as a methodological template for other scientific domains.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed results for hallucination and diversity evaluation.
The time window and specific disciplines covered in the dataset are not clearly specified.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with open-source and closed-source models
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the proposed RAG framework across different model types.

robustness_checks.method: Evaluation of retrieval and generation components separately
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the contribution of each component to the overall performance of the RAG framework.

_source.title: What Should I Cite? A RAG Benchmark for Academic Citation Prediction

arXiv_2305.16490v1.title: Prototype-Based Interpretability for Legal Citation Prediction
arXiv_2305.16490v1.research_idea: The paper introduces a prototype-based architecture for legal citation prediction (LCP) to enhance model interpretability, aligning with the decision-making process of lawyers by incorporating precedents and legislative provisions.
arXiv_2305.16490v1.research_question: How can a prototype-based model improve the interpretability of legal citation prediction while maintaining strong performance?
hypotheses: A prototype-based model will achieve strong performance in legal citation prediction.
Incorporating prototypes of precedents and provisions will improve the interpretability of the model's decisions.

independent_variables.name: provisions

dependent_variables.name: citation_prediction

control_variables.name: case_text

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a prototype-based architecture with a modified loss function to enhance interpretability. It compares performance with a baseline fine-tuning approach.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance and interpretability of the prototype-based model against a standard fine-tuning approach.

partial_results.note: Performance is compared before and after incorporating legal expert feedback, but specific metrics are not provided in the text.

conclusion_claims: The prototype-based architecture successfully enhances interpretability in legal citation prediction while maintaining strong performance.
Incorporating legal expert feedback improves the practical relevance and accuracy of citation predictions.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention the datasets used for training and evaluation.
Specific performance metrics are not provided in the text.

robustness_checks.rationale: To analyze the learned latent space and ensure the model's decisions are grounded in relevant legal references.

arXiv_2510.19246v2.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction
arXiv_2510.19246v2.research_idea: The paper proposes a Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) that combines multi-agent feature extraction with robust graph representation learning to address the challenges of predicting citations for newborn papers, particularly in the presence of long-tailed distributions and implicit factors.
arXiv_2510.19246v2.research_question: How can we design bias-aware models that deliver stable predictions on low-citation papers while revealing the underlying drivers of scientific impact?
hypotheses: Prior citation predictors overfit explicit signals, leading to degradation on long-tail papers and under distribution shifts.
A multi-source informed graph learning framework can enable robust prediction even without early citation information.
arXiv_2510.19246v2.data_description: Two large-scale academic datasets containing metadata about papers, authors, institutions, venues, and citation networks.

independent_variables.name: reproducibility
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how reproducible a paper's findings are, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_network
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the collaboration structure of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: text_quality
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the quality of the paper's text, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: topic_hotness
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how hot or trending the paper's topic is, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: venue_prestige
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the prestige of the venue where the paper was published, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: author_reputation
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the reputation of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: The number of citations a paper receives over a given time period.

control_variables.name: early_citation_signals
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of early citations on the prediction model.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a combination of multi-agent feature extraction and robust graph representation learning to model citation prediction.

network_construction.construction_method: Heterogeneous-network embeddings are used to represent the citation network, incorporating metadata and external resources.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To evaluate the performance of the model on different citation groups and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.

conclusion_claims: The proposed Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) achieves significant improvements in citation prediction for newborn papers.
The model reduces error metrics by around 13% and improves the ranking metric by 5.5% compared to baseline methods.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information about the time window of the data.
The paper does not discuss the generalizability of the model to other domains or types of academic content.

robustness_checks.rationale: To optimize worst-case group risk across environments and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.
To perform what-if analyses on controllable factors under monotonicity and smoothness constraints.

_source.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction

arXiv_2012.05742v2.title: Longitudinal Citation Prediction using Temporal Graph Neural Networks
arXiv_2012.05742v2.research_idea: The paper introduces the task of sequence citation prediction, where the goal is to predict the trajectory of citation counts over time for scholarly works. It proposes a model that combines graph convolutional networks and LSTMs to exploit both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks.
arXiv_2012.05742v2.research_question: Can leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks improve the accuracy of predicting citation counts over time?
hypotheses: Modeling a paper's citation network is useful for predicting citation counts over time.
Leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks will significantly improve the performance of predicting citation counts over time.
arXiv_2012.05742v2.data_description: The dataset contains a dynamic citation network spanning over 42 years, with an updated graph for each year. It includes information about papers, their citations, authors, venues, and abstracts.

independent_variables.name: topological_information
independent_variables.definition: Features derived from the structure of the citation network, such as node degrees and graph embeddings.

independent_variables.name: temporal_information

dependent_variables.formula: The sequence of citation counts for a paper over time.

control_variables.name: paper_metadata
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of paper characteristics such as authors, venues, and abstracts on citation counts.

statistical_method.specification: An encoder-decoder model combining graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks to model both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks.

network_construction.construction_method: A dynamic citation network is constructed from the Semantic Scholar dataset, with an updated graph for each year. Each node represents a paper, and edges represent citations between papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To test the importance of topological and temporal information in predicting citation counts over time.

partial_results.note: The exact performance metrics are not provided in the text.

conclusion_claims: Leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks greatly increases the performance of predicting citation counts over time.

limitations: The paper does not provide specific performance metrics for the proposed model.
The paper does not discuss potential limitations of the model or the data.

arXiv_2406.15311v1.title: The disruption index suffers from citation inflation and is confounded by shifts in scholarly citation practice
arXiv_2406.15311v1.research_idea: The disruption index (CD) is shown to be systematically decreasing over time due to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices, not due to a decline in innovation. This calls into question the validity of using CD to measure innovation trends.
arXiv_2406.15311v1.research_question: Is the disruption index (CD) a reliable measure of innovation trends, or is it confounded by secular growth and citation practices?
hypotheses: The disruption index (CD) decreases over time due to citation inflation (increasing reference list lengths).
The disruption index (CD) is confounded by shifts in scholarly citation practices, such as self-citation.
arXiv_2406.15311v1.data_source: SciSciNet database, PNAS publications (2011-2015), and prior studies (e.g., Wu et al., Park et al.)
arXiv_2406.15311v1.data_description: The study uses 7.8 million articles from 1995-2015 from the SciSciNet database and a quasi-experimental comparison of PNAS and PNAS Plus articles from 2011-2015.

independent_variables.name: time

independent_variables.name: team_size
independent_variables.definition: Number of coauthors on a paper

independent_variables.name: reference_list_length
independent_variables.definition: Number of references cited in a paper

dependent_variables.name: disruption_index_CD
dependent_variables.formula: CDp = (Ni - Nj) / (Ni + Nj + Nk), where Ni, Nj, Nk are subsets of citing nodes

control_variables.name: citation_window
control_variables.rationale: To account for right-censoring bias in citation data

control_variables.name: data_quality
control_variables.rationale: To account for missing citations in early years of the dataset

statistical_method.specification: Multivariable regression analysis of 7.8 million articles, including controls for data quality and measurement biases.

network_construction.construction_method: Citation networks are built using forward and backward citations within a 5-year citation window.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To conduct a quasi-experiment comparing articles with different reference list lengths but otherwise similar characteristics.

key_quantities: effect of team size on CD

result_claims.metric: relationship between CD and team size
result_claims.value: shifts from negative to positive for team size ≥8

result_claims.metric: effect size of temporal and team-size trends

partial_results.note: The observed decline is attributed to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices, not to a decline in innovation.

conclusion_claims: The disruption index (CD) is susceptible to measurement error due to citation inflation and shifts in citation practices.
The relationship between CD and team size is not consistently negative and shifts to positive for larger teams.
limitations: The study relies on observational data and quasi-experimental designs, which may not fully account for all confounding variables.
The results are based on a specific citation window and may not generalize to other time periods or citation practices.

robustness_checks.method: quasi-experimental comparison of PNAS and PNAS Plus articles
robustness_checks.rationale: To isolate the effect of reference list length on CD while holding other variables constant.
To validate the mathematical deduction of the relationship between CD and reference list length.

arXiv_2504.20081v3.title: Billions at Stake: How Self-Citation Adjusted Metrics Can Transform Equitable Research Funding
arXiv_2504.20081v3.research_idea: The paper introduces the Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI), a new metric that recalibrates citation counts by accounting for discipline-specific self-citation patterns, aiming to provide a more equitable assessment of research impact.
arXiv_2504.20081v3.research_question: How can citation metrics be adjusted to account for self-citation patterns in order to provide a more accurate and equitable assessment of research impact?
hypotheses: Excessive self-citation inflates traditional citation metrics by 10-20%.
Adjusting for self-citation patterns reduces the gender citation gap by approximately 8.5%.

arXiv_2504.20081v3.data_description: Analysis of 5,000 researcher profiles across diverse disciplines.

conclusion_claims: The Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI) provides a more accurate and equitable assessment of research impact by accounting for self-citation patterns.
Adjusting for self-citation can reduce the gender citation gap and improve the fairness of research funding allocation.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information about the specific datasets or databases used for the analysis.
The methodology for calculating the Self-Citation Adjusted Index (SCAI) is not fully described in the provided text.

Scientometrics_2306.01949v1.title: The disruption index is biased by citation inflation
Scientometrics_2306.01949v1.research_idea: The disruption index (CD) is systematically biased by citation inflation, a phenomenon driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which undermines its utility for cross-temporal analysis of scientific and technological disruption.
Scientometrics_2306.01949v1.research_question: Is the observed decline in the disruption index over time a true reflection of reduced innovation or an artifact of citation inflation?
hypotheses: The disruption index (CD) is biased by citation inflation, leading to a systematic decline over time.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase the density of citation networks and confound measures of disruption.
Scientometrics_2306.01949v1.data_source: Microsoft Academic Graph (MAG) citation network, Clarivate Analytics Web of Science
Scientometrics_2306.01949v1.data_description: The MAG dataset includes millions of research publications with metadata on citations and reference lists. The Web of Science dataset provides citation counts and growth rates of scientific production.

independent_variables.name: reference_list_length
independent_variables.definition: The number of references cited in a publication, denoted by rp = |{r}p|.

independent_variables.name: citation_volume
independent_variables.definition: The total number of citations produced per year, denoted by C(t) ≈ n(t)r(t).

independent_variables.name: self_citations
independent_variables.definition: The number of self-citations in a publication's reference list, which increases triadic closure in citation networks.

dependent_variables.name: disruption_index
dependent_variables.formula: CDp = (Ni - Nj) / (Ni + Nj + Nk), where Ni is the number of citing nodes that cite p but not {r}p, Nj is the number of citing nodes that cite both p and {r}p, and Nk is the number of citing nodes that cite {r}p but not p.

control_variables.name: team_size
control_variables.rationale: Team size is a proxy for the production effort associated with a research output and is correlated with reference list length.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: Publication year is controlled to account for time-dependent trends in citation practices and scientific production.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses deductive analysis, empirical analysis of the MAG citation network, and computational modeling of synthetic citation networks to demonstrate the effects of citation inflation on the disruption index.

network_construction.construction_method: The citation network is constructed using the subgraph Gp = {r}p ∪ p ∪ {c}p, where {r}p is the set of references cited by p, and {c}p is the set of nodes citing p or any member of {r}p.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare changes in reference list lengths and citation practices over time.

result_claims.value: grows exponentially with an annual rate of 0.051

partial_results.note: The decline in the disruption index is attributed to citation inflation rather than a true decline in innovation.

conclusion_claims: The disruption index is systematically biased by citation inflation, making it unsuitable for cross-temporal analysis of scientific and technological disruption.
Citation inflation is driven by increasing reference list lengths and self-citations, which increase the density of citation networks and confound measures of disruption.
limitations: The analysis is based on descriptive statistics and computational modeling rather than controlled experiments.
The paper does not provide a fully validated alternative to the disruption index for cross-temporal analysis.

robustness_checks.method: computational modeling of synthetic citation networks
robustness_checks.rationale: To isolate the effects of citation inflation and demonstrate that the disruption index converges to 0 in the absence of other confounding factors.

_source.title: The disruption index is biased by citation inflation

arXiv_2306.09911v1.title: Uncited articles and their effect on the concentration of citations
arXiv_2306.09911v1.research_idea: This paper investigates the evolution of citation concentration over time, emphasizing the role of uncited articles in explaining variations in citation distribution. It shows that the inclusion of uncited articles leads to a downward trend in citation concentration.
arXiv_2306.09911v1.research_question: Are citations becoming more concentrated in a small number of articles, or are they becoming more decentralized due to recent geopolitical and technical changes in science?
hypotheses: Citation concentration is decreasing over time due to a decline in uncited articles.
The reduction in uncited articles is driven by the growing significance of Asia and Europe.
arXiv_2306.09911v1.data_description: Publication metadata from the WoS Core Collection, covering the period from 1980 to 2020. Two datasets were used: one restricted to journals indexed throughout the entire period and one including all articles and references in the complete WoS collection.

all articles and references in the complete WoS collection

control_variables.rationale: Longer reference lists may influence citation patterns

control_variables.rationale: The expansion of bibliometric databases affects the visibility and citation potential of articles

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and inequality indices (e.g., Gini coefficient, Theil index) to analyze the distribution of citations over time. It also compares results from reference-based and citation-based approaches.

network_construction.construction_method: Citations between articles are used to construct a directed and weighted citation network, with edges representing citations and weights representing the number of citations.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To analyze the impact of uncited articles on the overall citation concentration and to compare citation patterns across regions.

partial_results.note: The observed trend is sensitive to the inclusion of uncited articles and the choice of citation window.

conclusion_claims: Citation concentration is decreasing over time, primarily due to a decline in the number of uncited articles.
The reduction in uncited articles is largely driven by the increasing significance of Asia and Europe in the global scientific landscape.
limitations: The study is limited to the Web of Science database, which may not fully represent all scientific publications, especially in the social sciences and humanities.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed trends are not artifacts of the methodological choices.
To assess the sensitivity of the results to the time period over which citations are counted.

arXiv_2503.23414v1.research_idea: This paper investigates the presence and impact of AI-generated academic papers on preprint repositories, particularly ResearchGate, and their role in citation manipulation. It demonstrates how such papers can bypass platform checks and contribute to inflating citation metrics like the H-index and i10-index.

These papers can contribute to inflating citation metrics such as the H-index and i10-index.

dependent_variables.name: i10-index_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Change in i10-index of authors citing AI-generated papers

control_variables.name: publication_date
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior

control_variables.name: platform_usage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior across platforms

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for field-specific citation practices

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive analysis of citation patterns and paper characteristics

network_construction.construction_method: Manual identification of citation links between AI-generated papers and other papers

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation patterns and academic impact between AI-generated and legitimate papers

partial_results.note: Limited to manual checks and may not be comprehensive

conclusion_claims: AI-generated papers can bypass platform checks and remain publicly accessible on ResearchGate.
These papers contribute to inflating citation metrics such as the H-index and i10-index.
limitations: The study is limited to a single platform (ResearchGate) and a specific research area (GenAI-enhanced Cybersecurity).
The controlled experiment involved a single fake paper and may not generalize to all AI-generated papers.

To assess the impact of AI-generated papers on citation metrics

others_2410.13989v1.title: Reproducibility study of “LICO: Explainable Models with Language-Image Consistency”
others_2410.13989v1.research_idea: This paper investigates the reproducibility of the LICO method, which aims to improve model interpretability and classification performance by leveraging natural language supervision from a vision-language model.
others_2410.13989v1.research_question: Can the claims made by Lei et al. (2023) regarding the LICO method be reproduced, particularly in terms of improved classification performance and interpretability?
hypotheses: Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves quantitative and qualitative model interpretability.
Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves classification accuracy.
Tasks with more classification categories benefit more from LICO because the prompt guidance comes from a manifold consisting of more classes.
others_2410.13989v1.data_source: CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and subsets of ImageNet
others_2410.13989v1.data_description: CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are benchmark datasets for image classification with 10 and 100 classes, respectively. ImageNet is a large-scale image dataset with subsets used for evaluation.

independent_variables.name: LICO_training
independent_variables.definition: Whether the model was trained with the LICO method (1) or without it (0).

independent_variables.name: number_of_classes
independent_variables.definition: The number of classification categories in the dataset (e.g., 10 for CIFAR-10, 100 for CIFAR-100).

dependent_variables.name: classification_accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Proportion of correctly classified images.

dependent_variables.name: interpretability_score
dependent_variables.formula: Quantitative measure of interpretability derived from Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and RISE methods.

control_variables.name: model_architecture
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in performance are not due to variations in model architecture.

control_variables.name: training_procedure
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in performance are not due to variations in training procedures.

statistical_method.specification: Comparative analysis of classification accuracy and interpretability scores between models trained with and without LICO.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance and interpretability of models trained with and without the LICO method.

partial_results.note: The effect of LICO on tasks with more classification categories could not be conclusively determined due to incomplete data.

conclusion_claims: The claims made by Lei et al. (2023) regarding the LICO method could not be consistently reproduced.
The study highlights the importance of rigorous evaluation and transparent reporting in interpretability research.
limitations: The original codebase was incomplete, requiring manual implementation of certain components.
The study focused on a subset of the original experiments, which may not fully capture the scope of the LICO method.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with multiple interpretability methods (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, RISE)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not dependent on a single interpretability method.

robustness_checks.method: Evaluation on multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet subsets)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are generalizable across different image classification tasks.

others_2502.04457v1.title: In order that – a data driven study of symptoms and causes of obsolescence
others_2502.04457v1.research_idea: This paper presents an empirical case study of grammatical obsolescence in progress, focusing on the purpose subordinator 'in order that'. It applies a data-driven approach to investigate symptoms and causes of obsolescence, combining philological analysis with statistical methods on data from mega-corpora.
others_2502.04457v1.research_question: What are the symptoms and causes of the obsolescence of the English purpose subordinator 'in order that'?
hypotheses: The frequency of use of 'in order that' is negatively correlated with time.
The obsolescence of 'in order that' is influenced by higher-order processes, both external (socio-cultural changes) and internal (rise of the to-infinitive).
others_2502.04457v1.data_source: Corpus of Historical American English (COHA), Corpus of Contemporary American English (COCA)
others_2502.04457v1.data_description: COHA contains 400 million words of American English from 1810 to 2009. COCA contains 560 million words of American English from 1990 to 2017.

independent_variables.name: time_period
independent_variables.definition: The year or decade in which the construction is used, used to assess the correlation with frequency of use.

independent_variables.name: genre
independent_variables.definition: The genre of the text (e.g., fiction, non-fiction, spoken, academic), used to assess distributional fragmentation.

independent_variables.name: higher_order_processes
independent_variables.definition: External (socio-cultural changes) and internal (rise of the to-infinitive) higher-order processes influencing the obsolescence of 'in order that'.

dependent_variables.name: frequency_of_use
dependent_variables.formula: Number of instances of 'in order that' per million words in the corpus.

control_variables.name: corpus_size
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the size of the corpus across time periods.

control_variables.name: genre_distribution
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in the distribution of genres across time periods.

statistical_method.specification: Kendall’s tau test is used to assess the correlation between time and frequency of use of 'in order that'.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To assess the distributional fragmentation of 'in order that' across different genres.

partial_results.note: Although 'in order that' is found in spoken language, its frequency is much lower compared to written language.

conclusion_claims: The purpose subordinator 'in order that' is showing clear symptoms of obsolescence, including a strong negative correlation between time and frequency of use and increased concentration in formal genres.
The obsolescence of 'in order that' is influenced by both external (socio-cultural changes) and internal (rise of the to-infinitive) higher-order processes.
limitations: The study is limited to the English language and the American English corpus, which may not generalize to other varieties of English or other languages.
The extrapolation method used to adjust for genre distribution may introduce some bias.

robustness_checks.rationale: To mitigate the bias introduced by changing genre distributions in the corpus over time.

others_2512.16943v4.title: Forsaking your own: unveiling the delayed recognition of Garfield’s work on the 'delayed recognition' phenomenon
others_2512.16943v4.research_idea: The paper investigates whether the concept of delayed recognition (DR) itself was subject to delayed recognition by analyzing the citation history of Garfield's 1980 paper on DR.
others_2512.16943v4.research_question: Has the phenomenon of delayed recognition itself suffered delayed recognition, particularly in the case of Garfield's 1980 paper?
hypotheses: Garfield's 1980 paper on DR did not receive adequate attention during its early years.
The paper experienced a delayed awakening in citations, similar to the DR phenomenon it describes.
others_2512.16943v4.data_source: Web of Science (WoS), Scopus, and manual analysis of citation data from Current Contents (CC) magazine.
others_2512.16943v4.data_description: Citation data for Garfield's 1980 paper on DR, including citation counts over time, with a focus on the period from 1980 to 2010.

independent_variables.name: year
independent_variables.definition: The year in which the citation was recorded.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: Number of citations received by Garfield's 1980 paper in a given year.

control_variables.name: publication_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior based on the type of publication (e.g., journal article, editorial).

control_variables.name: journal
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in citation behavior based on the journal in which the paper was published.

statistical_method.specification: Analysis of citation trends over time using descriptive statistics and visual inspection of citation trajectories.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare citation behavior before and after the introduction of the term 'Sleeping Beauty' by van Raan (2004a).

result_claims.value: 10 citations up to 2004, 20 citations from 2007 to 2010

partial_results.note: Citations were largely self-citations in the early years, which may have skewed the results.

conclusion_claims: Garfield's 1980 paper on delayed recognition experienced a delayed awakening in citations, consistent with the DR phenomenon it describes.
The paper was in a state of deep sleep for 28 years before experiencing a feeble awakening in 2007.
limitations: The citation data for Garfield's work in Current Contents (CC) is not accurately reported in WoS and Scopus.
The analysis is limited to a single paper and does not generalize to all of Garfield's work on DR.

robustness_checks.method: Manual verification of citation data from CC
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the accuracy of citation counts for Garfield's 1980 paper.
